comments.text:
responsibilities identified
- `hooks/useIndexData.js` currently owns the full tracker read/bootstrap lifecycle for `/`: cache-first bootstrap restore, network refresh of exercises/programs, initial history fetch, older-history pagination, IndexedDB hydration, auth/patient invalidation reset, error-copy shaping, and tracker bootstrap timing marks.
- The file header is directionally accurate, but the actual file now contains more than one independently changeable workflow inside that umbrella.
- File size is 323 lines, which is above the hook hard cap in `docs/NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md`.

recommended extractions or divisions
- Recommendation 1: split the older-history pagination workflow out of `useIndexData.js` into its own focused hook, likely something like `hooks/useIndexHistoryPagination.js`. Confidence: high.
  Why: `loadMoreHistory` plus `historyLoadingMore`, `historyHasMore`, `historyNextCursor`, merged-log caching, and incremental-history error handling are a distinct workflow from initial tracker bootstrap. A future agent could reasonably change paging, cursor behavior, or merge/cache rules without changing the initial exercises/programs/bootstrap load.
- Recommendation 2: keep the initial tracker bootstrap and reload lifecycle together, but narrow it to that concern only. Confidence: high.
  What stays in that bootstrap-focused hook: cache-first bootstrap restore, background refresh of exercises/programs, initial history page fetch, bootstrap-level error state, `fromCache`, auth/patient reset behavior, and tracker bootstrap timing marks.
- Recommendation 3: treat the top-level pure helpers (`getLoadErrorMessage`, `getCachedBootstrapSummary`, `inferCachedHistoryPageState`) as optional extraction candidates only if they gain second use or the bootstrap split needs them shared. Confidence: medium.
  Why: they are structurally fine where they are today; extracting them immediately would reduce line count a little, but it would not address the main ownership problem by itself.

what should remain in the original file
- The original file should remain the tracker bootstrap read hook for `/`, not become a general tracker-data umbrella.
- It should continue to own the initial route bootstrap state that the route host expects in one place: `exercises`, `programs`, first-page `logs`, bootstrap loading/error flags, cached bootstrap restore, and reload orchestration.
- The auth/patient invalidation reset belongs with the bootstrap owner because it governs when the tracker read state is cleared and reloaded.

keep-together notes
- Exercises, programs, and the first history page should stay together as one bootstrap concern. Splitting those apart would likely create artificial fragmentation because the route uses them as one initial tracker snapshot.
- The tracker timing marks can stay with the bootstrap owner. They describe bootstrap phases rather than a separate product concern.
- The file already relies on `lib/index-data.js`, `lib/offline-cache.js`, and `lib/fetch-with-offline.js` for lower-level work, which is the right layer boundary; the main issue is not misplaced `lib/` logic, it is too many stateful workflows in one hook.

open questions
- `historyError` is currently shared by both initial history load and older-history pagination. If pagination moves out, it is worth deciding whether incremental-history failures should keep sharing one error channel or get a separate incremental error owned by the pagination hook. Confidence: medium.
- `historyHasMore` and `historyNextCursor` are seeded during initial bootstrap but then advanced by pagination. That shared ownership is the main coupling point in a split. Confidence: high.

confidence summary
- Split older-history pagination from initial bootstrap: high
- Keep initial bootstrap lifecycle together in `useIndexData.js`: high
- Delay extracting the small pure helpers unless they become shared or needed by the split: medium
- No evidence that API-fetch adapters, offline-cache calls, or performance marks belong in a different top-level layer than hook + lib: high

title: Add unified commit preflight and hook guidance
description: Implement a single preflight command that reports likely commit blockers together, including README guard, Biome, structure, gitleaks, and commit-message requirements. Update hook messaging to direct agents to the preflight command instead of reactive retry loops.

close_reason: Implemented scripts/commit-preflight.mjs, wired npm run commit:preflight, updated README and hook messages to direct agents to the preflight command, and verified the staged snapshot passes README/Biome/structure/gitleaks/commit-message checks together.

title: Build shared effective-offline detector
description:
Build the shared effective-offline detector used across auth, fetch wrappers, queue sync, and route bootstrap hooks.

Why first:
This is the foundation for the rest of the work. The current codebase relies heavily on navigator.onLine, which is already known to be unreliable on iOS and during some browser/network transitions. A shared detector should combine browser online/offline events with recent fetch failure/success evidence so the app can classify expected offline states consistently.

Initial file scope:
- hooks/useAuth.js
- hooks/useUserContext.js
- new shared helper such as lib/network-status.js

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Shared helper exists for effective offline detection instead of raw navigator.onLine-only checks
- [ ] Auth/session code can use the shared helper without widening page responsibilities
- [ ] The helper can record network failures and successes for reuse by fetch wrappers and hooks
notes: Implemented the first-pass shared effective-offline detector. Added lib/network-status.js as the pure browser/request-evidence connectivity store and hooks/useEffectiveConnectivity.js as the React subscription wrapper. Updated hooks/useAuth.js and hooks/useUserContext.js to stop relying on raw navigator.onLine-only checks and to record network success/failure evidence through the shared helper. Updated README.md and docs/IMPLEMENTATION_PATTERNS.md so the new helper and hook are discoverable for future queue/bootstrap work.
close_reason: Implemented shared effective-offline detector and adopted it in auth and user-context.

title: Add shared fetch wrapper for offline-aware reads
description:
Add one shared fetch wrapper for protected-route bootstrap and related authenticated reads.

Why second:
Once the app has a shared effective-offline signal, fetch calls should stop hand-rolling network-failure detection in each hook or lib file. The wrapper should classify expected offline/network failures differently from true server or application errors and expose a reusable typed result or normalized error.

Initial file scope:
- new shared helper such as lib/fetch-with-offline.js
- lib/index-data.js
- lib/pt-editor.js
- lib/pt-view.js
- hooks/useRehabCoverageData.js
- hooks/useUserContext.js

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Shared fetch wrapper exists for network/offline classification
- [ ] Protected bootstrap/data loaders can reuse the wrapper instead of duplicating fetch-failure heuristics
- [ ] True HTTP/app errors remain distinguishable from expected offline conditions
notes: Implemented the shared offline-aware read wrapper in lib/fetch-with-offline.js. Added typed offline, HTTP, and parse error classes plus shared auth-header construction. Updated lib/index-data.js, lib/pt-editor.js, lib/pt-view.js, lib/users.js, and hooks/useRehabCoverageData.js to route authenticated read requests through the wrapper instead of duplicating bare fetch plus local offline heuristics. Updated README.md and docs/IMPLEMENTATION_PATTERNS.md so the wrapper is now the approved shared read pattern.
close_reason: Implemented shared offline-aware fetch wrapper and adopted it in shared read loaders.

title: Harden service worker handling for protected offline requests
description:
Update the service worker policy for protected document, RSC, and bootstrap API requests so expected offline use returns controlled responses instead of uncaught no-response failures.

Why third:
This directly addresses the current console errors, but it should come after the shared offline detection/fetch classification foundation is defined so the request-handling behavior is consistent with the rest of the app.

Initial file scope:
- app/sw.js
- app/layout.js
- hooks/useProgramBootstrapWarmup.js

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Protected document or RSC requests do not reject into uncaught no-response console errors during expected offline use
- [ ] Bootstrap API requests used by protected pages return controlled offline behavior instead of noisy service-worker failures
- [ ] The service worker remains limited to request/navigation handling and does not become the owner of app data state
notes: 2026-04-11 implementation update: removed the manual protected /program document and RSC warmup fetches from hooks/useProgramBootstrapWarmup.js, kept the hook focused on IndexedDB/bootstrap warming, and preserved documented tracker-triggered /program route warming through router.prefetch('/program') in app/(protected)/ProtectedClientWarmers.js using the shared effective-connectivity hook. No first-pass changes were made to app/sw.js or app/layout.js; those remain aligned with the current Serwist cacheOnNavigation path pending runtime verification.
close_reason: Implemented documented /program warmup path and verified tracker-online to /program-offline navigation no longer reproduces the earlier uncaught no-response console errors on nextjs.pttracker.app.

title: Adopt shared offline handling across protected page hooks
description:
Adopt the shared offline-status and fetch-handling utilities across the four protected page data flows: tracker, program, pt-view, and rehab.

Why fourth:
This is the integration pass after the shared infrastructure exists. Each route should keep its IndexedDB cache restore and user-facing offline notices, but it should stop duplicating low-level network heuristics.

Initial file scope:
- hooks/useIndexData.js
- hooks/useProgramPageData.js
- hooks/usePtViewData.js
- hooks/useRehabCoverageData.js
- hooks/useUserContext.js

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Tracker, program, pt-view, and rehab all use the shared offline/network handling path
- [ ] Existing IndexedDB fallback behavior stays owned by the page hooks
- [ ] Offline notices remain route-appropriate while low-level failure classification becomes shared
notes: 2026-04-11 implementation update: adopted shared offline handling across the protected route hooks. Added lib/network-status.js plus hooks/useEffectiveConnectivity.js for shared effective-online state, added lib/fetch-with-offline.js for authenticated JSON read classification, migrated shared loaders and hooks to that wrapper, and updated tracker/program/pt-view/rehab route-level copy to distinguish expected offline fallback from non-offline live refresh failure while keeping IndexedDB fallback owned by each route hook.
close_reason: Shared offline handling is now adopted across tracker, program, pt-view, rehab, and user-context flows via shared connectivity and authenticated read helpers.

title: Capture offline console error evidence from /program
description:
Console evidence from offline testing on https://nextjs.pttracker.app/program shows expected offline use is still producing uncaught service-worker/network errors instead of handled offline fallbacks.

Observed signatures:
- The FetchEvent for "<URL>" resulted in a network error response: the promise was rejected.
- sw.js: Uncaught (in promise) no-response: no-response :: [{"url":"https://nextjs.pttracker.app/program?_rsc=cfdtb"}]
- GET https://nextjs.pttracker.app/program?_rsc=cfdtb net::ERR_FAILED
- sw.js: Uncaught (in promise) no-response: no-response :: [{"url":"https://nextjs.pttracker.app/api/vocab"}]
- GET https://nextjs.pttracker.app/api/vocab net::ERR_FAILED
- sw.js: Uncaught (in promise) no-response: no-response :: [{"url":"https://nextjs.pttracker.app/api/reference-data"}]
- GET https://nextjs.pttracker.app/api/reference-data net::ERR_FAILED
- script.js: POST https://nextjs.pttracker.app/9d2f11868210772d/view net::ERR_INTERNET_DISCONNECTED
- Warning: preloaded CSS not used quickly enough after load

Interpretation:
While offline, service-worker intercepted protected document and bootstrap API requests are rejecting through to console rather than returning a handled cached response or explicit offline fallback response. The analytics POST and CSS preload warning are lower priority; the main follow-up should focus on /program document fetch plus /api/vocab and /api/reference-data handling.
close_reason: Evidence captured and resolved by the deployed offline handling changes.

title: Design reusable offline fetch handling across protected app pages
description: Review current offline console failures for service-worker intercepted document and API requests on protected app pages (/program, tracker, pt-view, rehab). Recommend a reusable app-wide method for classifying offline/network failures, suppressing expected console noise, and returning cached or explicit offline fallback responses instead of uncaught no-response errors. Scope includes public/sw.js, shared offline cache helpers, and route bootstrap hooks. This bead tracks analysis/design only unless implementation is explicitly requested.
notes: 2026-04-11 final design record: approved offline architecture uses lib/network-status.js for shared effective-online state, lib/fetch-with-offline.js for authenticated JSON read classification, router.prefetch('/program') in app/(protected)/ProtectedClientWarmers.js for documented tracker-triggered program warming, and route-owned IndexedDB fallback in the protected page hooks. Manual protected /program document and RSC warmup fetches were intentionally replaced rather than removed without replacement. Verified on https://nextjs.pttracker.app that tracker loaded online, /program was prefetched, and offline navigation to /program rendered the editor without the previously reported uncaught no-response console errors.
close_reason: Closed per operator confirmation: reusable offline fetch-handling design is done in the other conversation.

title: Intermittent missing audio on first exercise or first side after opening tracker
description:
Source Reference: operator report 2026-04-10
Status: open
Priority: P1
Risk: medium
Scope: current
Agent Owner: codex
Agent Eligible: codex,claude
Tags: tracker,audio,startup,intermittent
File Scope:
- hooks/useTimerAudio.js
- hooks/useTimerSpeech.js
- hooks/useLoggerFeedback.js
- components/TimerPanel.js
- app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js

Issue:
When opening the app, sound is sometimes missing on the first exercise, sometimes only on the first side of the first exercise, sometimes for the whole session until restart.

Context:
Operator reports intermittent startup audio loss over repeated use in the last ~20 minutes. Symptoms vary: often the entire first exercise has no sound, sometimes only the first side of the first exercise, less often sound never starts at all and app restart may be required. Investigate startup audio initialization, browser speech/audio unlock timing, and any first-use state or side-transition conditions that could suppress sound.

Constraints/Caveats:
Treat this as a separate issue from comparison-speech logic. Preserve current timer and speech behavior unless a fix is implemented under this bead. Check both speech and non-speech cue paths.

Validation Checklist:
- [ ] Identify whether the failure is speech-only, beep-only, or both
- [ ] Trace first-exercise and first-side initialization path for audio unlock/state setup
- [ ] Check whether restarting the app changes any initialization state that explains recovery

Dependencies:
- blocks: none

Reactivation Trigger:
n/a
notes: 2026-04-11 code inspection (read-only): Appears implemented in current repo, with remaining validation gap only outside static code inspection. Evidence: hooks/useBrowserAudioContext.js:9-28 now awaits AudioContext resume before returning a running context; hooks/useTimerAudio.js:31-34 awaits ensureAudioReady() and ensureSpeechReady({ warmOnly: true }) for ensure_audio_ready, 70-80 serializes effect execution so warm-up completes before later cues, and 85-94 warms media on first pointerup; hooks/useBrowserSpeech.js:9-80 resumes speech synthesis and warms voices/utterance readiness; hooks/useLoggerFeedback.js:62-71 warms speech on first pointerup for tracker-wide announcements. No contradictory older audio path was found in the current live files inspected for this bead.
close_reason: Closed per operator confirmation: startup audio fix is working. Follow-up observation window is tracked separately in the deferred AMC bead for one-week watchfulness.

title: Manual logging does not run tracker progress comparison announcements
description:
Source Reference: pt-4lty
Status: open
Priority: P1
Risk: medium
Scope: current
Agent Owner: codex
Agent Eligible: codex,claude
Tags: tracker,manual-log,comparison,speech
File Scope:
- hooks/useManualLog.js
- hooks/useTrackerSession.js
- lib/logger-progress-comparison.js
- hooks/useLoggerFeedback.js

Issue:
Manual logging currently does not run the same progress-comparison announcement logic that fires on the Log & Next path, even though manual logging should preserve the same comparison behavior.

Context:
Confirmed during investigation of pt-4lty. Log & Next flows through hooks/useTrackerSession.js, which calls getProgressComparison(...) and then speakText(...). Manual logging flows through hooks/useManualLog.js, which appends normalized sets and may announce All sets complete, but does not call getProgressComparison(...). The operator confirmed the symptom only happens on Log & Next, and also stated that manual logging should call the same comparisons.

Constraints/Caveats:
Keep this separate from the duration-specific incorrect comparison content in pt-4lty. The goal here is parity between logging paths, not changing the comparison rules themselves unless separately required. Preserve current manual-log validation and session-state behavior.

Validation Checklist:
- [ ] Manual logging triggers the same comparison-announcement path as Log & Next
- [ ] Manual logging still preserves existing add/remove/edit/manual-set behavior
- [ ] All sets complete timing still works correctly after manual-log saves

Dependencies:
- discovered-from: pt-4lty
- blocks: none

Reactivation Trigger:
n/a
notes:
2026-04-11 code inspection: still not implemented. Evidence: hooks/useManualLog.js:115-130 normalizes/appends manual sets and only calls maybeAnnounceAllSetsComplete; the comparison path exists only in hooks/useTrackerSession.js:146-153 via getProgressComparison(...) + speakText(...); app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:178-185 does not pass comparison/speech helpers into useManualLog. Remaining work: wire the same comparison-announcement path into manual save without regressing existing manual-log behavior.
Implemented shared tracker progress-announcement callback so Log & Next and manual logging now reuse the same comparison speech path. Changes: moved comparison announcement orchestration into hooks/useLoggerFeedback.js, wired announceSessionProgress into hooks/useTrackerSession.js and hooks/useManualLog.js through app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js, and added sided manual-set inference so side-specific comparisons stay scoped when all manually logged sets are on one side. Validation: npx biome check hooks/useLoggerFeedback.js hooks/useManualLog.js hooks/useTrackerSession.js app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js passed. Browser verification still pending in a separate pass if needed.
Follow-up structural refactor completed on SRP/SoC grounds while preserving the comparison-announcement fix. Manual-log workflow orchestration now stays in hooks/useManualLog.js while pure set shaping/validation/normalization moved to lib/manual-log-state.js. Tracker session orchestration now stays in hooks/useTrackerSession.js while exercise-session lifecycle moved to hooks/useTrackerExerciseSessionState.js, pending-set workflow moved to hooks/useTrackerPendingSetFlow.js, and finalization/optimistic-log merge moved to hooks/useTrackerSessionLifecycle.js. Focused Biome validation passed for the affected tracker/manual-log files.
close_reason: Closed per operator confirmation: manual logging progress announcements are done.

title: Duration dosage summary falls back to reps when seconds only live in seconds_per_rep
description:
Source Reference: pt-4lty
Status: open
Priority: P1
Risk: medium
Scope: current
Agent Owner: codex
Agent Eligible: codex,claude
Tags: tracker,ui,dosage,duration
File Scope:
- lib/dosage-summary.js
- lib/timer-panel.js
- components/ExercisePicker.js

Issue:
Some duration exercises display dosage as rep-based text such as 3 x 1 reps even though the timer opens with the correct 30-second duration.

Context:
Discovered while investigating pt-4lty. In backup data, examples such as Soleus Stretch and Calf Stretch On Stairs are stored with dosage_type = duration, sets = 3, eps_per_set = 1, seconds_per_rep = 30, and seconds_per_set = null. lib/dosage-summary.js formats duration only from seconds_per_set, while lib/timer-panel.js resolves timer duration from seconds_per_set ?? seconds_per_rep, so the timer can be correct while the picker summary falls through to the generic reps branch.

Constraints/Caveats:
Keep this separate from the progress-comparison bug in pt-4lty. Preserve existing timer behavior while correcting summary text. Verify against duration exercises that already store seconds_per_set so they do not regress.

Validation Checklist:
- [ ] Duration exercises with seconds_per_rep and null seconds_per_set show X x Ys in picker dosage text
- [ ] Duration exercises with seconds_per_set already populated still show the same correct summary
- [ ] Hold and rep dosage summaries remain unchanged

Dependencies:
- discovered-from: pt-4lty
- blocks: none

Reactivation Trigger:
n/a
notes:
2026-04-11 code inspection: still not implemented. Evidence: lib/dosage-summary.js:8-9 reads seconds_per_rep only for hold and seconds_per_set only for duration; duration formatting at lib/dosage-summary.js:28-33 falls through to rep text when seconds_per_set is null. The timer already uses the correct fallback in lib/timer-panel.js:17-21 via seconds_per_set ?? seconds_per_rep. Remaining work: make duration summary use the same fallback so duration exercises render X x Ys instead of rep text.
Implemented on 2026-04-11 in lib/dosage-summary.js. Duration summaries now use seconds_per_set ?? seconds_per_rep, matching the existing timer fallback in lib/timer-panel.js. Validation: direct node check confirmed duration via seconds_per_rep now renders 3 x 30 sec, duration via seconds_per_set still renders 3 x 30 sec, and hold/reps cases stayed unchanged. Targeted Biome check passed for lib/dosage-summary.js.
close_reason: Closed per operator confirmation: duration dosage summary fix is done.

title: Duration exercise set logging reports decreasing rep delta by remaining sets
description: A duration-based exercise shows an incorrect message while logging sets: when the prescription is X sets, after completing set Y the UI reports 'X-Y fewer reps than last time'. Example: with a 3-set dosage, after logging the first set it says 2 fewer reps than last time; after the second set it says 1 fewer rep than last time; after the third set the rep comparison disappears. Investigate the cause, capture findings on this bead, and report the root cause back to the operator.
notes:
Problem: Multi-set exercises announced  ewer total reps than last time on Log & Next before the comparable session was complete, which made duration exercises speak a countdown-like false delta (2 fewer, then 1 fewer).

Root Cause: lib/logger-progress-comparison.js compared running in-session total reps against the most recent prior comparable session immediately after each accepted set. On multi-set exercises, that total-volume comparison ran before the current comparable side/session reached its prescribed set count. Duration exercises exposed this most clearly because their sets store placeholder eps = 1, so partial progress looked like a shrinking rep deficit.

Change Made: Added a completion gate in lib/logger-progress-comparison.js so total-volume comparison only runs after the comparable set stream reaches the prescribed set count (current_sets / sets). Best-set improvement comparison still runs as before.

Files Touched:
- lib/logger-progress-comparison.js

Validation: Verified against backup-driven scenarios using  ackup-2026-04-06_23-12-52.json. Result after the change: duration set 1 and set 2 on 3-set exercises return no total-volume comparison; distance remains silent; completed 1-set hold and rep exercises still compare as before.

Follow-ups:
- pt-vdht (duration dosage summary field mismatch)
- pt-yn98 (manual logging should also run comparison announcements)
close_reason: Fixed by deferring total-volume comparison until the comparable session reaches its prescribed set count

title: Agent-managed repo safeguards baseline
description:
## Summary
Parent epic for the core repo safeguards needed because PT Tracker is 100% agent-created and agent-managed. The operator should not need to know what to look for in order for the repo to stay healthy.

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Core safety and maintenance safeguards are tracked as child issues under this epic.
- [ ] The epic covers formatting/linting, secret scanning, structure enforcement, and dependency maintenance as the current baseline.
- [ ] Child issues carry the concrete implementation details and can close independently as each safeguard lands.

## Notes
Created after discussion that the repo must rely on tools, hooks, and automation rather than operator detection of drift or risk.
close_reason: all steps complete

title: Add structure-check repo discipline hook
description:
## Summary
Add a repo discipline check script and hook wiring so the agent-managed repo enforces key architecture rules automatically instead of relying on docs alone.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A tracked structure-check script exists for the repo.
- [ ] It enforces at least required file headers, file caps, forbidden import-layer rules, and thin app/**/page.js rules.
- [ ] It is wired into the repo hook path with clear failure messages.
- [ ] README/docs briefly note the behavior.

## Notes
Requested after discussion that the repo is 100% agent-managed and cannot rely on the operator noticing structure drift.
notes: Implemented staged-file structure enforcement via scripts/check-structure.mjs and wired it into the tracked pre-commit hook. Current checks cover required one-line file headers for pages/components/hooks/lib JS files, file caps from docs/NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md, thin app/**/page.js guardrails, and the most reliable forbidden import-layer violations. Validation 2026-04-06: node scripts/check-structure.mjs --staged completed successfully against the current staged set.
close_reason: Implemented staged structure-check enforcement and wired it into pre-commit.

Added .github/workflows/secret-scan-deep.yml and secret-scan-fast.yml, restored .trufflehog-exclude-paths.txt and .gitleaks-strict.toml, and added scripts/run-gitleaks-staged.ps1 to scan the Git index snapshot locally. title: Set up Biome for repo formatting and light linting
description:
## Summary
Add a lightweight Biome setup for PT Tracker with conservative defaults and agent-friendly automation. Keep the rollout simple: format first, light linting, clear hook behavior, and no CI enforcement yet.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Repo has a Biome config with conservative defaults.
- [ ] package.json includes Biome scripts for check/format/lint and staged helper use.

title: Program editor update client calls wrong exercise API endpoint
description:
## Summary
The shipped /program client bundle includes lib/pt-editor.js and its updateExercise function issues PUT requests to /api/exercises?id=<exerciseId>, but the actual update handler lives at /api/exercises/[id].

## Evidence
- Live analyzer on http://localhost:4000/ selected source index 290 (pt-editor.js) in the /program client route.
- Built client/server chunks contain  etch(/api/exercises?id=, { method: 'PUT', ... }).
- Repo update handler is implemented in pp/api/exercises/[id]/route.js.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Program editor updates call /api/exercises/<id> for PUT requests.
- [ ] Saving lifecycle fields such as s_needed and lifecycle_effective_start_date persists successfully.
- [ ] Browser verification confirms the save sticks on the deployed Next.js site.
notes:
Implemented the client fix in lib/pt-editor.js: updateExercise now uses /api/exercises/<id> for PUT, matching the actual route handler. Build passes locally.
Code sweep complete: lib/pt-editor.js was the only live Next.js exercise update callsite still using /api/exercises?id=. Patched to /api/exercises/<id>.
Committed client endpoint fix in 80de01b. Next step is push/deploy and live browser verification that PRN save plus effective start round-trip now persist.
Live verification on https://nextjs.pttracker.app passed after commit 80de01b: Double Knee To Chest now saves as As Needed, reloads as [PRN] Double Knee To Chest in /program, and appears under PRN only in /tracker.
close_reason: Fixed and verified on live Next.js deployment after commit 80de01b.

title: Effective start date is not showing in lifecycle editor
description:
## Summary
The lifecycle editor no longer shows the expected effective start date for exercises.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Open the Next.js deployment program editor.
2. Select an exercise that should already have an effective start date.
3. Expand Lifecycle & Status.
4. Observe the Effective Start field.

## Expected
- The field should show the saved lifecycle_effective_start_date for the exercise.

## Actual
- The field is blank or missing the expected saved value.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Exercises with a saved lifecycle_effective_start_date show that date in the Lifecycle & Status editor.
- [ ] Editing and saving an exercise does not clear the saved lifecycle effective start date unless the user explicitly removes it.
notes:
Likely same root cause as pt-retl/pt-65dq: /program update client was sending PUT to /api/exercises?id=<id> instead of /api/exercises/<id>, which can prevent lifecycle fields including lifecycle_effective_start_date from persisting and round-tripping into the editor.
Live verification after PRN save fix: Double Knee To Chest had no saved effective start in the latest backup JSON. When effective start was explicitly set to 2026-02-02 in the live /program form and saved, the field cleared on the returned payload/view instead of persisting. This remains an active bug separate from the PRN save-path fix.
close_reason: Closed per user confirmation; effective start date issue was not reproducible for the user and browser automation input method likely caused the earlier result.

description:
## Summary
Browser verification on https://pt-rehab-git-nextjs-pt-tracker.vercel.app with the admin account shows the PRN lifecycle UI is not present in the deployed app.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Sign in to the nextjs preview with the admin account.
2. Open /program and select Double Knee To Chest.
3. Expand Lifecycle & Status.
4. Observe the lifecycle dropdown contents and selected value.
5. Return to /tracker and inspect the top controls in the exercise picker.

## Expected
- /program includes an As Needed lifecycle option and Double Knee To Chest reflects its PRN state.
- /tracker includes the routine | prn | all visibility control.

## Actual
- /program shows Double Knee To Chest as Active and the lifecycle dropdown only contains Active, Archived, and Deprecated.
- /tracker shows only the sort select and does not render the lifecycle visibility control.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Preview /program renders the As Needed lifecycle option and shows PRN state for exercises marked as_needed.
- [ ] Preview /tracker renders the routine | prn | all lifecycle filter.
- [ ] Browser verification confirms PRN labeling/filter behavior works on the deployed site.
notes:
Starting end-to-end save-path debugging for lifecycle fields. Will trace form payload, API response, and persisted values for as_needed plus lifecycle_effective_start_date.
Switching to live-bundle and network inspection on nextjs.pttracker.app to verify the actual deployed save path before changing code. Local client route mismatch is a candidate but needs confirmation against the deployed bundle/request flow.
Live analyzer confirmation: /program client bundle includes pt-editor.js, and the emitted bundle still calls PUT /api/exercises?id=<id>. The actual update handler is app/api/exercises/[id]/route.js, so the client update endpoint is mismatched.
Implemented client fix in lib/pt-editor.js so updateExercise now sends PUT to /api/exercises/<id> instead of /api/exercises?id=<id>. Running build and code sweep before re-verifying in the browser.
Verification on nextjs.pttracker.app now passes for the originally reported save symptom: PRN UI is deployed, Double Knee To Chest persists as As Needed, reloads correctly in /program, and shows under PRN only in /tracker. Remaining lifecycle-date persistence is tracked separately on pt-lmb6.

title: Implement PRN/as-needed as a patient_programs feature
description:
## Summary
Implement PRN/as-needed behavior as a patient-program feature instead of a global exercise lifecycle flag.

## Why
The same exercise may need to be routine for one patient and PRN for another, so the behavior belongs on patient_programs rather than exercises. This is no longer just a design decision bead; it is a feature build that requires schema, API, and UI changes.

## Scope
- Add patient-program-level PRN state or mode
- Update API reads/writes so PRN is stored and returned from patient_programs rather than inferred only from exercises.lifecycle_status
- Rework /program data shaping and selector structure so PRN grouping and editing flow through the patient-program model
- Preserve the visual separation behavior in the editor UI
- Align downstream tracker, pt-view, and rehab behavior with the patient-program PRN model

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] PRN state is modeled on patient_programs instead of requiring a global exercises lifecycle value
- [ ] /program can view and edit PRN status through the patient-program path
- [ ] PRN items remain visually separated in the editor UI
- [ ] Tracker, pt-view, and rehab logic read the patient-program PRN state correctly
- [ ] Legacy global-exercise PRN assumptions are removed or intentionally bridged during migration

## Notes
This feature will require a table/API/code-structure rewrite rather than a small UI patch. Treat it as a real implementation feature, not a quick decision follow-up.
notes:
User direction 2026-04-11: treat this as a feature because it requires rewriting the table/model and coding structure. Existing code inspection still shows PRN modeled globally on exercises via lifecycle_status. Visual separation in /program already exists, but the remaining work is the patient_programs data model, API contract, and dependent route behavior rewrite.
-
close_reason: Planning captured on bead; user prefers Claude continue independently.

title: Fix category and pattern NativeSelect wiring in exercise form
description:
## Summary
Category and Pattern in the exercise editor do not update when selected because ExerciseFormCore passes NativeSelect an event-shaped onChange handler from field(name), but NativeSelect calls onChange with the selected string value. Required Equipment works as a comparison because it passes NativeSelect a string setter directly.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Open /program and create or edit an exercise.
2. Open the Basic Information section.
3. Try to select a value from Category or Pattern.
4. Observe that the field does not retain the selected value and save validation still reports the field as required.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Category selection updates basics.pt_category correctly.
- Pattern selection updates basics.pattern correctly.
- Required Equipment and other NativeSelect users continue to work.
- Verification covers iOS PWA exercise-editor behavior for both fields.

## Scope
Implement the smallest safe fix in the exercise form layer, most likely by adapting Category and Pattern to NativeSelect's string-based callback contract instead of the event-based field(name) helper.
notes:
Implemented: Category and Pattern now use a string-based selectField(name) helper in components/ExerciseFormCore.js so they match NativeSelect's onChange(value) contract. Search pass found the mismatch isolated to ExerciseFormCore; other NativeSelect call sites already use string setters or (value) => callbacks. Added guardrail documentation in components/NativeSelect.js and docs/IMPLEMENTATION_PATTERNS.md. Status: implemented, awaiting verification on iOS PWA / preview.
Pushed implementation commit 6772ee5. Remaining scope is verification: confirm Category and Pattern selections now persist and save correctly on preview / iOS PWA.
User confirmed on 2026-04-06 that the fix is working on iOS. Verification result: Category and Pattern selections now work in the iOS PWA exercise editor.
close_reason: Implemented in commit 6772ee5 and verified by user on iOS.

title: Investigate iOS PWA category and pattern dropdowns not selectable
description: User-reported bug investigation: on iOS PWA, the category and pattern dropdown lists cannot be selected. Scope: determine the likely program-level cause by tracing the relevant Next.js UI, event handling, overlay/layout behavior, and iOS-safe interaction patterns. This bead is for root-cause investigation first; implementation is separate unless the cause is trivial and safely fixable within the same lane.
notes:
Root cause identified in components/ExerciseFormCore.js. Category and Pattern pass NativeSelect the shared field(name) handler via {...field('pt_category')} / {...field('pattern')}. That helper expects a DOM event and reads e.target.value, but components/NativeSelect.js calls onChange(selectedValueString). Result: selecting a category or pattern sends a string into a handler that dereferences e.target.value, so state never updates and selection appears broken. Comparison: the Required Equipment dropdown works because SelectTagSection passes NativeSelect onChange={setInput}, which matches NativeSelect's string-based callback contract.
Follow-up implementation bead created per discovered-from rule for the identified handler-contract bug so the fix does not get stranded after investigation closure.
close_reason: Investigation complete: root cause identified in ExerciseFormCore/NativeSelect onChange contract mismatch.

description: Running log of iOS offline/PWA test results from verification session. Reference for cutover decision and remaining fix work.
acceptance_criteria: - [ ] all test observations documented and linked to active beads - [ ] no observations lost on session timeout
notes:
ROLE: admin user throughout.

TEST RESULTS (iOS PWA, private browsing confirmed as equivalent):

[FAIL] /program does not warm from tracker on iOS:

- Root cause: Next.js Link prefetch doesn't fire for hidden hamburger menu items on iOS — SW never caches /program HTML without an explicit visit
- Fix: add background fetch('/program') in useProgramBootstrapWarmup to explicitly warm SW page cache
- Bead: pt-gt6h

[PASS] Tracker loads offline after clean online warm ✓

[UX BUG] /~offline page: 'Try again' button is white text on white background — unreadable

OUTSTANDING:
- pt-gt6h: SW page warmup fix (implement fetch('/program') in useProgramBootstrapWarmup)
