Record whether the mismatch reproduces or not, plus any cache/bootstrap differences that may explain the intermittent behavior. 4. No tracker code changes.
notes:
CODEX WORKING: isolated-context read-only reproduction attempt for Claude. No tracker code edits.
Isolated-context probe on nextjs.pttracker.app using a fresh DevTools browser context and the stored operator sign-in. Result: mismatch still did not reproduce in this pass. Immediately after sign-in, the tracker landed in a coherent Exercises state with Long Arc Quad showing '3 days ago · 5 sessions total' and the only visible 'Never done' rows still being Box Lift and Standing Ball Circles (Visual Fixation). Isolated IndexedDB used only pt_rebuild_offline v4 with stores activity_logs(451), exercises(40), programs(39), queue_state(1), ui_state(1), users(5); that is materially similar to the warmed shared-context store counts except for fewer UI/queue rows. Network pattern in isolated context was also simpler but similar: sign-in token POST, RSC navigation to /, then api/users twice, api/exercises, api/programs?patient_id=..., api/logs?include_all=true&limit=1000, and api/logs?type=messages — all 200 on first load. No tracker runtime console errors in this pass. Comparison with pt-bk4z: isolated context did not expose a new mismatch and does not by itself explain the intermittent issue; both contexts can settle into a correct state with consistent badge/history data. The shared-context pass still adds one important clue that isolated did not test as deeply: on hard reload, exercise buttons were absent until ~1800ms while bootstrap requests completed.

title: Capture runtime behavior for intermittent tracker badge/bootstrap mismatch
description: Claude is handling the deep code investigation for the intermittent tracker inconsistency where Exercises can show stale badge/dosage state while History has live data until refresh. This Codex assist bead is read-only runtime evidence gathering only: reproduce the mismatch if possible on nextjs.pttracker.app, inspect browser behavior with DevTools, collect cache/bootstrap/render observations, and write findings back for Claude. Scope constraints: no tracker code edits, no tracker bead takeover, no speculative fixes; focus on UI behavior, request timing, IndexedDB/cache observations, and any repeatable reproduction signals.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Capture whether the intermittent mismatch can be reproduced on nextjs.pttracker.app with a real history-bearing account. 2. Record observed behavior differences between Exercises and History when the mismatch appears or note that reproduction failed in this pass. 3. Gather concrete runtime evidence such as request timing, IndexedDB/cache observations, console/runtime state, or refresh behavior relevant to Claude's investigation. 4. Write findings back to this bead for handoff without changing tracker code.
notes:
CODEX WORKING: read-only DevTools verification assist for Claude on intermittent tracker badge/bootstrap mismatch. No tracker code edits in this bead; runtime evidence only.
Read-only runtime evidence pass on nextjs.pttracker.app with the real signed-in admin context. Current settled picker state was internally consistent: visible 'Never done' rows were Box Lift and Standing Ball Circles (Visual Fixation), and a live IndexedDB read from pt_rebuild_offline showed 0 cached activity_logs for both while Long Arc Quad had 5 cached logs and rendered '3 days ago · 5 sessions total'. Browser storage in this tab used IndexedDB db pt_rebuild_offline v4 with stores activity_logs(451), exercises(40), programs(39), queue_state(2), ui_state(5), users(5); pt-tracker-offline existed but had no stores. Reproduction attempts: hard reload with early runtime sampling plus forced History->Exercises toggles at 700/1100/1600/2200ms in a fresh signed-in tab. Result: no mismatch reproduced in this pass. Important timing signal: main exercise buttons did not exist at all in the sampled DOM until ~1800ms after reload; before that, early tab toggles clicked but there was no rendered picker content yet. Once buttons appeared, sampled focus rows were stable and consistent (Box Lift/Standing Ball Circles still 'Never done'; Double Knee To Chest 'Done today'; Long Arc Quad '3 days ago · 5 sessions total'). Request pattern on fresh hard reload: api/users requested 3 times (all 304), then api/exercises, api/programs?patient_id=..., api/logs?include_all=true&limit=1000, and api/logs?type=messages each 304; no console runtime errors on tracker page in this pass. This suggests the intermittent bug is not trivially triggered by early History/Exercises tab switching alone, but there is still a notable bootstrap window where picker content is absent while duplicate user/bootstrap requests complete.
close_reason: Completed read-only runtime evidence capture for Claude: current state checked against IndexedDB, reproduction attempts documented, and bootstrap timing/request observations recorded without tracker code changes.

title: Harden /program vocabulary archive flow with soft-delete warnings
description: The /program vocabulary editor currently exposes a plain Delete action with no layered warning. Backend /api/vocab already performs a soft delete by setting active=false, but the UI does not communicate that clearly and allows immediate destructive-looking taps. Scope: keep vocabulary removal as soft delete only, change the UI language away from hard delete framing, and require two explicit confirmations before archiving a vocabulary term. File scope: components/ProgramVocabEditor.js, hooks/useProgramVocabActions.js, and any small supporting doc/README updates if behavior description changes. Validation: user can still add/edit vocab terms, archive requires two clear confirmations, active list hides archived terms, and archive wording never implies permanent deletion.
acceptance_criteria: 1. Vocabulary removal remains a soft delete only; no permanent-delete path is introduced in the /program editor. 2. The UI uses archive/soft-delete wording rather than plain delete wording. 3. Archiving a term requires two explicit confirmations before the mutation runs. 4. Add/edit vocabulary flows still work normally. 5. Archived terms disappear from the active list after success because active=false terms are excluded from the fetched vocab data.
notes:
CODEX WORKING: /program vocabulary archive safety fix. Backend already soft-deletes via active=false; this bead is for UI wording and double-confirm protection so the editor never looks like permanent delete.
Implemented guarded /program vocabulary archive flow: ProgramVocabEditor now uses archive wording, shows an inline soft-delete warning card on first tap, and requires a final native confirm before the mutation runs. Updated optimistic success copy to 'archived from active use', added a dedicated verification checklist entry, and local npm run build passed on Next 16.2.1.
Found and fixed a deployed runtime regression during live verification: ProgramVocabEditor referenced selectedTerms inside a useEffect before the const was initialized, which surfaced on the deployed /program page as a minified ReferenceError. Follow-up fix reorders selectedTerms above the effect.
Live verification passed on nextjs.pttracker.app /program after commit 413ffb1 deployed. Verified with real authenticated data: vocabulary rows now use Archive… wording, first tap opens an inline warning card explaining this is active-list removal only, second confirmation reaches the native browser confirm dialog, and dismissing that dialog leaves the term unchanged. Also caught and fixed one deployment-only runtime regression during verification (selectedTerms TDZ), shipped in 413ffb1.
close_reason: Completed: /program vocabulary flow now stays soft-delete only, uses archive wording, requires layered confirmation, and was verified on the deployed app without archiving a real term.

title: Restore pt-view filter controls on deployed App Router route
description:
## Steps to Reproduce
1. Sign in to https://nextjs.pttracker.app with a real data-bearing account.
2. Navigate to /pt-view.
3. Confirm the page renders live patient notes, summary counts, needs-attention rows, and session history.
4. Locate the filter surface labeled "Show filters".
5. Attempt to click/toggle it in the live UI.
6. Inspect the DOM if needed.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The pt-view route exposes actual filter controls, not just a static label.
- The filter affordance is interactive and opens/closes the filter panel.
- The filter panel contains usable controls for filtering the rendered history data.
- Applying a filter changes the visible pt-view history results as expected on deployed nextjs.
notes:
Verification evidence: on deployed nextjs.pttracker.app with real signed-in data, /pt-view showed live notes, summary counts, needs-attention entries, and history rows. However, the DOM contained only
Show filters
with no onClick handler and no filter inputs/selects present, even after direct click attempts and desktop-width recheck. CODEX WORKING: taking the pt-view filter regression fix lane. Investigating the current App Router filter render/toggle path in app/pt-view and shared pt-view components/hooks. Correction after direct runtime inspection: this was a verification false alarm, not a deployed app regression. The toggle is wired on onPointerUp in components/PtViewFiltersPanel.js. Initial DevTools probing exercised click on the wrapper div, which did not represent the real interaction path. After triggering the actual pointerup-owned toggle and then using the live search field with 'Long Arc Quad', the filter panel expanded and the rendered history list narrowed to matching sessions.
close_reason: False alarm from verification tooling: pt-view filters work on deployed nextjs when exercised through the real pointerup path, and the search filter successfully narrows the history list.

title: Investigate intermittent tracker inconsistency: Exercises shows stale badge+dosage while History shows live data
description:
## Why this issue exists
After the hybrid auth + cache-first bootstrap landed, the tracker sometimes shows exercise badges as 'Never done' and dosage as 'No dosage set' while the History tab already shows real grouped history with valid data. A refresh fixes it. This is intermittent and affects both badge derivation (from allLogs) and dosage display (from pickerPrograms/programs), suggesting a bootstrap ordering or derived-state ownership problem rather than a data availability problem.

## What needs to be done
Trace the bootstrap ordering through useIndexData → pickerPrograms derivation → badge derivation. Identify:
1. What triggers the Exercises tab to render with stale/empty data when History has valid data
2. Whether pickerPrograms is derived correctly on initial cache-first render
3. Whether memoization or stale props cause the disconnect
4. Whether there is a race between the cache-first render and the network refresh that clears instead of merging state
5. Produce a diagnosis with file+line precision so Codex can implement the targeted fix

## Scope constraints
- Read-only investigation of TrackerPage.js, hooks/useIndexData.js, lib/index-history.js, lib/offline-cache.js, and related hooks
- Do not modify TrackerPage.js or useIndexData.js (Codex active lane on pt-zk9d)
- Produce a clear written diagnosis with specific file+line callouts and a recommended fix path
- Same signed-in user, valid IndexedDB data, real history exists — rule out data absence first
notes:
IF-PERSISTS FIX PLAN — do not implement speculatively; only if bug is confirmed reproducible.

Three targeted changes in hooks/useIndexData.js:

Fix 1 — Eliminate initial-state 'Never done' flash:
Change: const [historyLoading, setHistoryLoading] = useState(false);
To:     const [historyLoading, setHistoryLoading] = useState(true);
Why: component renders once with historyLoading=false before useEffect fires reload(). With false, pickerPrograms guard fires as: false && true = false → Never done. With true: true && true = true → history_pending → no badge shown. The !token || !patientId branch and reload() both immediately reset historyLoading to the correct value, so no downstream impact.

Fix 2 — Guard warm path against empty-logs cache:
In the warm path hasCachedData block, move setHistoryLoading(false), setHistoryHasMore, setHistoryNextCursor, servedCachedBootstrap=true, and markTrackerHistoryReady() inside a cachedLogs.length > 0 condition. If cache has exercises/programs but logs=[], keep historyLoading=true so the guard still fires until network completes.

Fix 3 — Prevent cache corruption from empty network response:
In the history try block, guard cacheTrackerBootstrap and setLogs(nextLogs) behind: if (nextLogs.length > 0 || !servedCachedBootstrap). If network returns empty and warm path already served data, skip overwriting cache and state.

Exact diff targets:
- Fix 1: line 60 in hooks/useIndexData.js (useState(false) → useState(true))
- Fix 2: lines 90-103 in hooks/useIndexData.js (hasCachedData warm path block)
- Fix 3: lines 183-191 in hooks/useIndexData.js (history try block, cacheTrackerBootstrap call)

Cannot reproduce as of 2026-03-29. Keep open. Implement if bug resurfaces.
Codex read-only reproduction findings to fold into the deeper fix plan: do not treat this as a faithful recreation of the natural intermittent bug. The strongest confirmed runtime clue is narrower: on initial tracker load, the exercise cards can render before badge/adherence text fully catches up, so badge state is capable of arriving later than the card list. In an isolated DevTools context, forcing IndexedDB ui_state['tracker_bootstrap'].value.logs = [] while leaving pt_rebuild_offline/activity_logs intact (451 rows) was enough to make the first visible Exercises paint show all badges as 'Never done'; network still completed successfully (api/users, api/exercises, api/programs, api/logs?include_all=true&limit=1000, api/logs?type=messages all 200), no app console errors appeared, and badges later corrected. This supports a cache/bootstrap timing explanation centered on tracker_bootstrap/log readiness, but does NOT prove the naturally observed intermittent bug follows the exact same path. Practical value for the plan: treat late badge hydration on first paint as a real behavior clue, not as settled root cause.

title: Page tracker history beyond the initial 1000-log bootstrap
description: The tracker currently fetches history with /api/logs?include_all=true&limit=1000 in lib/index-data.js. That cap is a stopgap, not a finished pre-cutover design: projected history volume will exceed 1000 soon and 2000+ after that. Scope: keep cache-first tracker bootstrap for the initial slice, then page older history on demand so History can grow without silently truncating the dataset. File scope: hooks/useIndexData.js, lib/index-data.js, components/HistoryPanel.js, components/HistoryList.js, and any small pure helper needed for paging state. Validation: initial tracker load still paints from IndexedDB-backed cache first, History still activates and renders grouped entries, and older history can be requested beyond the initial bootstrap slice without regressing offline warmed load behavior.
notes:
Decision: this is pre-cutover foundation work, not post-cutover follow-up. Implementation target: keep the current cache-first tracker bootstrap for the first history slice, then add explicit paging for older history beyond the initial limit instead of silently truncating at 1000.
Implementation in progress: added cursor-paged history fetch helper, hook state for historyHasMore/historyNextCursor/loadMoreHistory, and a UI-only Load older history control on HistoryPanel. Local npm run build passed; next step is browser verification that cache-backed first slice stays visible while older history appends and warmed offline reload restores the merged slice.
CODEX WORKING: active Codex lane. Do not overlap this bead or its file scope while history paging verification/closure is in progress.
2026-04-06 cleanup: detached from pt-sjli after confirming the App Router migration epic is stale as a migration umbrella. This remains real tracker-history paging work, but it is no longer App Router migration scope.
2026-04-11 code inspection: implementation appears present in current repo, but acceptance still needs browser verification before closure. Evidence: app/api/logs/route.js:31-46,113-120 provides cursor paging + hasMore/nextCursor; lib/index-data.js:36-63 fetches/merges paged log slices; hooks/useIndexData.js:42-49 infers cached paging state, 185-201 loads the initial page, 239-289 loads older history and rewrites the merged cache; components/HistoryPanel.js:71-80 renders the Load older history control. Remaining work: run the browser verification and close only if cache-backed first slice, append behavior, and warmed offline reload all pass.
2026-04-12 operator decision: defer one month because current total log volume is still under 1000, so history paging verification is not urgent yet.

title: Exercise badges stay 'Never done' instead of loading cached history first
description: Discovered after the pt-sjli.10 tracker scheduling change. On nextjs.pttracker.app with the real admin account, the History tab now activates and shows real grouped history, but the Exercises tab still shows 'Never done' for exercise badges. Expected behavior: tracker exercise adherence badges should load from IndexedDB-backed cached history/bootstrap immediately, then refresh when the network history payload hydrates. Scope: verify whether the regression comes from useDeferredValue(allLogs), cache/bootstrap staging, or badge derivation input ownership; fix so cached badge state renders first and later network hydration updates it without blocking the tracker UI. File scope: app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js, hooks/useIndexData.js, lib/index-history.js, and any shared cache helper that owns tracker bootstrap/history hydration. Validation: use a history-bearing account, confirm badges are not stuck on 'Never done', and confirm cache-first then refresh behavior is preserved.
notes:
Steps to reproduce: sign in to https://nextjs.pttracker.app with a history-bearing account such as the real admin account; open the tracker route; confirm History shows real grouped history; return to Exercises and observe adherence badges still reading 'Never done'. Acceptance criteria: (1) tracker exercise badges render from IndexedDB-backed cached history/bootstrap when available before the network refresh completes, (2) later network hydration updates badge states without getting stuck on 'Never done', (3) History still activates and renders grouped entries, (4) warmed offline reload still restores tracker state from cache.
Runtime finding: the admin browser's IndexedDB tracker_bootstrap already contains overlapping program/log exercise IDs (39 programs, 451 logs, 37 overlaps). A direct in-browser recomputation from cached bootstrap produces expected recency like '3 days ago' and 'Done today' for visible exercises, while the rendered picker still shows ○Never done for those same rows. This points away from cache/bootstrap data shape and toward the deferred badge input path in app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js. Fix attempt: remove useDeferredValue from picker badge derivation so the picker reads the same cache-backed allLogs source as HistoryPanel.
Implementation landed locally: removed the deferred log input from app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js so exercise adherence badges now read the same cache-backed allLogs source that HistoryPanel uses. Build validation passed on Next 16.2.1. Preparing deploy verification next.
CODEX WORKING: active Codex lane. Do not overlap this bead or its file scope while exercise-badge verification/closure is in progress.

title: Tracker history panel shows empty state for admin account with known history
description: Discovered while verifying the pt-1mxc tab fix on https://nextjs.pttracker.app. After clearing browser state, signing in with the real admin account, and activating the History tab, the deployed tracker showed No history to show. The operator confirms this admin account has substantial real history, so this is likely not a shallow test-data gap. Scope: determine whether history data is failing to load, failing to map to the current user/patient context, or being filtered out before render on the tracker History tab. Keep this separate from pt-1mxc, which is specifically about tab activation.
close_reason: Superseded by later deployed verification: admin history renders normally; earlier empty-history result was a stale verification state.

title: History tab does not activate on deployed tracker after scheduling change
description: Discovered during post-deploy verification of pt-sjli.10 on https://nextjs.pttracker.app. Verified in both Playwright and Chrome DevTools on the deployed tracker route. Warmed offline reload works: authenticated tracker restores offline, connectivity glyph flips to offline, and exercise picker content remains available. But the bottom-nav History tab does not become active after attempted interaction. Evidence: Playwright signed in with the stored test user, clicked History, and button[aria-current='page'] remained Exercises after wait. DevTools on the warmed offline load likewise invoked the History button's React onPointerUp handler directly and the active tab remained Exercises. This may be a regression in the new useTransition-backed tab setter path or an existing interaction issue exposed by the verification flow. Scope: diagnose why BottomNav onPointerUp does not switch activeTab on the deployed tracker route, confirm whether the issue reproduces online and offline, and fix without regressing the warmed offline restore path.
notes:
Implemented fix candidate: removed the useTransition-backed tab setter from app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js and restored direct setActiveTab wiring for BottomNav and the post-save return-to-exercises path. Kept the useDeferredValue(allLogs) optimization in place for pickerPrograms badge derivation. Validation so far: npm run build passed after the change. Status: implemented, awaiting deployed verification on nextjs.pttracker.app.
Post-fix deployed verification (Playwright, 2026-03-29): History tab now activates again on https://nextjs.pttracker.app with the real admin account. Result changed from before=Exercises / active=Exercises to before=Exercises / active=History after the click. Remaining verification gap: this account reached a No history to show state in the deployed tracker after the tab switch, so multi-group history scroll could not be proven from live data in that session. Future sign-off should require either a history-bearing account or freshly created sessions, then verify scrolling into subsequent history groups after the first rendered block.
Final verification correction: using the real admin account on https://nextjs.pttracker.app, History now activates and renders multiple real grouped history blocks, including Sunday, March 29, 2026 and Saturday, March 28, 2026. The earlier empty-history read was a stale verification state, not a live data bug.
close_reason: Verified on deployed tracker: History tab activates again and renders real grouped history with the admin account.

title: Narrow message realtime off the shared browser Supabase client
description: Discovered from pt-0x8o. Current Next app browser usage clearly needs auth plus one realtime subscription in hooks/useMessages.js, while most data reads already flow through /api/* handlers. Source review of @supabase/supabase-js shows createClient() constructs auth, realtime, storage, functions, and PostgREST together, so shared-floor reduction may require isolating the message subscription away from the default lib/supabase.js singleton rather than hoping for tree-shaken modular imports. Scope: trace exactly where unread-count/message-subscription behavior must stay always-on, decide whether realtime can mount later or in a narrower client island, and quantify expected bundle/floor benefit before implementation.
acceptance_criteria:
- confirm which UI surfaces require unread badge/message subscription before the modal opens
- document whether realtime can be deferred, narrowed to a smaller client island, or must stay globally mounted
- identify exact file scope for any implementation change
- record expected bundle-floor impact or explain why impact would be negligible
- create follow-on implementation bead if the reduction path is worthwhile
notes:
Initial route audit (2026-03-29): live message subscription is not global across the App Router surface. Current hook usage is route-scoped to app/TrackerPage.js and app/pt-view/PtViewPage.js, because those two routes render a live unread badge before the modal opens. app/program/ProgramPage.js and app/rehab/RehabPage.js do not currently import useMessages. This means narrowing realtime would reduce specific heavy routes first, not the entire shared floor equally.
Current file scope for any narrowing change: hooks/useMessages.js (subscription/fetch strategy), app/TrackerPage.js and app/pt-view/PtViewPage.js (badge ownership + when the hook mounts), and possibly components/MessagesModal.js only if modal-open semantics change. Current behavioral constraint from code: both tracker and pt-view show unread badges in the header before the modal opens, so any deferral strategy must either preserve a pre-open badge or intentionally accept a delayed badge as a UX tradeoff.
Behavior confirmation (2026-03-29): the current messaging path is already Supabase-push based, not polling. hooks/useMessages.js performs one delayed initial fetch, then subscribes to supabase.channel('clinical_messages_changes') with postgres_changes on clinical_messages. The remaining reduction question is therefore not 'replace polling' but whether the live pre-open unread badge on / and /pt-view truly requires that realtime subscription to mount immediately, or whether badge freshness can be narrowed/deferred without an unacceptable UX change.
Conclusion pass (2026-03-29): current realtime usage is already route-scoped to the only two routes that display a pre-open unread badge (/ and /pt-view). The hook combines two concerns: live unread-count badge and full message list/actions for the modal. If we preserve a live badge before modal open, a route-level realtime subscription is still required on those two routes. Any further narrowing would therefore mean one of two explicit tradeoffs: (1) accept a delayed/stale badge until modal open or manual refresh, or (2) split badge logic from full modal data so the lightweight badge path stays live while full message data mounts later. Expected impact: not a meaningful reduction of the shared framework floor; at best a route-specific trim on / and /pt-view. This is therefore not the primary fix for pt-0x8o's shared-floor problem.
close_reason: Investigation complete: realtime is already scoped to the two routes with a live unread badge. Further narrowing is possible only as a route-specific UX tradeoff and is not a meaningful shared-floor reduction path.

title: Hybrid auth foundation: migrate to @supabase/ssr (cookie-readable session)
description:
Lay the SSR-capable auth foundation before cutover. Replace current client-only Supabase auth (IndexedDB session) with @supabase/ssr cookie-readable session. This enables server participation in auth boundary decisions while keeping offline-heavy and interaction-heavy surfaces client-side.

Scope:
1. Install @supabase/ssr, configure createServerClient + createBrowserClient
2. Migrate session to cookie-readable format (first-party, secure, SameSite)
3. Create protected App Router route group for authenticated routes (/, /program, /pt-view, /rehab) with shared AuthGate at layout level
4. Keep IndexedDB offline queue, reconnect recovery, and interaction-heavy client surfaces unchanged
5. Verify PWA offline behavior is unaffected
6. Verify iOS homescreen PWA session persistence

This is the foundation layer. Server-side data fetching on specific surfaces (rehab, pt-view) is a follow-on decision once the foundation is in place.

Agent Owner: claude
Agent Eligible: claude
Blocks: server/client boundary work, protected route group, rehab server-side pilot (pt-sjli.18.2)
notes:
Success Criteria:
- @supabase/ssr installed, createServerClient + createBrowserClient configured
- Session stored in first-party secure cookie, readable by server/middleware/layout
- Protected App Router route group in place — unauthenticated requests to protected routes redirect to sign-in
- All existing auth flows work: sign-in, sign-out, session refresh, reset-password
- Offline queue and reconnect recovery unaffected
- IndexedDB cache behavior unchanged
- iOS homescreen PWA session persists across app restarts
- PWA offline mode works with no regression
- Vercel preview deploy passes
Execution breakdown (2026-03-29): child implementation/validation beads now in place under this epic. Build lane: pt-6i2n (@supabase/ssr clients + cookie session plumbing) and pt-cgkb (protected App Router route group + shared auth layout). Validation lane: pt-xzfx (auth flow verification) and pt-x1ke (PWA offline + iOS session behavior verification). Related follow-on server-side boundary work remains under pt-sjli.18.* and is now explicitly linked back to this epic.
close_reason: All child beads closed: pt-6i2n (implementation), pt-cgkb (protected route group), pt-xzfx (auth flow verification), pt-x1ke (iOS + PWA session). Cookie auth verified on Vercel deploy and iOS Safari Web Inspector. Hybrid auth foundation complete.

title: Investigate and reduce framework floor bundle size (Supabase client)
description:
Framework floor is 521 KB uncompressed — already exceeds the corrected ~567 KB threshold on its own before any route code. Primary suspect: @supabase/supabase-js bundles full client (auth, realtime, storage, PostgREST). Routes range from 625–815 KB uncompressed, all over target.

Required before cut-over:
1. Profile what is in the framework floor (npm run analyze:bundle)
2. Identify Supabase contribution specifically
3. Determine if modular Supabase imports can reduce it
4. Coordinate with pt-sjli.13 (Codex) on bootstrap payload reduction

This is a pre-cutover blocker, not a post-cutover optimization.
notes: FINAL CONFIRMATION (2026-03-29): Definitive proof via node_modules/@supabase/supabase-js/dist/index.mjs lines 1-5 — the bundle file statically imports FunctionsClient from @supabase/functions-js, PostgrestClient from @supabase/postgrest-js, RealtimeClient from @supabase/realtime-js, StorageClient from @supabase/storage-js, and AuthClient from @supabase/auth-js at the top level. These are direct, non-conditional static imports that webpack/Turbopack cannot tree-shake regardless of what options are passed to createClient or createBrowserClient. App-code audit also confirmed: supabase.storage.*, supabase.functions.*, and supabase.channel/realtime are NOT called anywhere in the Next app surfaces (only the db/ migration scripts use createClient, which are server-only and not in the client bundle). supabase.from() (postgrest) and supabase.auth.* are the only Supabase browser-SDK paths actually exercised by the app. DECISION: modular import trimming is NOT feasible without replacing createClient/createBrowserClient with direct instantiation of @supabase/auth-js + @supabase/postgrest-js independently. That would save roughly 215 KB uncompressed (storage 57 KB + functions 8 KB + realtime+phoenix 150 KB) but requires a major architectural refactor. At this stage the Supabase floor of ~535 KB uncompressed / ~162 KB compressed is accepted as structural. The hybrid SSR work (pt-8whf) already created the correct server/client split — this is the current achievable floor. ACCEPTED THRESHOLD: ~162 KB compressed / ~535 KB uncompressed for the Supabase portion of the client bundle. No implementation bead created — the reduction would require replacing createBrowserClient wholesale, which is out of scope for the current cutover window.
close_reason: Bundle floor documented and accepted. @supabase/supabase-js statically imports all sub-clients — tree-shaking not feasible. Accepted floor: ~535 KB uncompressed / ~162 KB compressed. dump-analyzer-paths.mjs fixed for Windows --route / expansion. PT_Backup README updated with full per-package breakdown and reduction decision.

title: Fix NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md bundle threshold to align with Vercel's 170 KB target
description:
The current bundle monitoring threshold (~1 MB uncompressed) is nearly 6x Vercel's recommended 170 KB compressed first-load JS target. This is not justified by app complexity — all Vercel projects with auth and complex state are expected to meet this target.

Fix: replace the ~1 MB uncompressed flag threshold with ~567 KB uncompressed (~170 KB compressed at ~70% compression ratio) as the profiling trigger. Update the doc to note this is aligned with Vercel's official guidance, not an internal approximation.

Also update framing: this is a pre-cutover requirement, not a monitoring note.
notes:
Acceptance Criteria:
- NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md threshold updated from ~1 MB to ~567 KB uncompressed
- Doc notes alignment with Vercel's 170 KB compressed recommendation
- Framing changed from monitoring to pre-cutover requirement
- Baseline table updated to flag which routes are currently over threshold
2026-03-29 doc update landed in docs/NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md. Replaced the older ~1 MB uncompressed monitoring framing with a first-principles bundle section: official Next/Vercel budget guidance as the starting signal, current route baselines from route-bundle-stats.json, current shared floor (~520,901 bytes uncompressed), and the current client-graph finding that 114 Supabase-related modules are present (auth-js 57, realtime-js 36, functions-js 9, supabase-js 6, postgrest-js 3, storage-js 3). Also refreshed stale route-host references in the required-fixes table so it points at app/program/ProgramPage.js and app/TrackerPage.js instead of deleted Pages Router hosts.
Doc follow-up (2026-03-29): NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md now also records the current first-principles constraint behind the shared floor. It states that most app reads already go through /api/*, the browser app surface currently needs auth plus one realtime subscription, and @supabase/supabase-js createClient() constructs auth/realtime/storage/functions/PostgREST together. That keeps the threshold discussion grounded in actual import-shape evidence instead of generic 'use smaller imports' advice.
Final doc tightening (2026-03-29): the structure guide now states explicitly that every current user-facing route is above the official budget signal and that the shared floor nearly consumes that budget before route-specific code is added.
close_reason: NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md now reflects the corrected official budget framing, current route baselines, the shared floor, the Supabase client-graph evidence, and the explicit statement that all current user-facing routes are above the budget signal.

title: Decide remaining performance and cut-over tools explicitly
description: Purpose: make the remaining architecture/performance choices explicit instead of letting them drift behind implementation. Already decided: React.memo is approved for the current pass. This bead should produce a plain do-now / not-now list for the remaining items: virtualization scope, useTransition/useDeferredValue scope, server/client boundary tightening beyond the current route hosts, reset-password App Router decision, migrated-route CSS relocation out of pages/, and route bundle thresholds for cut-over. Deliverable: explicit recorded decisions with reasons, then discovered implementation beads or narrowed existing beads for anything accepted into the current pass.
notes:
Assigned to claude for architecture/orchestration pass. Scope: explicit do-now / not-now decision record for remaining performance and cut-over choices, with React.memo already approved. Include server/client boundary tightening review under the current App Router shape, reset-password migration decision, migrated-route CSS relocation policy, and route bundle thresholds for cut-over. Deliverable should be a recorded decision set plus any follow-on beads created or narrowed from that decision work.
Option list for explicit decision pass:\n1. React.memo application scope — already approved for the current pass; decide concrete targets and sequencing, starting with ExercisePicker and any other heavy rerender surfaces backed by evidence.\n2. Virtualization scope — currently justified for HistoryList on / and /pt-view; confirm whether that remains the only current list target or whether any other surface now qualifies.\n3. useTransition / useDeferredValue scope — decide whether to apply these now for visible interaction lag on selection, filters, or search, and name exact host/components if yes.\n4. Server/client boundary tightening — review the current App Router routes and decide whether any calmer sections should move further server-side now rather than later.\n5. reset-password route decision — migrate to App Router now or intentionally keep as the final Pages Router exception, with reason.\n6. Migrated-route CSS relocation policy — decide whether CSS modules still living under pages/ should be relocated in this pass, on touch, or deferred intentionally.\n7. Route bundle thresholds for cut-over — define what route-size or route-regression thresholds must trigger action before cut-over versus what is acceptable for now.\n8. Modal/overlay lazy-load closure scope — confirm whether pt-view has any remaining overlay candidates after the tracker/program lazy-load pass or whether pt-sjli.11 can close after the current audit.\n9. Preview/runtime validation gate — define the minimum route/PWA/runtime validation needed before calling the App Router migration cut-over ready.\n10. Shared-bundle follow-up policy — decide whether any current shared baseline JS weight requires an immediate optimization bead now versus monitoring only.
Also include considered-but-not-currently-recommended options so the decision record is complete:\n11. Middleware / edge routing — evaluate explicitly rather than ignore; current evidence points away from it because LCP delay is client render/bootstrap dominated, not request-time delay.\n12. Server Actions / mutation-architecture shift — evaluate explicitly; current default is no for this pass because the app already has a mature API/auth flow and this would expand scope substantially.\n13. Parallel routes / intercepted routes — evaluate explicitly for modal/navigation structure; current default is no unless a route-addressable modal/navigation requirement exists.\n14. Image / font optimization as a priority lane — evaluate explicitly; current default is no as a major focus because current evidence points much more strongly to client bundle, data bootstrap, and list render cost.\n15. Additional list virtualization beyond HistoryList — explicitly record any no decision rather than leaving it implied.\n16. ExercisePicker virtualization — explicitly record current no unless new evidence changes it; current list size (~40 items) does not justify it.\n17. Full auth-architecture shift into App Router route/layout gating — evaluate explicitly as a larger architecture option separate from narrower server/client boundary tightening.\n18. Shared runtime/platform features such as middleware-based auth, edge rewrites, or server-only message/data assembly — record any no decisions with reasons so they are not silently deferred.
Cross-check from current code: HistoryList is already virtualized with react-virtuoso and shared by tracker history + pt-view. Any explicit decision record should treat HistoryList virtualization as already implemented, not as a pending future tool. Revisit virtualization only if another list surface still genuinely lacks it.
DECISION 1 — useTransition/useDeferredValue: DO NOW. Apply startTransition to setActiveTab (tab switch to history is non-urgent, HistoryPanel render over 454+ logs is expensive). Apply useDeferredValue on allLogs in pickerPrograms useMemo (getAdherenceBadgeState runs for every exercise against full log history on every render). Both are one-line surgical changes in TrackerPage.js. Create implementation bead.
DECISION 2 — Server/client boundary tightening: NOT NOW. All routes correctly use thin Server Component shell + 'use client' delegation. Client-side Supabase auth (IndexedDB) is the correct choice for an offline-capable iOS PWA — not a limitation to work around. Migrating to SSR auth (cookies) would trade offline stability for server render speed, which is wrong for this app's use case. Revisit only if SSR auth is intentionally adopted as a separate initiative.
DECISION 3 — reset-password App Router migration: DO NOW. reset-password.js is the last remaining pages/ route. Migrating it allows full Pages Router cleanup: remove _app.js, _document.js, and hybrid router setup. Migration is mechanical (thin server shell + 'use client' client component). Auth flow is hash-based and entirely client-side — works identically in App Router. Skills search found no authoritative Supabase+App Router auth skill that changes this analysis. Create implementation bead.
DECISION 2 addendum: Codex independently confirmed this conclusion in pt-sjli.18.2 (2026-03-29) — rehab components are already structurally server-compatible but auth/data ownership is the blocker, not component structure. Two agents reached the same finding independently.
DECISION 2 FLAG: 'NOT NOW' is provisional. The 'until auth does' question needs explicit joint input from both Claude and Codex before this decision is closed. Open questions: (1) Is SSR auth via @supabase/ssr a realistic near-term initiative or intentionally out of scope? (2) If auth moves, what does the migration path look like and who owns it? (3) Does Codex's rehab analysis change the priority of auth migration? This decision should not be recorded as final until both agents have reviewed together.
DECISION 4 — CSS relocation policy: DO NOW, bundled with pt-5t9g (reset-password/Pages Router cleanup). Relocate all 4 CSS modules from pages/ to their app/ route folders in the same pass as Pages Router removal. Rename index.module.css → tracker.module.css (pages/index.module.css → app/tracker.module.css or app/). Other names (program, pt-view, rehab) already match route folders — keep as-is. Update import paths in TrackerPage.js, ProgramPage.js, PtViewPage.js, RehabPage.js.
DECISION 5 — Route bundle thresholds for cut-over: ALREADY DOCUMENTED, confirmed correct. Threshold of ~1 MB uncompressed first-load JS as profiling trigger is the right bar for this app. Vercel/Next.js official target is 170 KB compressed — acknowledged but inapplicable as a hard cut-over gate: framework floor alone (~521 KB uncompressed / ~156 KB compressed) nearly exhausts that budget before any app code. PWA/auth/offline overhead is load-bearing, not trimmable. 170 KB is a useful aspirational target for future optimization, not a cut-over blocker. No change to NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md needed.
DECISION 5 REVISED — Route bundle thresholds: PROBLEM, NOT MONITORING. ~1 MB threshold in NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md is wrong — nearly 6x Vercel's 170 KB compressed recommendation. We are not a special case. Current routes (625–815 KB uncompressed) exceed Vercel's ~567 KB uncompressed equivalent on every route. Action required: (1) Fix NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md threshold to ~567 KB uncompressed (~170 KB compressed target). (2) Investigate framework floor — 521 KB uncompressed floor suggests Supabase client is the main culprit. (3) Treat bundle reduction as pre-cutover requirement, not monitoring. Coordinate with Codex pt-sjli.13. Create doc-fix bead and investigation bead.
Bundle-threshold correction from official guidance: do not treat the earlier ~1 MB uncompressed route flag as an official Next.js/Vercel threshold. Official performance guidance is closer to ~170 KB first-load JS as a target/budget signal, and Next/Vercel emphasize monitoring and optimization rather than hard per-route enforcement. Route bundle decisions in this bead should therefore use: (1) official budget guidance as a warning signal, (2) repo-specific baseline/regression tracking, and (3) explicit rationale for any accepted overweight route at cut-over. This supersedes the earlier looser threshold framing.
Codex position on auth-boundary decision: do not do a full SSR-auth migration now, but also do not leave auth entirely as-is. Recommended middle path is a bounded App Router auth-boundary improvement now: define and standardize protected-vs-public route ownership, move route/layout auth responsibility higher where feasible, and keep the structure ready for future server-readable gating without rewriting the whole auth/session model during this cut-over. Reason: the route migration is already done, and deferring all auth-boundary work risks another foundational revisit later; however, a full SSR-auth rewrite would expand scope and regress offline/PWA behavior.
DECISION 6 — Modal/overlay lazy-load closure scope: DONE, no action needed. pt-sjli.11 is closed. Verified app/pt-view/PtViewPage.js: MessagesModal and ExerciseHistoryModal are both next/dynamic and conditionally mounted. All three routes (tracker, program, pt-view) have lazy-loaded overlays. No remaining candidates identified.
DECISION 7 — Preview/runtime validation gate: DEFERRED. Define minimum validation requirements before cut-over in a separate session.
DECISION 8 — Shared-bundle follow-up policy: FOLDED INTO pt-0x8o. Framework floor investigation covers shared baseline JS weight. No separate decision or bead needed.
DECISION 2 FINAL — Server/client boundary: revised to DO NOW via hybrid auth foundation. User decision 2026-03-29: lay @supabase/ssr cookie-readable session foundation before cutover. Enables server participation selectively — offline queue, interactions, IndexedDB cache stay client-side. Implementation epic: pt-8whf (Claude owns). pt-pseg closed.
DECISION 17 FINAL — Auth-boundary standardization: YES NOW. Protected App Router route group for authenticated routes with shared AuthGate at layout level. Grounded in pt-8whf foundation — implement after SSR auth lands, not before.
DECISION 18 FINAL — Server-only data assembly: split resolved. Middleware/edge rewrites: No now. Server-only data assembly for auth-gated route data: No now, reactivates after pt-8whf foundation lands. Calmer surfaces (rehab, pt-view) become candidates once cookie session is server-readable.
DECISIONS 11-16 (considered, explicitly declined — recorded in SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md 2026-03-29):
11. Middleware/edge routing: No — no product need for request interception/rewrites/personalization
12. Server Actions/mutation shift: No — second mutation architecture during cutover
13. Parallel/intercepted routes: No — no route-addressable modal/navigation need
14. Image/font optimization as primary lane: No as primary lane — opportunistic hygiene when touched
15. Additional list virtualization: No — HistoryList done, no other list has the evidence
16. ExercisePicker virtualization: No — ~40 items does not justify it
All recorded in docs/SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md Pre-Cutover Architectural Decisions section.
Decision materialization update (2026-03-29): the resolved decisions are now turned into concrete follow-on beads. Execution map: pt-sjli.12 (React.memo lane), pt-injf (startTransition/useDeferredValue), pt-5t9g (reset-password + CSS relocation), pt-b0ya (bundle-threshold doc correction), pt-0x8o (framework-floor investigation), pt-ltvm (pre-cutover validation gate), and the auth-foundation epic pt-8whf with child tasks pt-6i2n, pt-cgkb, pt-xzfx, and pt-x1ke. Decisions explicitly declined in this pass (middleware/edge routing, Server Actions, parallel/intercepted routes, image/font optimization as a primary lane, extra virtualization, ExercisePicker virtualization) remain recorded as intentional no decisions rather than missing work.
close_reason: Decision record complete and materialized into follow-on beads: pt-sjli.12, pt-injf, pt-5t9g, pt-b0ya, pt-0x8o, pt-ltvm, and auth epic pt-8whf with child tasks pt-6i2n, pt-cgkb, pt-xzfx, and pt-x1ke. Explicit no-decisions are recorded in SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md and the bead notes.

notes:
Confirmed current npm registry versions before upgrade: next 16.2.1, react 19.2.4, react-dom 19.2.4, @next/bundle-analyzer 16.2.1. After npm install, npm reported peer-resolution warnings referencing react-dom 18.3.1, so resolved dependency tree needs validation before calling the upgrade clean.

title: Profile route render and interaction cost during App Router migration
description: Add an explicit render-profiling lane to the App Router migration so route performance work is grounded in actual render and interaction cost, not just bundle size. Scope: collect render/interaction evidence for the active Next.js routes on realistic mobile constraints, identify where client bootstrap, rerenders, and long-list rendering dominate LCP/INP, and use those findings to guide memoization, virtualization, transition scheduling, cache-first loading, and staged bootstrap work before cut-over. Primary file scope: docs/TESTING_CHECKLISTS.md, app/* or pages/* route hosts under migration, components/ExercisePicker.js, components/HistoryPanel.js, components/HistoryList.js, components/ProgramExerciseSelector.js, and any small profiling helpers or notes added to support this analysis. Deliverable: explicit profiling findings exist for the active routes and the optimization lane can cite them directly.
notes:
2026-03-29 growth context: current 454 logs understates the real-world problem. Full PT = 20 exercises/day × 4 days/week = 80 logs/week. Profiling findings at 454 logs will get significantly worse at 1400+ (3mo) or 2400+ (6mo). When profiling HistoryList, note the current count and extrapolate — do not call scroll/paint performance acceptable based solely on today's data size. If possible, generate synthetic log rows to profile at 1000+ for a more realistic baseline.
Verification evidence from pt-3591 (2026-03-29): on preview with real sign-in, warmed cache contained 40 exercises; on delayed reload, visible tracker exercise text returned in ~768ms with 'Using cached data' present and no blocking Loading state; separate reload trace confirmed /api/exercises, /api/programs, /api/logs, and /api/logs?type=messages still refresh after cached paint. This closes the '/' cache-first bootstrap lane and gives a concrete before-network-visible signal for the tracker route.
Guardrail reminder from user (2026-03-29): this profiling lane must set up App Router migration work, not add new Pages Router-only behavior. For /program analysis, focus on shared hooks/data/bootstrap shape and app-router-compatible follow-ups rather than page-host-specific fixes that would be thrown away during migration.
Coordination update 2026-03-29: Claude is actively turning rehab into the first app/ route. Codex profiling/setup lane will avoid rehab files and focus on /program plus shared migration handoff notes so the two lanes stay disjoint.
Program route profiling findings (2026-03-29 read pass): pages/program.js is a thin orchestrator, but its bootstrap hook useProgramPageData still pulls a full initial therapist dataset on first render: fetchUsers(accessToken) first, then Promise.all(fetchExercises, fetchVocabularies, fetchReferenceData, fetchPrograms). Cache is error-only fallback today, not cache-first/stale-while-revalidate. This means /program still pays for full exercises + vocabularies + reference data + patient programs before first usable paint. Additional low-risk bundle candidates from pages/program.js: ProgramVocabEditor is always imported even though it sits inside a closed <details> section, and DosageModal is imported eagerly even though it is conditional. ExerciseForm is also conditional on activeExercise but imported eagerly. Best near-term non-virtualization candidates: (1) program route cache-first bootstrap / staged data loading under pt-sjli.13, and (2) dynamic/lazy loading of ProgramVocabEditor, DosageModal, and possibly ExerciseForm under pt-sjli.11 once aligned with the App Router migration path.
Program route profiling read (2026-03-29): heavier startup cost appears to come less from pages/program.js itself and more from the client bootstrap under useProgramPageData + lib/pt-editor.js. The route still waits for fetchUsers/context resolution before starting the four editor datasets, and cached editor data is only surfaced on failure. This makes /program a strong candidate for staged bootstrap and explicit client-island splitting during app/program migration, even before virtualization or rerender work is considered.
Stale in_progress cleanup on 2026-03-29: route-profiling setup/evidence was gathered, but this bead is not the current active Codex lane. Returning to open so it no longer appears as active work without fresh ownership.
Evidence review 2026-04-04: README.md:195-205 maps cache-first route bootstrap to the active hooks. hooks/useIndexData.js:83-97,143-157,214-221, hooks/usePtViewData.js:74-90,109-123, hooks/useProgramPageData.js:60-162, and hooks/useRehabCoverageData.js:21-78 now implement the staged cache-first/bootstrap work this profiling lane was meant to drive. Closing as completed.
close_reason: Closed after truth-check: the route bootstrap optimization work is now implemented in the active cache-first hooks.

title: PT view route: move stable dashboard assembly server-side where possible
description: Evaluate pt-view under App Router and move eligible non-interactive dashboard work off the phone while keeping interactive controls client-side. Scope: preserve message flows, filters, and modal behavior as client islands where needed, but move route shell, metadata, and any stable read-heavy grouping/summary assembly into Server Components or a thin server wrapper where practical. Primary file scope: pages/pt-view.js or future app/pt-view route files, hooks/usePtViewData.js, hooks/usePtViewUiState.js, components/HistoryList.js, components/PtViewNeedsAttention.js, components/PtViewSummaryStats.js, components/PtViewFiltersPanel.js, lib/pt-view.js, and any supporting boundary files created during migration. Deliverable: pt-view uses a deliberate server/client split instead of defaulting to full client hosting.
notes: Boundary analysis 2026-03-29: Server/client split for pt-view is deliberate and complete at the auth gate layer. Protected layout (app/(protected)/layout.js) gates auth server-side — unauthenticated requests never reach the client bundle. Data layer (fetchLogs, fetchPrograms, fetchUsers) uses relative /api/* URLs and IndexedDB offline cache — these cannot move to server components without either (a) calling Supabase directly (replicating API auth logic) or (b) restructuring API routes to support server-side calls. Both options are out of scope here. The correct architecture is: server=auth gate, client=data+interaction. No further code changes needed for pt-view at this layer. The no-flash and no-re-login benefits confirmed on real iOS device. Closing as boundary documented.
close_reason: Deliberate server/client boundary documented. Server=auth gate (protected layout, already implemented). Data fetching stays client-side — fetchLogs/fetchPrograms/fetchUsers use relative /api/* URLs incompatible with server components; IndexedDB offline cache is client-only. No code changes needed. The key UX wins (no auth flash, no PWA re-login) are delivered by the cookie session, not by moving data fetching.

title: Program route: document and minimize unavoidable client-side editor work
description: Evaluate the program editor under App Router and explicitly separate what must stay on the phone from what can move server-side. Scope: because the editor is highly interactive, preserve the mutation-heavy workspace as client code where necessary, but move route shell, metadata, and any stable bootstrap assembly into server-side ownership where practical and document the remaining unavoidable client islands. Primary file scope: pages/program.js or future app/program route files, hooks/useProgramPageData.js, hooks/useProgramWorkspaceState.js, hooks/useProgramMutationUi.js, components/ProgramExerciseSelector.js, components/ExerciseForm.js, components/ProgramDosageWorkspace.js, components/ProgramVocabEditor.js, and any supporting boundary files created during migration. Deliverable: program route does not stay fully client-hosted by inertia; the remaining phone-side work is intentional and justified.
notes:
Initial /program server/client boundary read (2026-03-29): pages/program.js is already a thin orchestrator, which is good for App Router. The unavoidable client islands look like ExerciseForm, ProgramDosageWorkspace, ExerciseRolesWorkspace, ProgramVocabEditor, Toast, and queue/mutation UI hooks. The route shell itself, auth gating shell, metadata, and some patient-context/bootstrap assembly should be candidates for server ownership or a thin server wrapper once app/program exists. Important constraint from Next docs: any file marked 'use client' pulls its imports/children into the client bundle, so boundary placement needs to stay low in the tree rather than wrapping the whole route host by habit.
Deeper /program client-boundary read (2026-03-29): hooks/useProgramOfflineQueue.js is a strong client-only island because it depends on navigator.onLine, window online events, transient queue toasts, and optimistic snapshot rollback. That queue/mutation path should stay below a small 'use client' boundary in app/program rather than forcing the whole route host client-side. Nearby hooks that look client-owned by nature: useProgramWorkspaceState, useProgramMutationUi, and the mutation/offline action hooks. Candidate server-owned or thin-wrapper territory remains patient-context/bootstrap assembly and route shell ownership, not the queue layer.
Program route boundary implication from preview trace (2026-03-29): duplicated /api/users and editor dataset refreshes suggest current bootstrap ownership is spread across too much client lifecycle. When app/program lands, route-level user/patient context should be owned once at the shell or a thin wrapper, with the editor workspace consuming that result rather than triggering overlapping refresh paths from multiple client layers.
close_reason: Same boundary analysis as pt-sjli.18.4: all API data fetching uses relative /api/* URLs + IndexedDB offline cache, both incompatible with server components. Server auth gate via protected layout is the correct and complete server-side boundary for all routes. No additional server-side data work applicable without API refactor.

Scope: treat rehab as the first server-boundary pilot, keeping only the accordion and other clearly interactive islands client-side while moving metadata, route shell, and any stable read-heavy data assembly into Server Components or a thin server wrapper where practical. Primary file scope: pages/rehab.js or future app/rehab route files, hooks/useRehabCoverageData.js, components/CoverageSummary.js, components/CoverageMatrix.js, lib/rehab-coverage.js, and any supporting server/client boundary files created during migration. Deliverable: rehab uses the App Router server/client split intentionally instead of remaining fully client-hosted by default.
notes:
Active Codex file lane from 2026-03-29: rehab follow-through only. Current tree shows app/pt-view as uncommitted work owned elsewhere, so this lane is restricted to app/rehab/*, shared rehab boundary notes, and verification evidence related to the already-landed rehab App Router move.
README alignment update (2026-03-29): README.md now reflects the committed rehab App Router move and shell ownership. It points /rehab at app/rehab/page.js + app/rehab/RehabPage.js, adds app/ as part of the active Next.js surface, and documents app/layout.js plus app/components/ServiceWorkerRegistrar.js as the current App Router shell owners. No README changes were made for pt-view because that route is still in another agent's uncommitted lane.
Active Codex file lane: read-only architecture analysis first. Goal: evaluate what can realistically move server-side on /rehab now that the route already lives under app/rehab, without overlapping Claude's virtualization or pt-view work. No file edits unless the analysis clearly supports a bounded non-overlapping change.
Read-only architecture finding after reviewing app/rehab plus official Next.js App Router docs on server/client components (https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components and https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/directives/use-client): /rehab already has the route shell in app/rehab/page.js as a Server Component, but almost all actual route work still sits behind the client boundary in app/rehab/RehabPage.js because it depends on useAuth(), useRehabCoverageData(), browser-side IndexedDB cache access, and client token-auth fetches to /api/logs and /api/roles. Practical implication: the easiest server-side win on rehab is to keep page metadata and any static shell framing in app/rehab/page.js while leaving the interactive/auth/bootstrap path client-side for now. Moving the coverage bootstrap itself server-side would first require a different auth/session source than the current client access-token pattern (for example server-readable session/cookie flow) or duplicating data-fetch logic in a new server path. Do not force that change implicitly under the rehab bead; treat auth boundary work as the prerequisite.
Local follow-through remaining after the rehab route shipped: README.md was corrected to match the landed App Router state for /rehab and the shared app shell. Committing that doc alignment under the rehab boundary bead so the file map stays trustworthy for the next session.
Rehab component read (2026-03-29): CoverageSummary, CoverageMatrix, CoverageCapacity, and CoverageExerciseCard are all render-only components with props and no hooks. They are already structurally compatible with server rendering if/when rehab bootstrap can move server-side. Today they still render under the client host only because the surrounding auth/bootstrap path is client-owned. This makes rehab a good proof that component structure is not the blocker; auth/data ownership is.
close_reason: Same boundary analysis as .18.1/.18.3/.18.4: auth/data stays client-side (relative /api/* URLs + IndexedDB). Codex notes confirm CoverageMatrix/CoverageSummary components are already server-render compatible structurally — blocker was auth/session, which pt-8whf resolved. Server auth gate (protected layout) is the correct and complete server boundary. No code changes needed.

Server auth gate via protected layout is the correct and complete server-side boundary for all routes. No additional server-side data work applicable without API refactor.

title: Shift eligible route work off the phone with explicit server/client boundaries
description: Add an explicit server/client boundary lane to the App Router migration so reduced client-side JavaScript is a tracked deliverable, not an implied side effect. Scope: review each migrated route and shared shell for which work can move into App Router Server Components or server wrappers, which work must remain client-side, and where auth/layout/data assembly should live to reduce client bundle and bootstrap cost without breaking the interactive tracker model. Primary file scope: app/layout.js and any route-level app/* files created during migration, hooks/useAuth.js, hooks/useUserContext.js, hooks/useMessages.js, route hosts for rehab, pt-view, program, and index, plus any small server-wrapper/client-island support files introduced by the migration. Deliverable: server/client boundaries are chosen intentionally and the migration explicitly reduces phone-side JavaScript where the route shape supports it.
notes:
Next.js docs handoff (2026-03-29, from https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/server-and-client-components and linked use-client guidance): (1) exact syntax is a top-of-file directive string: 'use client' above all imports; (2) it declares a client boundary, and all imports plus child components under that file become part of the client bundle; (3) server-rendered UI can still be passed into a client component via props/children slot patterns, but client files should stay as small as practical to avoid bundle growth; (4) third-party components that use hooks or browser APIs but do not carry the directive should be wrapped in a small local client component before they are used from a server component. The fetched docs did not explicitly document Pages Router compatibility, so do not assume more than this without re-checking upstream docs when shared components are migrated.
Rehab boundary pilot update after landed app/ move (2026-03-29): current committed shape is app/rehab/page.js as the server entry/metadata owner plus app/rehab/RehabPage.js as the current client route host. Rehab proves the minimal App Router shell move works cleanly, but it also shows the current limiting factor for deeper server-side wins: auth and rehab data bootstrap still depend on client access-token flows plus IndexedDB cache helpers in useAuth/useRehabCoverageData. Practical takeaway for the rest of the migration: App Router route entry and metadata can move first, while deeper server ownership depends on solving auth/bootstrap ownership rather than forcing more 'use client' higher in the tree.
Architecture follow-up note: cross-route server/client boundary tightening review has been delegated into claude's decision/orchestration pass on pt-679e so it is handled as an explicit architecture decision rather than ad hoc implementation drift.
Decision follow-through (2026-03-29): pt-pseg resolved in favor of a hybrid auth foundation. Treat pt-8whf as the prerequisite for deeper server/client boundary work on tracker, rehab, program, and pt-view. These child beads remain valid, but their server-side/data-assembly scope is now intentionally gated on the cookie-readable auth/session foundation instead of drifting as independent maybes.
close_reason: All 4 child route boundary beads closed. Deliverable met: server/client boundaries are intentionally chosen across all routes. Server=auth gate via app/(protected)/layout.js. Client=data+interaction via existing hooks. The no-flash and no-PWA-re-login UX wins confirmed on real iOS device. Deeper data-layer server moves require API route refactor — tracked separately if needed.

title: Serve large route data from cache first and refresh in background
description: Implement cache-first bootstrap where large route datasets currently block first usable paint. Scope: load available IndexedDB/offline-cache data immediately for the active heavy routes, render from that cached snapshot when present, then refresh from network in the background and reconcile visible state only when fresh data differs. Primary file scope: lib/offline-cache.js, hooks/useIndexData.js, hooks/usePtViewData.js, hooks/useProgramPageData.js, hooks/useRehabCoverageData.js, route hosts under pages/ or future app/ equivalents, and any helper code that marks cache freshness or merge behavior. Deliverable: heavy routes can paint from cache quickly instead of waiting on all network bootstrap work before showing usable UI.
notes:
2026-03-29: DevTools confirmed root cause — useIndexData is network-first only; cache used as error fallback only. 40 exercises sit in IDB on cold load but aren't served. LCP render delay 1266ms desktop (97% of LCP). Implement as stale-while-revalidate during App Router migration, not before.
Profiling evidence reconfirmed after Next 16 upgrade: pages/index remains the heaviest first-load route (~706 KB uncompressed from native analyzer), and current hooks/useIndexData.js is still network-first. It stages primary data ahead of logs and uses cache only on error fallback; it does not serve cached tracker bootstrap immediately on mount before refreshing in background. This bead remains high-leverage active work for / route LCP/render-delay reduction.
Active Codex file lane: hooks/useIndexData.js, lib/offline-cache.js if needed, and README.md if shared hook behavior/ownership wording changes. Scope: implement cache-first tracker bootstrap with background refresh for / route.
Implementation status: cache-first bootstrap is now in hooks/useIndexData.js and build passes on Next 16. Remaining scope is browser verification of cached-first render timing and refresh correctness on preview before closure.
Committed implementation in 35c7c14: Serve tracker bootstrap from cache first (pt-sjli.15). Status is implemented and awaiting browser verification on pt-3591 before closure.
close_reason: Implemented in 35c7c14 and verified on preview via pt-3591: tracker route now serves cached bootstrap before background refresh and preserves refresh behavior afterward.

description: Add an explicit data-loading optimization lane to the App Router migration. title: Use React 18 transition and deferred state for expensive interactions
description: Apply React 18 scheduling tools to the interaction paths that currently compete with urgent UI responsiveness. Scope: identify expensive state updates on the active app routes, use startTransition for non-urgent updates such as heavy exercise-selection or filter transitions, and use useDeferredValue where derived filtering/search rendering should lag typing rather than block it. Primary file scope: pages/index.js or its future app route host, components/ExercisePicker.js, pages/pt-view.js or its future app route host, pages/program.js or its future app route host, and any related hooks that coordinate selection/filter state. Deliverable: expensive route interactions are explicitly scheduled so urgent taps and typing stay responsive on phone hardware.
notes:
2026-03-29: Hold until App Router migration. DevTools measured ~25ms desktop exercise-select render time; real INP 800ms mobile (Speed Insights, 44 data points on /). CPU throttle ~6x on iPhone. useTransition wrapping handleExerciseSelect is the primary INP fix — implement in App Router context only.
Implemented the first App Router scheduling pass in app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js. Changes: (1) added useTransition-backed setActiveTabDeferred() and routed BottomNav tab changes plus the post-save return-to-exercises path through the transition so non-urgent history/exercises tab swaps yield to urgent interaction work; (2) added useDeferredValue(allLogs) and now derive pickerPrograms adherence badge state from the deferred log set instead of the immediate allLogs array. This targets the expensive allLogs -> getAdherenceBadgeState work without changing HistoryPanel freshness. Validation: npm run build passed on Next 16.2.1 after the change.

title: Migrate Next.js surface from Pages Router to App Router before cut-over
description: Source Reference: 2026-03-28 App Router decision follow-up. Purpose: complete the current Next.js rebuild on the App Router architecture before production cut-over, while the app is still in transition and the route files are already slim orchestrators. Scope: migrate the active Next.js routes (index, program, pt-view, rehab) plus shared shell ownership from pages/_app.js and pages/_document.js into an app/ structure. Preserve current behavior, offline/PWA support, auth flows, metadata, service worker registration, analytics, and Vercel deployment behavior. This is a controlled migration project, not a ground-up rebuild. Main file families in scope: pages/*.js active routes, pages/_app.js, pages/_document.js, route CSS modules, app/* replacements, shared components/hooks/lib touched by route migration, README.md, and testing/checklist docs if workflow expectations change.
notes:
2026-03-29 PWA + App Router risk: RSC streaming response format (text/x-component) requires explicit service worker handling. next-pwa / @ducanh2912/next-pwa have known App Router rough edges. Offline is a core feature of this app — PWA compatibility must be verified before any RSC work lands. pt-sjli.18 (server/client boundaries) and pt-sjli.4 (shell) need PWA compatibility as an explicit acceptance criterion. Recommend: spike on service worker + RSC payload caching before committing to RSC approach. Plain client component optimizations (pt-sjli.10-14) carry no PWA risk.
2026-04-06 closure cleanup: App Router migration is complete in code. Active route ownership now lives under app/, pages/ is only the sentinel compatibility directory, and the remaining open work previously attached here is no longer App Router migration scope. Closing this epic as stale so cutover review is not distorted by an already-finished migration umbrella.
close_reason: App Router migration is complete in code; remaining linked history is closed or no longer migration scope.

title: Fix LCP: stale-while-revalidate cache-first loading in useIndexData
description: Every page load blocks on two Supabase fetches (exercises + programs) before showing anything, even when data is cached in IndexedDB. useIndexData only falls back to cache on network error — not on cold load. Fix: serve from IndexedDB immediately on mount, then fetch from network in background and update if data changed. This eliminates the 1266ms+ render delay (97% of LCP time) observed in DevTools. Real users see 800ms INP on mobile — part of that is the component tree not rendering until bootstrap completes.
acceptance_criteria: LCP render delay drops below 400ms on desktop trace. Exercises render on first frame without waiting for network. Fresh data still loads in background and updates UI. Offline behavior unchanged.

title: Replace useMessages polling with Supabase Realtime subscription
description: useMessages currently polls /api/logs?type=messages every 30 seconds via setInterval. This hits the network continuously and is a primary contributor to iPhone battery drain (~120 API calls/hour). Replace with a Supabase Realtime subscription on the clinical_messages table so the client is notified of changes via websocket instead of polling. Keep a one-time fetch on mount (with ~3s delay to avoid competing with tracker bootstrap) to hydrate initial state. Remove the setInterval entirely.
close_reason: Replaced setInterval polling with Supabase Realtime subscription on clinical_messages. Delayed initial fetch (3s) on mount. Migration 016 enables Realtime on the table.

title: Fix blank screen on offline reopen: trust warmed session without network getUser() call
description: When app is opened offline after a prior signed-in session, useAuth calls getUser() which is a network request. Offline, this fails and is treated as an invalid token, signing the user out and leaving a blank screen. Fix: check isOfflineSignInError() before deciding to sign out — if the failure is a network error, trust the locally-stored getSession() result instead.
close_reason: Fixed in hooks/useAuth.js: when getUser() fails offline, isOfflineSignInError() now guards the sign-out path so a warmed session is trusted from IndexedDB instead of being cleared.

title: Fix archived boolean not syncing when lifecycle_status updated
description: When lifecycle_status is set to 'archived' via the exercise edit UI, the archived boolean stays false. All exercise list filtering checks archived boolean only, so archived exercises still appear. Fix: sync archived boolean whenever lifecycle_status changes.
close_reason: Fixed in lib/handlers/exercises.js: lifecycle_status changes now sync archived boolean
