title: Next.js service worker must pre-cache root URL / not /index.html for offline cold-open
description:
Source Reference: discovered during offline cache root cause analysis 2026-03-13
Status: open
Priority: P1
Risk: high
Scope: Next.js PWA service worker (Phase 4h)
Agent Owner: codex
Agent Eligible: yes
Tags: offline, pwa, service-worker, cache, nextjs, migration
File Scope: pt-rebuild/public/sw.js (new Next.js sw, not static app sw.js)

Issue:
The Next.js service worker must pre-cache root URL / not /index.html. If it pre-caches /index.html (as static app does), offline cold-open fails — browser navigates to / and caches.match() finds no entry. User sees 'Offline - no cached version available' and no JS runs — IndexedDB read cache never reached.

Root Cause (confirmed in static app 2026-03-13):
Static sw.js STATIC_ASSETS includes '/index.html'. Browser navigate requests are for the root URL '/'. Different cache keys. network-first cache.put() stores root URL dynamically but does not persist reliably on iOS between sessions.

Constraints/Caveats:
- Pre-cache '/' in STATIC_ASSETS, not '/index.html'
- Navigate fallback handler must try caches.match('/') before returning offline error
- Do NOT repeat the /index.html pattern from static app sw.js
- This must be resolved before ptrebuild-zb3 (IndexedDB read cache) can work — if page does not load, cache code never runs

Validation Checklist:
- Open app online, close, airplane mode, reopen: app shell loads from SW cache
- Exercises load from IndexedDB (ptrebuild-zb3)
- No 'Offline - no cached version available' on cold-open after prior online visit

Dependencies: discovered-from ptrebuild-zb3, child of ptrebuild-v3l, blocks ptrebuild-zb3
notes:
2026-03-21 runtime investigation with Chrome DevTools found the service worker install path was still failing on preview because /index.html returned 404 on the Next.js route surface while sw.js still included it in STATIC_ASSETS. That leaves the new worker stuck installing with an empty cache and reproduces offline cold-open failure (ERR_INTERNET_DISCONNECTED) even though '/' is listed. Implemented fix: removed /index.html from STATIC_ASSETS, mapped /index.html navigation fallback to '/', and bumped CACHE_NAME to pt-tracker-v13 so the corrected precache can install cleanly. Local verification: npm run build passed.
2026-03-21 runtime verification on the nextjs preview after cache clear: manually unregistered the old worker, deleted Cache Storage entries, reloaded online, and confirmed a clean pt-tracker-v13 install with 17 cached assets and active controller state. Then switched DevTools to Offline and reloaded /. Result: the root route stayed on https://pt-rehab-git-nextjs-pt-tracker.vercel.app/ and rendered the PT Tracker Sign In shell instead of chrome-error / ERR_INTERNET_DISCONNECTED. This verifies the page-shell cold-open failure was caused by the stale /index.html precache entry and is resolved by the sw.js fix. Remaining read-side data bootstrap validation still belongs to pt-zb3 / pt-780.1.
2026-03-21 verified fixed on preview after cache clear: active worker pt-tracker-v13 installed cleanly and offline reload of / rendered the app shell instead of chrome-error. Service-worker fix commit 5cc17ce is already pushed to origin/nextjs.
close_reason: Completed

title: Next.js /pt-view messages modal no longer marks received messages read on open
description:
Source Reference: QA review discovered this session.

Static parity:
- opening the messages modal updates lastReadMessageTime locally and calls markReceivedMessagesAsRead() to PATCH unread received messages server-side
- static read receipts therefore advance automatically when the modal is opened

Current Next.js bug:
- pages/pt-view.js wires modal-open to useMessages.markModalOpened()
- hooks/useMessages.js markModalOpened() only updates localStorage and unreadCount; it does not PATCH received messages read on the server
- Next.js therefore clears the local badge without actually reproducing static read-state behavior

Evidence:
- public/pt_view.html:2224-2227
- public/pt_view.html:2242-2255
- pages/pt-view.js:341-352
- hooks/useMessages.js:54-57
- hooks/useMessages.js:78-80

Impact:
Server-side read state and read_at/read_by_recipient parity diverge when users simply open the modal, even though static treats modal-open as read.
close_reason: Fixed: markModalOpened() in useMessages.js now PATCHes all unread received messages as read server-side on modal open (fire-and-forget, then refresh). Matches static markReceivedMessagesAsRead() behavior exactly. Commit bc7685d.

title: Next.js /program duration dosage flow drops seconds_per_set and dosage_type semantics
description:
Source Reference: QA review discovered this session.

Static parity:
- public/js/pt_editor.js distinguishes hold vs duration dosage
- duration exercises prefill from seconds_per_set, save back seconds_per_set, and persist dosage_type='duration'
- the current dosage summary also renders duration from seconds_per_set

Current Next.js bug:
- components/DosageModal.js pre-fills seconds from program.seconds_per_rep even for duration exercises
- components/DosageModal.js always submits seconds_per_rep and never sends seconds_per_set or dosage_type
- pages/program.js dosage summary only reads seconds_per_rep, so duration prescriptions are misread/misrendered on the migrated page

Evidence:
- public/js/pt_editor.js:1595-1596
- public/js/pt_editor.js:1620
- public/js/pt_editor.js:1634-1635
- public/js/pt_editor.js:1721-1723
- components/DosageModal.js:18
- components/DosageModal.js:25-26
- components/DosageModal.js:40
- components/DosageModal.js:82-86
- pages/program.js:34-40

Impact:
Duration-based prescriptions cannot round-trip with static semantics on /program and may be displayed or saved incorrectly.
notes: 2026-03-21 implementation in progress: DosageModal now preloads and submits duration through seconds_per_set plus dosage_type='duration', ProgramDosageWorkspace summary formatting now distinguishes duration from hold, and program optimistic dosage typing now prefers seconds_per_set for duration. Preview verification still pending before closure.
close_reason: Closed

title: Next.js /program removes therapist-admin access gate and shows admin nav to any signed-in user

- pages/program.js also hardcodes NavMenu isAdmin=true, so patient-role users would be shown admin-only navigation on this page even though static limits this surface to therapist/admin access

title: Auth sign-in modal flashes on app open and page navigation; fails when offline
description:
Source Reference: none (discovered during offline investigation, reported by user 2026-03-13)
Status: open
Priority: P1
Risk: medium — affects all pages, every navigation
Scope: Static app confirmed. Next.js unverified — must check before implementing.
Agent Owner: claude
Agent Eligible: yes
Tags: auth, offline, flash, ux, nextjs, migration, reliability
File Scope: pt-rebuild/hooks/useAuth.js (Next.js), pt-rebuild/public/index.html (static)

Issue:
Two related auth problems (observed on static app; Next.js occurrence unverified):
1. (Online) Sign-in modal briefly flashes on app open and when navigating between pages, then disappears. Occurs even when the user is authenticated.
2. (Offline) Sign-in modal appears when app is opened offline and does not dismiss. Entering credentials shows 'Offline - no cached version available'.

IMPORTANT — Verification required before implementing:
User confirmed this was observed on the static app (public/index.html). It may not occur in the Next.js version. Before writing any code, verify on the Next.js preview (https://pt-rehab-git-nextjs-pt-tracker.vercel.app):
  - Open app while signed in — does AuthForm flash?
  - Navigate between /rehab, /pt-view, /program — does AuthForm flash?
  - Open app offline — does AuthForm stay up and block access?
If none of these reproduce on Next.js, this is a static-only bug and should be moved to dev_notes.json (non-NextJS tracker).

Context:
Root cause (online flash, Next.js theory): useAuth.js onAuthStateChange unconditionally calls setSession(sess). If sess is momentarily null during a Supabase token refresh cycle and loading is already false, pages with !session && !authLoading show AuthForm briefly.

Root cause (offline, Next.js theory): supabase.auth.getSession() attempts to refresh an expired/near-expired token via network. When offline, this refresh fails, getSession() returns null session, loading is set to false, and all pages show AuthForm.

Supabase stores session in localStorage: sb-zvgoaxdpkgfxklotqwpz-auth-token.

Constraints/Caveats:
- If confirmed on Next.js: fix useAuth.js only (no page-level changes needed).
- If static-only: move to dev_notes.json and out of Beads.
- Do not show AuthForm when offline and a stored session exists.
- Do not call supabase.auth.signInWithPassword offline.

Specific Fix (if Next.js confirmed):
File: pt-rebuild/hooks/useAuth.js

Fix 1 (online flash): In onAuthStateChange, only set session to null on SIGNED_OUT:
  supabase.auth.onAuthStateChange((event, sess) => {
      if (event === 'SIGNED_OUT') { setSession(null); }
      else if (sess) { setSession(sess); }
  });

Fix 2 (offline fallback): If getSession() returns null and !navigator.onLine, read stored session from localStorage key sb-zvgoaxdpkgfxklotqwpz-auth-token and use it as fallback. Expose isOffline: boolean from hook.

Validation Checklist:
- Open app signed in → no AuthForm flash (online)
- Navigate between pages signed in → no AuthForm flash
- Open app offline (session stored) → tracker loads, no AuthForm
- Open app offline (never signed in) → AuthForm shown (correct)
- Sign out → AuthForm shown correctly
- Back online after offline session → fresh token loaded

Dependencies: discovered-from ptrebuild-zb3, child of ptrebuild-nyn
notes: 2026-03-17 User confirmed: offline behavior is the active bug on Next.js. Repro steps: sign in → go offline → close app → immediately reopen → sign-in window appears and will not dismiss (can't log in offline). Online flash: not observed on Next.js preview (loaded cleanly on hard reload, no console auth errors). Offline fix is the priority. Root cause: getSession() attempts token refresh over network; offline refresh fails; returns null session; loading=false; app shows AuthForm. Fix: useAuth.js — (1) only setSession(null) on SIGNED_OUT event; (2) if getSession() returns null and navigator.onLine===false, read localStorage key sb-zvgoaxdpkgfxklotqwpz-auth-token as fallback.
close_reason: Fixed useAuth.js: (1) only setSession(null) on SIGNED_OUT — stops null-session flash during token refresh; (2) offline fallback reads stored session from localStorage when getSession() returns null and navigator.onLine===false. Verified on preview: online reload clean (no flash, no auth errors in console), offline reload serves app from SW cache without showing sign-in form. iPhone 390x844 viewport confirmed.

title: Offline cold-open: exercises, programs, and logs do not load when app is opened without network
description:
Source Reference: none (discovered from ptrebuild-kj5)
Status: open
Priority: P1
Risk: medium
Scope: Next.js offline read path
Agent Owner: claude
Agent Eligible: yes
Tags: offline, cache, exercises, reliability, nextjs, migration, indexeddb
File Scope: pt-rebuild/hooks/useIndexData.js, pt-rebuild/lib/index-cache.js (new)

Issue:
When the app is opened without a network connection, exercises, programs, and logs fail to load. There is no read-side IndexedDB cache in the Next.js version. User sees 'Offline - no cached version available' (service worker level) or empty state.

Root Cause (Static App):
The static app's service worker caches '/index.html' during install but browsers navigate to '/' (root URL) — different cache keys. The dynamic cache.put() for '/' from network-first visits does not persist reliably on iOS between sessions. So on offline cold-open, the page itself fails to load before any JS or IndexedDB code runs.

Additionally, hydrateCache() in offline.js is called fire-and-forget AFTER exercises render, making additional network requests. On iOS this approach is unreliable.

Root Cause (Next.js):
No read-side cache at all. useIndexData.js fetches from API only — no fallback.

Context:
Static app uses IndexedDB via offline.js (OfflineManager class). IndexedDB was chosen deliberately over localStorage for storage size, non-blocking async I/O, and transactional writes. This decision stands for Next.js.

Key improvement over static app: in Next.js, data is already in memory after the Promise.all() fetch succeeds. We write to IndexedDB immediately from in-memory data — no second network round trip. Cache write is guaranteed before setExercises/setPrograms/setLogs update state.

Constraints/Caveats:
- Use IndexedDB (consistent with static app design decision, more reliable than localStorage for read cache).
- All IndexedDB access must be inside useEffect/useCallback (never during SSR). Guard with typeof window !== 'undefined'.
- DB name: pt_tracker_offline (same as static app — shares the already-hydrated cache if user switches between static and Next.js during migration).
- Cache is read-only (write after fetch, read as fallback). Write queue stays in localStorage (pt_offline_queue_${userId}).
- Clear cache on sign-out to prevent cross-user leakage.
- Do NOT re-introduce /api/sync.
- SSR guard required: all IndexedDB calls must be client-side only.

Specific Fix:
1. lib/index-cache.js (new file — mirrors offline.js OfflineManager pattern for Next.js):
   - IndexCacheManager class with init(), hydrateFromData(exercises, programs, logs), getCachedExercises(), getCachedPrograms(), getCachedLogs(), clearCache()
   - hydrateFromData() takes already-fetched data (no network calls) — writes to IndexedDB transactionally
   - DB name: pt_tracker_offline, DB version: 2 (same stores: exercises, programs, activity_logs)
   - Export singleton: export const indexCache = new IndexCacheManager()

2. hooks/useIndexData.js — modify reload():
   - On mount: call indexCache.init() (client-side only, inside useEffect)
   - After successful Promise.all fetch: call indexCache.hydrateFromData(exercises, programs, logs) — synchronous write before setExercises
   - In catch block: attempt indexCache.getCachedExercises/Programs/Logs(); if any non-empty, use cached data; expose fromCache: boolean
   - On sign-out (userId becomes null): call indexCache.clearCache()

Validation Checklist:
- Open app online → data loads from API → IndexedDB written immediately
- Open app offline (cache exists) → data loads from IndexedDB → no error
- Open app offline (cache empty, first use) → error shown: offline, no cached data
- Sign out → IndexedDB cache cleared
- Sign back in → fresh API fetch, cache updated
- No SSR errors (typeof window guard)

Dependencies: discovered-from ptrebuild-kj5, child of ptrebuild-nyn

Note: The service worker cache key bug (/ vs /index.html) is tracked separately in dev_notes.json (static app fix). Next.js PWA service worker fix is in ptrebuild-v3l — see constraint added there.

Closure Gate:
- Do not close this implementation bead until the linked parity bead (pt-780.1) is verified or closed.
- This bead covers implementation of offline read-cache behavior; pt-780.1 remains the final acceptance surface for offline startup and cache-fallback parity.
notes:
2026-03-13 QA evidence: confirmed from source. hooks/useIndexData.js has no read-side cache path at all: reload() only does Promise.all network fetches at hooks/useIndexData.js:12-24, then sets error on failure at hooks/useIndexData.js:25-27. When token/patientId are absent it simply clears in-memory state at hooks/useIndexData.js:33-40. lib/index-offline.js currently implements only the queue helpers (queueKey/loadQueue/saveQueue/clearQueue) at lib/index-offline.js:10-44 plus payload shaping; there are no data-cache helpers for exercises/programs/logs. pages/index.js consumes useIndexData directly at pages/index.js:29 and surfaces the resulting load error banner at pages/index.js:223-224. Sign-out clears only the offline queue via clearQueue() in pages/index.js:174-177; there is currently no corresponding cached-read data lifecycle. This bead is accurate as written.
2026-03-21 implementation in progress: wiring tracker bootstrap into the shared offlineCache helper instead of introducing a parallel cache layer. Current changes add IndexedDB-backed cache writes/fallback to hooks/useIndexData.js after successful exercises/programs/logs fetches, add targeted clearPrograms/clearExercises/clearLogs helpers to lib/offline-cache.js for sign-out cleanup, and update README ownership notes to document tracker read-side fallback on the shared cache path.
2026-03-21 landed implementation on nextjs in commit fd8ac79. hooks/useIndexData.js now writes exercises/programs/logs into the shared offlineCache after successful fetches, falls back to cached tracker bootstrap data when network fetches fail, exposes fromCache for downstream offline-state UI, and clears tracker read-side stores when auth drops to avoid cross-user leakage. lib/offline-cache.js now includes targeted clearExercises/clearPrograms/clearLogs helpers, and README ownership notes were updated to reflect tracker use of the shared IndexedDB cache.
2026-03-21 preview check after push: deployed preview loaded successfully, but the fresh isolated Chrome DevTools context did not have a saved authenticated session, so signed-in tracker bootstrap fallback could not be exercised end-to-end in this pass. Remaining runtime verification needed: warm the tracker online while signed in, confirm exercises/programs/logs are written to IndexedDB, then switch offline and verify the tracker hydrates from cache instead of showing the load-error state. Do not close pt-zb3 until that authenticated cache-fallback check is complete and pt-780.1 acceptance is handled.
2026-03-21 follow-up implementation: added tracker shell feedback for cache fallback and aligned failed-load copy with the parity spec. pages/index.js now renders a visible 'Using cached data' banner when useIndexData reports fromCache, hooks/useIndexData.js normalizes the no-cache exercise failure to 'Failed to load exercises. Check your connection.' and log failure to 'Failed to load history.', and pages/index.module.css / README were updated to document the new shell feedback path.
2026-03-21 follow-up implementation committed locally in c9f90d7: tracker read-side fallback now uses a patient-scoped bootstrap snapshot in offline-cache/useIndexData instead of any generic cached exercises/programs/logs. This avoids applying cached tracker data from the wrong resolved patient context during offline fallback. Not yet pushed or preview-verified.
close_reason: Closed

title: Update vexp docs for Codex deferred tool discovery
description:
## Summary
Repo vexp guidance currently treats the Codex-visible 4-tool subset as a local vexp-cli regression that requires patching the installed package. Current live investigation found a narrower case: in Codex desktop, the full mcp__vexp__ namespace can appear only after tool_search loads deferred MCP metadata, even when the installed server already supports the full set.

## Why
Without this note, future agents may patch the local vexp-cli install unnecessarily when the real issue is Codex tool discovery or stale client metadata.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] docs/VEXP_WORKFLOW.md distinguishes deferred Codex tool discovery from a true server-side reduced tool surface
- [ ] Repair guidance clarifies when package patching is actually needed
- [ ] Any related generated vexp instruction text mismatch is documented or queued

## Constraints/Caveats
- Keep the existing package-patch path for true server-side reduced registration cases.
- Do not make doc edits until explicitly requested if the user only asked for investigation output.
close_reason: Reorganized docs/VEXP_WORKFLOW.md so agents can find usage guidance first and repair guidance separately. Added a dedicated 'How To Use vexp' section with a quick tool guide, and moved 4-tool troubleshooting and local package repair guidance into a separate 'How To Fix vexp' section with Codex deferred-discovery notes.

title: Investigate CGO build support for bd doctor tooling
description:
Source Reference: user request on 2026-04-13
Status: in progress
Priority: P2
Risk: low
Scope: current
Agent Owner: codex
Agent Eligible: codex
Tags: beads,bd-doctor,cgo,windows,investigation
File Scope:
- C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\PT_Backup\beads\
- local bd binary/build behavior

Issue:
Investigate whether the local Beads source can be built with CGO on Windows in a way that enables the optional tooling used by `bd doctor`, and document what is currently possible in this workspace.

Context:
The repo docs note a custom embedded-Dolt Windows build and mention CGO-based install commands. We need to inspect the local Beads clone, `bd doctor` expectations, and build tags/dependencies to determine whether a CGO-enabled build would unlock additional doctor checks or fixes.

Constraints/Caveats:
- Investigation only; no bd rebuild unless the user asks
- Prefer source/code inspection over assumptions
- Treat live repo docs and local PT_Backup clone as primary evidence

Validation Checklist:
- [ ] Identify what `bd doctor` checks for in source/docs
- [ ] Identify any doctor features gated by build tags, CGO, or external tools
- [ ] State whether a CGO-enabled Windows build could enable those tools here
- [ ] Note the practical build path and limitations

Dependencies:
- parent: pt-f2m9c

Reactivation Trigger:
- Reopen if upstream Beads changes doctor tooling requirements or Windows build guidance
notes:
Findings 2026-04-13:
- Local Beads source clearly gates several doctor checks behind CGO. In cmd/bd/doctor/checks_nocgo.go, non-CGO builds skip Orphaned Dependencies, Duplicate Issues, Test Pollution, Child-Parent Dependencies, Git Conflicts, Stale Closed Issues, Stale Molecules, Persistent Mol Issues, Stale MQ Files, and Patrol Pollution with "Skipped: requires CGO".
- cmd/bd/store_factory_nocgo.go also blocks embedded Dolt in non-CGO builds and allows only server mode.
- Current live bd binary in PT Tracker shows exactly that behavior: `bd doctor --check=validate --json` reports the validate checks as skipped for lack of CGO, and `bd context --json` shows this repo is currently running in Dolt server mode.
- There is an additional source-level limit: cmd/bd/doctor.go exits early when `isEmbeddedMode()` is true and prints "bd doctor is not yet supported in embedded mode." That means a CGO build alone does not make doctor usable in embedded mode; the doctor command itself currently blocks that path.
- Toolchain check on this machine looks close to ready (`go env` reports CGO_ENABLED=1 with CC=gcc/CXX=g++, and `gcc --version` reports MinGW gcc 15.2.0). However, a direct Windows build attempt from the local clone with `go build -tags gms_pure_go -o %TEMP%\bd-cgo-test.exe ./cmd/bd` exited with code -1073741819 and produced no binary, so the build is not currently proven working end-to-end on this machine.

Conclusion:
- Yes, upstream/source-wise, a CGO-enabled build is the path that unlocks the skipped doctor validation/maintenance/migration checks.
- No, a CGO-enabled build by itself will not enable full `bd doctor` tooling for embedded mode, because cmd/bd/doctor.go explicitly refuses embedded mode today.
- In this workspace, the practical route for those doctor tools is a successful CGO build plus server mode. Enabling doctor inside embedded mode would require a source change, not just a different build flag.
close_reason: Investigation completed: CGO gates several doctor checks, but embedded mode is still blocked by doctor.go; local Windows CGO build attempt was not yet successful.

title: Shared repo and file maintenance
description:
Shared epic for ongoing repo and file maintenance work that improves maintainability without being a user-facing feature or a concrete defect fix.

## Purpose
Provide one stable parent for maintenance work such as:
- SRP/SOC refactors
- file-boundary and ownership cleanup
- shared hook/component/lib maintenance
- script and tooling maintenance
- docs and structure upkeep tied to maintainability rather than product behavior

## Belongs Here
- structure and ownership refactors
- shared file cleanup
- scripts/tooling upkeep
- maintainability-focused docs updates
- non-bug technical debt reduction

## Does Not Belong Here
- user-facing new features
- bug/regression fixes with a concrete failing behavior
- pure coordination notes

notes:
Final read-only review completed on the Codex-owned file set only: README.md, docs/IMPLEMENTATION_PATTERNS.md, app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js, app/(protected)/TrackerRouteShell.js, components/ExercisePicker.js, hooks/useProgramOfflineQueue.js, hooks/useSessionLogging.js, hooks/useProgramMutationQueueActions.js, hooks/useProgramQueueSync.js, hooks/useSeedSetLogging.js, hooks/useSessionLogSubmission.js, hooks/useExercisePickerActiveDrag.js, hooks/useExercisePickerDragDomEffects.js, hooks/useExercisePickerManualReorder.js, hooks/useExercisePickerModel.js, lib/session-log-draft.js, and lib/session-logging.js. No blocking findings recorded.

Findings: none.

Why this is ready to commit:
- Layer boundaries are now aligned with repo rules: shared session-log helpers live in lib/session-logging.js, hook files consume them instead of exporting cross-hook utilities, and the session logger split keeps draft shaping in lib/session-log-draft.js while transport remains in dedicated hooks.
- ExercisePicker is render-only, with query/display shaping in useExercisePickerModel.js and drag workflow split across focused hooks (manual reorder, active drag, DOM effects).
- /program queue responsibilities are separated cleanly: useProgramOfflineQueue.js owns queue state and hydration, useProgramQueueSync.js owns replay/reconnect policy, and useProgramMutationQueueActions.js owns enqueue-time optimistic save and rollback.
- TrackerPage.js remains an orchestrator and TrackerRouteShell.js remains render-only after the picker-model handoff.
- README.md and docs/IMPLEMENTATION_PATTERNS.md accurately describe the new ownership boundaries for the reviewed file set.

Open questions / assumptions:
- This review was limited to the listed Codex-owned files and did not widen into Claude-owned timer work or unrelated docs.
- This was a structure-and-ownership review only. No builds, tests, browser checks, staging changes, commits, or pushes were performed because this delegated bead is read-only.

Conclusion: the reviewed Codex commit set is ready to commit from an SRP/SOC, layer-rule, and docs-accuracy perspective.
Diff addendum: compared the current Codex file set against the original HEAD versions for the same files to look for hidden behavioral changes. No behavioral regressions or code-semantics changes were found in the diff review.

What I checked:
- TrackerPage/TrackerRouteShell now pass a picker model object instead of raw picker props, but the same selection, sort, lifecycle-filter, and dosage-edit wiring remains intact.
- ExercisePicker extraction preserves the same visible-card fields, drag-hold timing, pointer event usage, preview reorder behavior, and drag ghost rendering; the logic was split across focused hooks rather than materially changed.
- useProgramOfflineQueue preserves the same queue hydration, immediate-save decision, offline toast/error behavior, replay trigger conditions, and post-sync refresh path; the logic moved into useProgramQueueSync and useProgramMutationQueueActions without changing the control flow.
- useSessionLogging preserves create, seeded-create, edit, add/remove/update/form-param, submit, and quick seed-set flows; those paths were extracted into lib/session-log-draft, useSessionLogSubmission, and useSeedSetLogging without changing the user-visible save behavior.
- lib/session-logging now owns helpers that were previously duplicated or embedded in hooks; the resulting create and queued payload shapes remain equivalent to the original logic.

Residual caution:
- This was a code-diff review, not a runtime verification pass. I did not run browser tests, network simulations, or commit hooks in this step.
API-compatibility addendum: traced the public API shape for the extracted files through their current call sites. No consumer mismatches found.

Checked consumer compatibility:
- TrackerOverlays still receives the same logger surface from useSessionLogging (isOpen, isEdit, exercise, performedAt, notes, sets, submitting, error, close, setPerformedAt, setNotes, addSet, removeSet, updateSet, updateFormParam, submit semantics).
- ProgramPage still receives the same useProgramOfflineQueue surface (mutationQueue, queueSummary, queueError, queueLoaded, queueSyncing, enqueueMutation, persistQueue, syncProgramMutations), and the downstream mutation hooks still call those members in the same way.
- TrackerPage and TrackerRouteShell were updated consistently for the ExercisePicker API change; the picker model handoff is end-to-end, with no stale callers still passing the old raw props.

Conclusion remains unchanged: no code or behavioral issues were found in this diff-and-call-site review, with the caveat that this is still not a runtime/browser verification pass.
Post-review outcome: the reviewed Codex commit set was committed as 01afd06 on nextjs. The remaining docs/README.md index wording that belonged to the same docs set shipped in follow-up commit dd66747 under bead pt-bykxt.
close_reason: Final read-only review passed; Codex commit set ready

title: Code review: hooks/useTimerExecutionState.js
description: Review finding for Claude SRP split: useTimerExecutionState has a side-preservation behavior change from the original useTimerSpeech.
notes:
## Resolution

Both Codex and Claude (new conversation) flagged the isOpen behavior change. Root cause confirmed and fixed.

### MEDIUM: Side-preservation fix applied

Removed  from  to restore original preservation semantics: side is preserved when the exerciseId matches AND resetToken has not changed, regardless of panel open/close state.

 is now used in a semantically meaningful way in the body: we only advance  in state while the panel is open, so a close-then-reopen cycle does not look like a new explicit reset.



This satisfies Biome (isOpen is read in the effect body) without biome-ignore, and preserves the original side-continuity semantics.

### LOW: _resetToken spread into getCanApply fixed

 now passes only the fields getCanApply reads (mode, counterValue, elapsedMs, completedReps) instead of spreading executionState. _resetToken and exerciseId no longer leak across the hook boundary.

### Caller safety verification (TrackerOverlays)

Full call chain traced:
- TrackerOverlays: selectedSide={currentSide} onSideChange={setCurrentSide}
- useTimerSpeech → useTimerExecutionState receives selectedSide prop and onSelectedSideChange
- setSelectedSide calls onSelectedSideChange?.(side) → setCurrentSide(side) in session state
- currentSide is always the source of truth; the selectedSide prop always reflects current session state
- The nextSide computation gives the prop priority: selectedSide ?? preservedSide ?? 'right'

The fix matches original behavior and is confirmed safe for the current single caller.
close_reason: Side-preservation behavior change fixed: removed isOpen from sameExerciseAndNoReset, uses isOpen for _resetToken update logic instead. _resetToken spread into getCanApply fixed by passing explicit fields. Both issues resolved.

notes:
Resolved 2026-04-13 by moving shared session-log helpers out of hooks/ and into lib/session-logging.js. The following helpers now live in the lib owner and are imported by both hooks: isBrowserOffline, isSessionLogNetworkFailure, createSessionLog, and buildQueuedSessionLogPayload. hooks/useSessionLogSubmission.js and hooks/useSeedSetLogging.js are now hook-only consumers rather than cross-hook utility providers. README.md and docs/IMPLEMENTATION_PATTERNS.md were updated to document the corrected ownership boundary.
Final outcome: the fix shipped in commit 01afd06 on branch nextjs. Shared session-log helper ownership is now split between work bead pt-3tu9g and this review bead, with docs coverage completed by the same commit.

title: Structure review: hooks/useMessages.js
description:
Review the structure and ownership boundaries of the target file and determine whether any responsibilities should be extracted or divided more cleanly.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Review the named file for structure, ownership, and cohesion only
- Identify responsibilities currently owned by the file
- Recommend extractions or confirm the file is structurally sound as-is
- Record findings back on this bead

## Design
Target file:
- C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\pttracker\hooks\useMessages.js

Scope limit:
- This bead is the only task context for the reviewing subagent.
- Read only this bead for task context.
- Then read only the required repo docs named in the delegated prompt.
- Do not run bd prime.
- Do not search for other beads.
- Do not create another bead.
- Do not inspect unrelated sibling files or neighboring files unless the target file directly imports or depends on them and they must be checked to judge ownership.
- Do not widen scope beyond the target file, except to inspect directly related imports/exports needed to judge ownership.
- Write all findings and recommendations back to this bead.

Question to answer:
1. What responsibilities does the target file currently own?
2. Do any of those responsibilities belong in a different layer or separate file?
3. If so, what are the clearest extraction or division points?
4. If not, is the file structurally sound as-is?

Required output:
- responsibilities identified
- recommended extractions or divisions, if any
- what should remain in the original file
- keep-together notes, if any
- open questions, if any
- confidence for each recommendation: high / medium / low

Parent-agent notes:
- Representative from the 20% bucket; assess whether messaging concerns remain cohesive or should separate.
- Pay particular attention to SRP/SOC boundaries for an AI-maintained repo.
- Files should be easy for future agents to locate, read, and edit without a change to one responsibility risking another unrelated responsibility.
notes:
Final judgment update after follow-up review:

This file does not fully adhere to SRP/SOC.

Why this differs from the earlier note
- The earlier note treated all behavior in hooks/useMessages.js as one broad "messaging concern."
- That standard was too broad for this repo's responsibility-first rules.
- After a stricter review centered on independently changeable concerns and collateral-risk boundaries, the file should be judged as mixing at least two distinct concerns inside one hook.

responsibilities identified
- Message synchronization lifecycle: delayed mount fetch, refresh callback, Supabase Realtime subscription setup, and cleanup.
- Unread/read-state policy: unread badge derivation, optimistic unread clearing, and modal-open mark-as-read behavior.
- Message mutation wrappers: send/archive/unarchive/markRead/remove orchestration over lib/messages.js helpers.

final SRP/SOC judgment
- hooks/useMessages.js is a valid hook-layer file, but it does not fully meet SRP.
- It has more than one reason to change.
- A change to refresh timing, subscription mechanics, or channel behavior is one change axis.
- A change to unread badge semantics or modal-open read behavior is another change axis.
- Those concerns are related, but they are independently changeable and can create collateral risk for each other because they share one owner.
- SOC is partial, not complete: the file stays in the hook layer and delegates API/data helpers to lib/messages.js, but inside the hook it still combines transport/sync concerns with unread-policy concerns.

recommended extractions or divisions
- Current responsibility: message synchronization lifecycle.
  Likely destination file type: separate hook file.
  Recommendation: keep refresh/subscription ownership in hooks/useMessages.js or move it to a dedicated hooks/useMessageSync.js owner.
  Confidence: medium.
- Current responsibility: unread/read-state policy.
  Likely destination file type: separate hook file or pure lib helper if kept stateless.
  Recommendation: extract unread badge and modal-open read-state policy into a dedicated hook such as hooks/useMessageUnreadState.js, or move the pure unread derivation further into lib/messages.js while keeping stateful modal-open behavior in a focused hook.
  Confidence: medium.
- Current responsibility: message mutation wrappers.
  Likely destination file type: remain in the orchestration hook unless mutation lifecycle becomes materially more complex.
  Recommendation: keep these thin wrappers with the orchestration owner for now; they are not the main SRP break on their own.
  Confidence: high.

what should remain in the original file
- If split, the original file should remain the route-facing orchestration surface that composes the extracted messaging sub-concerns and returns the public hook API expected by callers.
- If not split immediately, it should at minimum be treated as a mixed-concern hook rather than a clean SRP/SOC pass.

keep-together notes, if any
- The file should not be split into many tiny pieces that are always loaded together.
- The important boundary is not "one file per function"; it is separating sync lifecycle from unread/read-state policy because those are the clearest independently changeable concerns.

open questions, if any
- None required for the final structural judgment.

confidence
- Final judgment that the file does not fully meet SRP/SOC: high.
close_reason: No extraction justified. refresh updates messages+unreadCount atomically — splitting would cause a badge flash. markModalOpened reads from messages state already owned here. File delegates all pure logic to lib/messages.js. Coupling between sync and unread state is intentional, not a SRP violation.

notes:
## Structure review findings for `hooks/useProgramPageData.js`

### Responsibilities identified
- Route-level `/program` data state shell and public setter surface for the page host (`setProgramDataSnapshot`) at lines 23-34 and 144-147.
- Cached bootstrap restore lifecycle for authenticated and pre-hydration fallback entry paths at lines 36-44 and 113-128.
- Live `/program` bootstrap refresh orchestration across users, exercises, vocabularies, reference data, and programs at lines 46-88.
- Route-level fallback and error-state handling for offline cached recovery vs hard failure at lines 89-107.
- Session-loss cleanup of role and access UI state at lines 134-142.

### Recommended extractions or divisions
- Extract current responsibility: therapist/admin access gating and access-error state shaping now embedded in the hook at lines 55-64.
  Likely destination file type: pure `lib/` helper, preferably in `lib/program-page-data.js` next to the existing cached-bootstrap shaping helpers.
  Why: the same role gate and identical `Therapist or admin access required.` state shape already exist in `lib/program-page-data.js` at lines 32-37. That makes access-state construction an independently changeable pure concern rather than hook-only orchestration.
  Confidence: high.

### What should remain in the original file
- The hook should keep the route bootstrap lifecycle itself: restoring cached bootstrap, triggering live refresh when session exists, handling pre-hydration cached restore from `initialAuthUserId` or `auth_state`, and exposing the state shell to the page.
- `loadData` should remain the orchestration point for fetch timing, cache writes, and route-state updates. Those pieces are all part of one `/program` page-data lifecycle concern and would be artificially fragmented if split further right now.
- `setProgramDataSnapshot` can stay here because it is the hook-owned state updater surface consumed by the page workflow.

### Keep-together notes
- The file header comment matches the actual domain: `/program` page bootstrap, cache restore, and live refresh.
- The cache-restore path, live refresh path, offline fallback path, and session-aware reset path are tightly coupled pieces of one route bootstrap concern. Future edits to cache timing or fallback semantics would reasonably need all of them together.
- Pure cached-snapshot shaping already sits in `lib/program-page-data.js`, and patient-context resolution already sits in `lib/users.js`. That existing split supports leaving the hook otherwise intact.
- I do not see a justified split into another hook. A second hook for the fallback restore or session cleanup would likely always be loaded together with this one and would reduce maintainability rather than improve it.

### Open questions
- Should the access-gate helper live in `lib/program-page-data.js` because it already owns `/program` bootstrap shaping, or does the team want a narrower dedicated `lib/program-access.js` if role-gate logic is expected to grow? This does not block the review result; it only affects exact destination naming.

### Overall judgment
- The file is structurally sound as a hook and mostly cohesive as-is.
- One meaningful extraction is justified: move duplicated access-gate state shaping into a pure `lib/` helper.
close_reason: buildAccessErrorState extracted to lib/program-page-data.js. useProgramPageData now imports and calls it instead of inline object. Committed 45d92d3.

notes:

Structure review findings for `hooks/useManualLog.js`

Layer assessment
- `hooks/useManualLog.js` is in the correct layer. It is a hook with state/effect-style orchestration only, no JSX, and it delegates pure set-shaping to `lib/manual-log-state.js`.

Responsibilities identified
- Owns manual-log modal state (`manualLogState`) for an in-progress tracker session.
- Opens the manual-log flow by seeding state from the selected exercise, draft session date, optional side, and optional seed set.
- Delegates add/remove/update/form-param mutations to pure helpers in `lib/manual-log-state.js` while keeping the React state transitions local to the hook.
- Validates and normalizes manual-log sets at submit time.
- Hands accepted manual-log sets back into the active tracker session by updating `draftSession`, reopening timer flow, announcing session progress, and bumping the panel reset token.
- Closes the modal and restores timer visibility when appropriate.

Recommended extractions or divisions
- No meaningful extraction is justified from this file as it exists today.
- Confidence: high.
- Reason: the repo docs and README explicitly define this hook as the owner of manual-log workflow orchestration, draft-session coordination, and submit-time handoff back into the active tracker flow. The pure data-shaping boundary is already extracted into the correct destination file type: `lib/manual-log-state.js`.

What should remain in the original file
- Modal open/close handlers.
- React state ownership for `manualLogState`.
- Wiring to tracker-session setters and callbacks (`setDraftSession`, `setIsTimerOpen`, `setPanelResetToken`, `announceSessionProgress`).
- Submit-time orchestration that bridges manual-log state back into the live tracker session.

Keep-together notes
- The open/add/remove/update/submit/close handlers are one workflow and are always loaded together by the caller. Splitting them further would create artificial fragmentation rather than a clearer ownership boundary.
- The current file shape supports future-agent maintainability well: a future agent changing manual-log orchestration can stay in this hook, while a future agent changing deterministic set shaping can stay in `lib/manual-log-state.js`.

Open questions

The pure data-shaping boundary is already extracted into the correct destination file type: `lib/manual-log-state.js`.

- Wiring to tracker-session setters and callbacks (`setDraftSession`, `setIsTimerOpen`, `setPanelResetToken`, `announceSessionProgress`).

- None from this file alone. The ownership boundary is clear in both the file header and repo docs.
Correction to earlier structure review for `hooks/useManualLog.js`

Final finding
- `hooks/useManualLog.js` does not fully meet the strictest SRP/SOC standard for a heavily AI-agent-maintained repo.
- It should be divided more cleanly.

Explanation
- The file currently owns two independently changeable responsibilities inside the same broad workflow.
- Responsibility 1: manual-log modal state management.
- This includes opening, closing, local state, add/remove/update of sets, and form-param updates.
- Responsibility 2: tracker-session handoff orchestration.
- This includes submit validation, normalization, merging into `draftSession`, reopening the timer, announcing session progress, and bumping the panel reset token.
- These concerns are related, but they are not the same responsibility. A future agent could reasonably need to change submit/handoff behavior without touching modal state transitions, or change modal behavior without touching tracker re-entry behavior.
- For an AI-maintained repo, that is a meaningful split boundary.

- Manual-log open/close lifecycle.
- Manual-log set mutation wiring via `lib/manual-log-state.js`.

- Current responsibility to extract: submit-side tracker handoff orchestration.

1. What responsibilities does the target file currently own?
2. Do any of those responsibilities belong in a different layer or separate file?
3. If so, what are the clearest extraction or division points?
4. If not, is the file structurally sound as-is?

Required output:
- responsibilities identified
- recommended extractions or divisions, if any
- what should remain in the original file
- keep-together notes, if any
- open questions, if any
- confidence for each recommendation: high / medium / low

Parent-agent notes:
- Over cap; review for queue lifecycle vs mutation/workspace concerns.
- Pay particular attention to SRP/SOC boundaries for an AI-maintained repo.
- Files should be easy for future agents to locate, read, and edit without a change to one responsibility risking another unrelated responsibility.
notes:
Delegated read-only structure review pending subagent findings.
-
Structure review findings for `hooks/useProgramOfflineQueue.js`

responsibilities identified
- Queue state ownership for `/program` offline mutations: in-memory state, ref mirror, persisted queue writes, and initial IndexedDB hydration (`persistQueue`, load effect, summary/error state).
- Replay and reconnect lifecycle: guarded sync execution, online listener wiring, and auto-replay trigger when a loaded queue is present (`syncProgramMutations`, effects at lines 100-110).
- Immediate mutation path with optimistic snapshot coordination: merge new mutation, decide whether to replay immediately, run the direct mutation attempt, refresh editor data on success, and roll back the optimistic snapshot on non-network failure (`enqueueMutation`, lines 112-160).

recommended extractions or divisions
- Extract the current responsibility `replay and reconnect lifecycle` into a focused hook file such as `hooks/useProgramQueueSync.js`. Confidence: high. Reason: this logic has its own triggers, locking rules, online subscription, and post-replay refresh/toast behavior. A future change to reconnect policy or replay timing should not require editing the optimistic enqueue path.
- Extract the current responsibility `immediate mutation attempt with optimistic snapshot rollback` into a focused hook/helper file such as `hooks/useProgramMutationQueueActions.js`. Confidence: medium. Reason: `enqueueMutation` is not just queue lifecycle; it also owns mutation execution policy, optimistic snapshot commit/rollback, backlog branching, and user feedback. Those are independently changeable from hydration and replay wiring. The boundary is clear, but the exact destination could be either a focused hook file or a small hook-local helper depending on how the parent hook wants to orchestrate the returned API.

what should remain in the original file
- The top-level `/program` offline queue orchestrator for this route concern: queue state fields exposed to callers, queue summary derivation, and the composition point that wires persistence, sync lifecycle, and enqueue actions together.
- The public hook API shape can remain here even if lower-level sync/action responsibilities move out.

keep-together notes
- Queue hydration/persistence and the exposed queue state belong together. They are the core ownership implied by the file header comment and by the README entry for `useProgramOfflineQueue`.
- The file is a hook and stays in the correct layer. This is not a layer violation; it is a cohesion/independently-changeable-concerns issue.
- The imported `lib/program-offline.js` already owns the pure queue and replay primitives. That supports keeping this file as orchestration only rather than also owning every replay trigger and optimistic execution branch inline.

open questions
- The hook currently gates replay on `programPatientId` but calls `loadData(session.access_token, session.user.id)` after sync and direct success. I did not inspect `loadData` beyond this file, so I cannot tell whether that mismatch is intentional route orchestration or a sign that refresh ownership should move elsewhere.
- If the intended contract is `one hook returns both queue state and all mutation actions,` keeping a thin orchestrator wrapper here still fits that contract after extraction.

overall assessment
- Meaningful extraction is justified. The file is over the hook cap and, more importantly, mixes queue-state ownership with two separately editable workflows: reconnect replay policy and optimistic mutation execution/rollback. The file is not structurally unsound, but it is carrying more than one independently changeable concern for an AI-maintained repo.
Follow-up confirmation on SRP/SOC and AI-agent maintainability

answer
- The current file does not fully meet the SRP/SOC and AI-maintainability standard yet, so I am not confirming it as-is.

why not
- `hooks/useProgramOfflineQueue.js` currently mixes three concerns that can change independently:
  1. queue state ownership and persistence
  2. replay/reconnect policy
  3. immediate optimistic mutation execution with rollback behavior
- Those concerns are related, but they are not the same responsibility for future maintenance. A change to reconnect timing, online-listener behavior, or replay locking should not require editing the optimistic direct-save path. A change to optimistic rollback or success/failure toast behavior should not require editing hydration and replay triggers.
- In an AI-maintained repo, this increases cross-risk: an agent changing one workflow has to load and reason about the others in the same file, which makes collateral edits more likely and makes the correct edit location less obvious.

reason for this clarification
- Your follow-up asked for an explicit check against the higher standard: minimal risk that one change affects another responsibility, and quick/easy agent navigation to the exact area that owns a concern. Under that stricter framing, my earlier note that the file was `not structurally unsound` needed to be sharpened. The file is still in the correct layer, but it is not yet at the stronger ownership boundary standard you asked me to validate.

updated proposal
- Keep `hooks/useProgramOfflineQueue.js` as the top-level queue orchestrator and public hook API owner for `/program` offline queue state.
- Move the replay/reconnect lifecycle responsibility into a focused hook file such as `hooks/useProgramQueueSync.js`.
  Reason: replay triggers, online subscription, locking, and post-replay refresh/toast behavior form one independently changeable concern.
- Move the immediate optimistic mutation execution and rollback responsibility into a focused hook/helper file such as `hooks/useProgramMutationQueueActions.js`.
  Reason: enqueue-time branching, direct mutation attempts, optimistic snapshot coordination, rollback, and backlog handoff form a separate workflow concern.

what this improves
- Changes to queue hydration/persistence stay local to the queue owner.
- Changes to reconnect/replay behavior stay local to the sync owner.
- Changes to optimistic mutation/rollback behavior stay local to the action owner.
- Future agents can find the likely edit target faster because the file names map more directly to the behavior being changed.
- The risk that a modification to one workflow accidentally changes another is reduced because the reasoning surface becomes narrower.

SRP/SOC conclusion
- Current file: partially aligned on layer, not fully aligned on single-responsibility ownership.
- Proposed split: materially better aligned with SRP/SOC, lower collateral-risk for unrelated changes, and better suited to fast, isolated edits by future agents.

confidence
- High that the replay/reconnect lifecycle should not stay mixed with the rest.
- Medium-high that the optimistic enqueue/direct-save/rollback path should also be separated from the queue-state owner.
Implemented the structure split for hooks/useProgramOfflineQueue.js. Queue state/hydration remains in useProgramOfflineQueue, replay/reconnect lifecycle moved to hooks/useProgramQueueSync.js, and enqueue-time optimistic save/rollback moved to hooks/useProgramMutationQueueActions.js. README and implementation patterns updated to point future edits to the right owner.
close_reason: Structure review completed and the recommended split was implemented in the same pass.

- open questions, if any
- confidence for each recommendation: high / medium / low

Parent-agent notes:
- Over cap; likely mixed UI responsibilities and interaction workflows.
- Pay particular attention to SRP/SOC boundaries for an AI-maintained repo.
- Files should be easy for future agents to locate, read, and edit without a change to one responsibility risking another unrelated responsibility.
notes:
-
responsibilities identified
- Layer: `components/`. The file is a shared UI surface for choosing tracker exercises.
- It currently owns four responsibilities: (1) search/sort/lifecycle-filter controls and empty-state rendering, (2) exercise card row rendering including selection, dosage affordance, PRN/adherence/category badges, and drag handle UI, (3) manual reorder interaction state/effects including pending-hold activation, pointer capture, drag preview ordering, body/document touch-action toggling, and drop commit, and (4) light picker-specific display shaping such as adherence badge text/tone and per-card dosage action label selection.

recommended extractions or divisions
- Extract the current manual reorder interaction workflow from `components/ExercisePicker.js` into a focused hook file such as `hooks/useExercisePickerManualReorder.js`. Confidence: high.
Why: the drag/reorder logic is a distinct state/effects concern with its own refs, timers, pointer listeners, capture/release, preview-order lifecycle, and commit path. A future change to drag thresholds, capture behavior, preview movement, or reorder commit rules should not require editing the picker's main JSX shell.
- Extract the current exercise row/card rendering from `components/ExercisePicker.js` into a dedicated UI component file such as `components/ExercisePickerCard.js`. Confidence: medium.
Why: the card is an independently changeable visual workspace: selection button, dosage action button, drag handle affordance, PRN badge, adherence badge, and category tag. Future UI-only edits to card layout or card actions would be safer in an isolated component instead of in the same file as drag lifecycle logic.

what should remain in the original file
- `ExercisePicker` should remain the owner of picker-level orchestration: query state, derived visible list, wiring of sort/lifecycle controls, empty state, list mapping, and composition of the drag overlay/ghost with the chosen hook/component outputs.
- The file header comment still fits that orchestrator role if the manual reorder state/effects are pulled out and the row UI is split cleanly.

keep-together notes
- The search input plus the two `NativeSelect` controls can stay together in the original file unless they grow into a larger standalone toolbar. They are thin picker-specific UI, not a separate workflow yet.
- The small adherence formatter can stay local unless another surface needs the same badge contract. It is pure shaping, but it is still tightly tied to picker badge copy right now.
- The drag ghost markup can stay with the picker host even if the reorder state moves to a hook. Repo rules favor hooks for state/effects only, so the ghost UI should stay component-side unless it becomes its own presentational component.

open questions
- The docs strongly support moving the manual reorder state/effects to a hook, but they leave some ambiguity about whether the hook should return only state/handlers or also pre-shaped drag-ghost data. I would keep the hook stateful only and leave ghost rendering in the component.
- `ExercisePickerCard` is a clear UI boundary, but it is always loaded with the picker. I still lean toward extraction because the card is independently editable, but this boundary is less mandatory than the drag hook split.
SRP/SOC confirmation pass
- Current state: `components/ExercisePicker.js` is not yet fully aligned with SRP/SOC for an AI-agent-maintained codebase.
- Reason: one file still owns both picker UI orchestration and the full manual reorder interaction lifecycle. Those are independently changeable concerns. A future agent changing drag thresholds, pointer-capture behavior, reorder preview rules, or drag commit behavior would have to edit the same file that also owns search/filter controls, list rendering, and selection/dosage wiring. That creates avoidable collateral-risk during maintenance.
- Agent-maintainability impact: the current file shape slows targeted edits because the file mixes UI composition with non-trivial pointer/timer/effect state. An agent looking for drag behavior must read through picker rendering, and an agent looking for picker UI changes must reason around drag lifecycle code. That is a meaningful cross-responsibility coupling risk.

updated conclusion
- I do not confirm the file as fully aligned in its current shape.
- I do confirm that the following boundary would bring it into stronger SRP/SOC alignment with lower maintenance risk for future agents:
  1. Move the manual reorder interaction state/effects from `components/ExercisePicker.js` into a dedicated hook file such as `hooks/useExercisePickerManualReorder.js`.
  2. Keep `components/ExercisePicker.js` as the picker orchestrator and UI host: query/filter/sort controls, visible-list derivation, list mapping, and drag-ghost rendering fed by the hook.
  3. Optional but still beneficial: move the card row UI into `components/ExercisePickerCard.js` so card-layout changes stay isolated from picker-host wiring.

reason for the changed proposal wording
- My earlier note already recommended the hook extraction and card extraction, but this follow-up question asks for an explicit confirmation against SRP/SOC and low-collateral-risk maintenance. Under that stricter standard, the drag/reorder hook extraction is not just a good cleanup; it is the key fix needed before I can say the file minimizes cross-responsibility impact.
- The card extraction remains a secondary improvement rather than the primary blocker. The main structural issue is the mixed UI-orchestrator plus state/effects workflow ownership in one component file.

new proposal summary
- Required structural fix for strong SRP/SOC alignment: `components/ExercisePicker.js` -> picker UI/orchestration only; `hooks/useExercisePickerManualReorder.js` -> manual reorder state/effects only.
- Secondary maintainability improvement: `components/ExercisePickerCard.js` -> row/card presentation only.

what this means for future agents
- With the hook split, an agent can find drag behavior in one place and picker UI in another, reducing the chance that a change to pointer/timer/reorder behavior affects search/filter/list rendering.
- With the optional card split, an agent can change row visuals or per-card actions without touching picker orchestration or drag lifecycle code.
Implemented the required structural fix for components/ExercisePicker.js by moving manual reorder state/effects into hooks/useExercisePickerManualReorder.js. ExercisePicker now stays the UI host for search/filter/sort, list mapping, and drag-ghost rendering. README and implementation patterns updated to document the split.
Follow-up SRP/SOC audit tightened the reorder split further: manual hold activation now lives in useExercisePickerManualReorder, active drag motion/preview ordering in useExercisePickerActiveDrag, and DOM listener/touch-lock cleanup in useExercisePickerDragDomEffects. This brought each hook under the hook cap and kept each owner focused on one drag concern.
close_reason: Structure review completed and the recommended split was implemented in the same pass.

title: Structure review: hooks/useSessionLogging.js
description:
Review the structure and ownership boundaries of the target file and determine whether any responsibilities should be extracted or divided more cleanly.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Review the named file for structure, ownership, and cohesion only
- Identify responsibilities currently owned by the file
- Recommend extractions or confirm the file is structurally sound as-is
- Record findings back on this bead

## Design
Target file:
- C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\pttracker\hooks\useSessionLogging.js

Scope limit:
- This bead is the only task context for the reviewing subagent.
- Read only this bead for task context.
- Then read only the required repo docs named in the delegated prompt.
- Do not run bd prime.
- Do not search for other beads.
- Do not create another bead.
- Do not inspect unrelated sibling files or neighboring files unless the target file directly imports or depends on them and they must be checked to judge ownership.
- Do not widen scope beyond the target file, except to inspect directly related imports/exports needed to judge ownership.
- Write all findings and recommendations back to this bead.

Question to answer:
1. What responsibilities does the target file currently own?
2. Do any of those responsibilities belong in a different layer or separate file?
3. If so, what are the clearest extraction or division points?
4. If not, is the file structurally sound as-is?

Required output:
- responsibilities identified
- recommended extractions or divisions, if any
- what should remain in the original file
- keep-together notes, if any
- open questions, if any
- confidence for each recommendation: high / medium / low

Parent-agent notes:
- Over cap; review for SRP/SOC boundaries and future-agent maintainability.
- Pay particular attention to SRP/SOC boundaries for an AI-maintained repo.
- Files should be easy for future agents to locate, read, and edit without a change to one responsibility risking another unrelated responsibility.
notes:
Delegated read-only structure review pending subagent findings.
-
Responsibilities identified
- Route-facing modal draft state: `isOpen`, current exercise/log, performed time, notes, sets, submitting, error, and `isEdit` (`hooks/useSessionLogging.js:6-15`).
- Draft initialization for create, create-with-seed, and edit (`hooks/useSessionLogging.js:17-46`).
- Draft set mutation rules: add/remove/renumber, set patching, and `form_data` mutation (`hooks/useSessionLogging.js:57-90`).
- Full submit transport: PATCH vs POST, offline enqueue, duplicate handling, and error mapping (`hooks/useSessionLogging.js:92-173`).
- Separate quick seed-set create path that bypasses most modal draft state but repeats create/offline transport behavior (`hooks/useSessionLogging.js:175-214`).

Recommended extractions or divisions
- Extract the current responsibility `draft set mutation rules` into a `lib` pure-helper file, likely `lib/session-log-draft.js` or similar. Confidence: high.
- Extract the current responsibility `write transport and offline enqueue flow` into a focused `hook` file, likely `hooks/useSessionLogSubmission.js` or similar, leaving `useSessionLogging` as the orchestrator. Confidence: medium.
- Extract the current responsibility `seed-set quick create path` into its own focused `hook` file, likely `hooks/useSeedSetLogging.js` or another narrowly named quick-log hook. Confidence: high.

What should remain in the original file
- Keep the original file as the route-facing session-log draft orchestrator: open/close lifecycle, draft state exposure, create/edit entry setup, and delegation to extracted mutation/submission owners.

Keep-together notes
- `openCreate`, `openCreateWithSeedSet`, `openEdit`, `close`, and the exposed draft state belong together; they are one UI-facing concern.
- `lib/session-logging.js` already appears to be the correct owner for pure payload/default/normalization helpers and does not need further split from this review alone.

Open questions
- I did not inspect callers, so the exact destination for `submitSeedSet` is slightly ambiguous: if it is tracker-only, the best home could be a route-local hook instead of a broadly shared hook. The need to separate it from the modal draft state is still high-confidence.
- The transport split is structurally justified, but the final seam depends on whether modal submit and quick seed submit should share one submission owner or two narrower owners.
SRP/SOC confirmation pass
- I do not consider the current `hooks/useSessionLogging.js` fully aligned with SRP/SOC for an AI-agent-maintained codebase.
- Reason for this change in assessment detail: your follow-up asked for an explicit confirmation standard about minimal cross-responsibility risk and fast future-agent editability, so I re-evaluated the file against that stricter bar.

Why it is not fully aligned
- A change to draft set-editing rules currently requires loading the same file that owns modal lifecycle and network/offline submission behavior.
- A change to offline enqueue or API error handling currently sits beside create/edit modal state and seed-set quick logging.
- A change to quick seed-set logging currently risks collateral edits to the larger modal draft workflow because both write paths live in one hook.
- This means one responsibility can be changed in a way that unintentionally affects another, and a future agent cannot isolate the relevant area as quickly as the repo rules intend.

Fixes needed
- Move the current responsibility `draft set mutation rules` into a `lib` pure-helper file, likely `lib/session-log-draft.js` or similar.
  Reason: this is deterministic data shaping, not React state ownership. Future agents should be able to adjust set numbering or `form_data` behavior without reasoning through submit and modal lifecycle code.
- Move the current responsibility `write transport and offline enqueue flow` into a focused `hook` file, likely `hooks/useSessionLogSubmission.js` or similar.
  Reason: network branching, enqueue behavior, duplicate handling, and error mapping are one side-effect concern. Keeping them separate lowers the risk that transport changes alter draft-state behavior.
- Move the current responsibility `seed-set quick create path` into its own focused `hook` file, likely `hooks/useSeedSetLogging.js` or another narrowly named quick-log hook.
  Reason: this path is a separate workflow with its own inputs and lifecycle. Future agents should be able to change quick logging without touching the modal draft state machine.

New proposal
- Keep `hooks/useSessionLogging.js` as the orchestrator for the session-log draft UI state only: open/close lifecycle, current draft state, and create/edit initialization.
- Let pure set-draft rules live in a dedicated `lib` owner.
- Let write/offline submission concerns live in one focused submission hook, or two narrower submission hooks if caller shape requires it.
- Let quick seed-set logging live in its own narrow hook instead of sharing a file with modal draft orchestration.

Why this proposal is better for AI-agent maintenance
- A future agent changing only set-draft semantics can go straight to one pure helper file.
- A future agent changing only offline/API behavior can go straight to one submission-focused hook.
- A future agent changing only quick logging can go straight to one narrow quick-log owner.
- That separation reduces collateral risk, makes file names match responsibilities more accurately, and improves fast file discovery for stateless future agents.
Implemented the structure split for hooks/useSessionLogging.js. The hook now stays draft-orchestration only; pure draft shaping moved to lib/session-log-draft.js, modal submit transport moved to hooks/useSessionLogSubmission.js, and quick one-set logging moved to hooks/useSeedSetLogging.js. README and implementation patterns updated to map the new owners.
close_reason: Structure review completed and the recommended split was implemented in the same pass.

title: Active application bugs and regressions

## Summary
Track active bug investigation and repair work across the PT Tracker app.

## Why
The repo has feature and maintenance umbrellas, but active user-facing bugs and regressions need a general home that is not route-specific and not parked under future work.

## Scope
- Active bug reports and regressions on any app route
- Investigation beads that identify root cause, evidence, and fixes
- Verified behavior mismatches that need repair or follow-up

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] The epic stays focused on real defects and regressions rather than feature ideas

notes:
Structure review for `hooks/useManualLog.js`

Responsibilities identified
- Owns manual-log modal state for an in-progress tracker draft session: open/closed flag, selected exercise-with-context, draft sets, and modal-local error state.
- Seeds the manual-log modal from tracker context, including sided-exercise handling, seed-set overrides, default form data, and draft-session performed date.
- Owns manual set editing behavior inside that modal: add set, remove set with renumbering, patch a set, and update form-parameter rows.
- Owns manual-log submit/close behavior for the tracker draft session: validation, normalization, append-to-draft-session, timer reopen, panel reset token increment, and completion announcement callback.

Recommended extractions or divisions
- No required extraction right now. The file is 136 lines, under the 150-line hook cap, and the current contents still read as one concern: manual tracker logging state plus modal lifecycle for an already-open tracker session. Confidence: high.
- Optional future extraction only if reuse appears: move the pure set-array transforms and submit-time normalization/validation shaping into a small `lib/manual-log-state.js` or similar helper. That would make sense only if the same draft-set rules need to be reused by another hook or surface. Today those helpers are single-use and tightly coupled to this hook's state shape, so extracting them now would likely create artificial fragmentation. Confidence: medium.

What should remain in the original file
- The React state for `manualLogState`.
- The modal lifecycle handlers (`openManualLog`, `handleManualModalClose`).
- The tracker-specific submit orchestration that writes into `draftSession`, reopens the timer, bumps `panelResetToken`, and calls `maybeAnnounceAllSetsComplete`.
- The bridge to `buildExerciseFormContext`, because that is part of this hook's job of turning tracker-selected exercise state into manual-log modal state.

Keep-together notes
- `openManualLog`, `updateManualSet`, and `handleManualModalSubmit` all depend on the same combined state contract: selected tracker exercise, sided-exercise context, draft-session date/set count, and modal-local set state. Splitting those across multiple hooks would increase coordination overhead without creating a cleaner ownership boundary. Confidence: high.
- The file does not appear to violate layer ownership. It stays in `hooks/`, uses React state/effects only, and delegates pure set-shaping work to `lib/session-logging.js` where that shared logic already exists. Confidence: high.

Open questions
- Should the hold-specific validation (`Please enter seconds per rep`) eventually live in a shared pure helper with `useSessionLogging` so both manual draft logging and history editing use the exact same validation wording/rules? I am not recommending that extraction from this file now, but it is the clearest possible shared-logic candidate if parity drift appears later. Confidence: low.
close_reason: Structure review completed and disposition applied: useManualLog split at orchestration vs pure set-logic boundary.
