notes:
Structure review findings for `hooks/useTrackerSession.js`

Responsibilities identified
- Owns active tracker-session orchestration state: `selectedExerciseId`, `selectedExercise`, `draftSession`, `isTimerOpen`, `currentSide`, `activeExercise`, `sessionStartedAt`.
- Owns exercise-context enrichment for tracker logging via `buildExerciseFormContext(...)`, including side-aware default form data and historical parameter lookup from `allLogs` plus current draft session sets.
- Owns exercise-selection and refresh wiring: `handleExerciseSelect(...)`, `handleTimerBack(...)`, and the refresh `useEffect(...)` that rehydrates the selected exercise when picker data or side changes.
- Owns pending-set / next-set flow: `pendingSetPatch`, `panelResetToken`, `handleTimerApplySet(...)`, `handleConfirmNextSet(...)`, `handleEditNextSet(...)`, `handlePreviousSet(...)`, and `handleBlockedNextSet(...)`.
- Delegates finish/save/cancel/backdate session finalization to `useTrackerSessionFinalization`, which is a good existing split.

Recommended extractions or divisions
1. Extract the pending-set / next-set workflow into a focused hook, for example `useTrackerPendingSetFlow`.
Confidence: high.
Why: lines 101-160 are a coherent sub-concern inside the broader session hook. They manage one state machine around `pendingSetPatch`, set confirmation/edit/manual fallback, undo, reset-token bumping, and comparison/speech side effects. That logic is independently editable from exercise selection and session-finalization wiring, and the current file is already above the hook hard cap at 163 lines. This is the clearest split point.
Suggested ownership for the extracted hook:
- `pendingSetPatch`
- `panelResetToken`
- `handleTimerApplySet(...)`
- `handleConfirmNextSet(...)`
- `handleEditNextSet(...)`
- `handlePreviousSet(...)`
- `handleBlockedNextSet(...)`
Likely inputs: `selectedExercise`, `draftSession`, `allLogs`, `buildExerciseFormContext`, `openManualLog`, `showToast`, `maybeAnnounceAllSetsComplete`, `speakText`, `setDraftSession`.

2. Keep `buildExerciseFormContext(...)` in this hook for now, but treat it as the next extraction candidate only if this file grows again.
Confidence: medium.
Why: it is used by multiple parts of the current session orchestration (`handleExerciseSelect`, `handleTimerApplySet`, and the selected-exercise refresh effect), so it is still acting as shared glue for the main session concern rather than a clearly separate workflow. Extracting it now would help line count, but it would also introduce another file that is tightly coupled to this hook’s state inputs (`draftSession`, `allLogs`, `currentSide`). That looks more optional than the pending-set split.
Potential future shape if needed:
- a focused hook such as `useTrackerExerciseContext`
- or a smaller helper boundary around selected-exercise enrichment plus refresh wiring

What should remain in the original file
- Session-level selection and lifecycle state: `selectedExerciseId`, `selectedExercise`, `draftSession`, `isTimerOpen`, `currentSide`, `activeExercise`, `sessionStartedAt`.
- `abandonDraftSession(...)` because it resets the core session-owned state and is used as coordination glue with finalization.
- `handleExerciseSelect(...)` and `handleTimerBack(...)` because they are route-facing session entry/exit orchestration.
- `buildExerciseFormContext(...)` for now, since it currently acts as the shared context builder across the remaining session concern.
- The existing `useTrackerSessionFinalization(...)` integration should stay as-is; that split already matches the repo’s one-concern-per-hook rule well.

Keep-together notes
- The existing separation between active-session orchestration here and finish/save/cancel/backdate work in `useTrackerSessionFinalization` should remain. Recombining them would move the file farther away from the repo hook guidance.
- `selectedExercise`, `currentSide`, and the refresh `useEffect(...)` should stay together with the main session ownership unless there is a deliberate extraction of the entire exercise-selection/context area. Those pieces appear tightly coupled and are refreshed from the same source data.

Open questions
- The file header and docblock still describe this hook as being for `pages/index.js`, while the current README route map is App Router-based. That may just be stale wording, but it is worth confirming so future agents do not anchor on an outdated owner.
- If the tracker still needs more active-session behavior added soon, it may be worth deciding now whether the long-term structure should be:
  - one parent session hook plus one pending-set sub-hook, or
  - a fuller split between selection/context and pending-set execution.
Right now the first option looks like the cleaner near-term move.

Overall assessment
- The file is not structurally unsound, but it is slightly past the hook hard cap and now contains one clear extra concern beyond its main session-orchestration role.
- Recommended action: extract the pending-set / next-set workflow first; keep the rest together unless later growth creates a second clean split.
close_reason: Structure review completed and disposition applied: useTrackerSession split to extract pending-set workflow and adjacent orchestration concerns.

title: Consolidate staggered git hook feedback
description:
Investigate why local commit and push checks report failures in separate passes instead of surfacing all actionable issues at once. Capture current hook order, identify which checks run at commit versus push, and recommend a lower-friction sequence or aggregation approach.

Why this issue exists:
Agents are seeing one failure at a time during commit workflow, such as style/lint feedback first and signature or policy feedback later, which creates avoidable repeated commit attempts and slower iteration.

Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] Current hook and validation order is documented from the repo
- [ ] The reason staggered feedback happens is identified
- [ ] A recommended approach is provided for surfacing more checks together or earlier
- [ ] Any repo-specific risks or constraints are called out
notes: Current local git validation is intentionally staggered across hook phases. In .beads/hooks/pre-commit, the repo runs README review, staged Biome format/lint (scripts/run-biome-staged.mjs), staged structure checks (scripts/check-structure.mjs), and staged gitleaks. In .beads/hooks/commit-msg, the repo separately enforces Beads issue IDs in the title and recognized AI contribution trailers. Because Git only runs commit-msg after pre-commit passes and after a commit message exists, those message-level errors can only appear on a later pass. This is why an agent can fix Biome first, retry commit, and only then be told about the missing AI trailer. Recommended lower-friction direction: keep semantic separation, but mirror commit-msg requirements earlier by either (1) adding a lightweight pre-commit advisory that inspects the pending message source when available, (2) auto-inserting the expected trailer/template in prepare-commit-msg, or (3) wrapping commit creation in a repo helper that runs pre-commit-style checks plus commit-message validation in one preview step before invoking git commit. Risk: true commit-msg validation still must remain in commit-msg because pre-commit does not reliably have the final message contents.
close_reason: Closed per operator confirmation: staggered git hook feedback item is done.

- No implementation work is mixed into the bead.
notes:
Responsibilities identified
- Layer check: `components/ExerciseFormTagSection.js` is correctly in the component layer. It is a UI-only helper used by `components/ExerciseFormCore.js` to render repeatable tag-entry sections for equipment, muscles, and form parameters.
- Current responsibilities in the file are cohesive: local input state for the current tag being added, option normalization for `NativeSelect`, add/remove UI wiring, and rendering the existing tag chips.
- The file header comment matches the implementation. I would not rename the file based on current contents.

Recommended extractions or divisions
- No extraction is required right now. The file is small, focused, and only serves one concern.
- If this control later needs richer behavior that varies by section (validation rules, duplicate messaging, async option lookup, drag reorder, inline editing, grouped options, or analytics/event reporting), that should not accumulate here. At that point, keep this file as the shared tag-section shell and extract the changing behavior into either:
  - a focused hook such as `useTagSectionInput` for add/normalize/duplicate handling, if the complexity is state/effect logic, or
  - explicit variant components if equipment/muscles/form-parameters start diverging in UI, rather than growing this file through boolean props.
- Small boundary note: option-shape normalization currently happens in both `NativeSelect` and `ExerciseFormTagSection`. This is not a structural problem yet, but if more callers start pre-normalizing options, ownership of option normalization should be clarified in one place instead of spreading across component wrappers.

What should remain in the original file
- The local `input` state.
- The select-to-add interaction and clear-on-success behavior.
- Rendering of the section label, select control, add button, optional hint text, and tag list.
- Simple duplicate prevention tied to the rendered `items` prop.

Keep-together notes
- Keeping this as a shared component used repeatedly by `ExerciseFormCore` is the right structure. The repeated tag-entry UI would be harder for future agents to maintain if it were copied back inline into `ExerciseFormCore`.
- `handleAdd` belongs here because it is directly coupled to the local `input` state and this component's immediate UI flow.
- Styling through `ExerciseForm.module.css` is acceptable for now because this helper appears to be a tightly coupled subcomponent of the exercise form family rather than a cross-domain generic control.

Open questions
- Should `NativeSelect` be the sole owner of option normalization shape, with `ExerciseFormTagSection` passing `options` through unchanged? Today both layers normalize string options. That is a mild maintainability ambiguity, not an urgent issue.
- If future requirements differ by tag type, should the repo prefer explicit section variants under the exercise-form family or a hook-backed shared shell? Based on the current composition guidance, explicit variants would be safer than adding behavior flags if UI differences become real.
close_reason: Completed read-only structure review and recorded findings

- Renders the lifecycle/status section of the exercise form, which matches the file header contract and the split noted in `ExerciseForm.js`.
- Owns UI for four related lifecycle concerns in one section: status selection, effective start/end dates, supersedes selection, and read-only lifecycle metadata (`superseded_by`, `added_date`, `updated_date`).
- Performs light view-shaping only: builds field IDs, filters the current exercise out of the supersedes options, resolves the display label for `superseded_by`, formats lifecycle labels, and trims ISO timestamps to `YYYY-MM-DD` for display.

### Recommended extractions or divisions
- No mandatory extraction is needed now. The file is 156 lines, under the 300-line component cap, and the contents are cohesive around one concern: lifecycle UI for the exercise form.
- Optional low-priority cleanup only: move the local `toTitleCase` helper at lines 10-15 to the existing shared formatter in `lib/text-format.js`, or add a lifecycle-specific pure helper if this exact status-label shaping is needed elsewhere later. This is duplication cleanup, not an ownership problem.
- Optional low-priority cleanup only: if other lifecycle-aware surfaces start repeating the same date slicing and lifecycle label logic, extract that formatting into a pure helper. Right now this component is the only direct consumer in scope, so extracting now would be premature.

### What should remain in the original file
- The rendered section layout and field grouping should stay here.
- The inline lifecycle guidance copy should stay here unless the product decides to centralize user-facing help text across multiple lifecycle surfaces.
- The small prop-driven view shaping should stay here for now because it is directly coupled to this section's render needs and does not yet justify a separate hook or lib file.

### Keep-together notes
- `status`, effective dates, supersedes selection, and read-only lifecycle metadata belong together in this component. Splitting them now would likely create artificial fragmentation because `ExerciseForm.js` already treats this as one form section and always loads it as a unit.
- The current structure keeps `ExerciseForm.js` at the orchestrator layer: it owns state and save wiring, while this file remains UI-only. That boundary is good and should be preserved.

### Open questions
- The file currently hardcodes lifecycle statuses at lines 6-8 while repo docs also describe lifecycle vocabulary in `docs/DATA_VOCABULARIES.md`. The hardcoded comment says this is approved behavior logic, so I treated it as intentional. If the product expects those values to become admin-managed domain data later, the ownership would need to move out of the component.
- There is a small ambiguity between the README note that lifecycle values are documented in repo docs and this component-local status list. That is documentation/ownership alignment to watch, but not a blocker for the current structure.

### Architecture judgment
- Layer check: this belongs in `components/` and currently stays within that layer.
- Cohesion: good.
- Future-agent maintainability: good as-is, with only minor optional deduplication if the same lifecycle display helpers spread to other files.
close_reason: Completed

notes:
## Read-only structure review for `components/ExerciseFormCues.js`

### Responsibilities identified
- `components/ExerciseFormCues.js` currently fits the `components/` layer: it is UI-only and receives all data via props (`guidance`, `onGuidanceChange`).
- Its actual domain is narrow and cohesive: render the Guidance & Cues section and provide local text-entry UX for four guidance arrays (`motor_cues`, `compensation_warnings`, `safety_flags`, `external_cues`).
- The local `GuidanceSection` helper stays within that same domain. It owns only per-section input draft state and the repeated add/remove ordered-list UI.

### Recommended extractions or divisions
- No extraction is recommended right now.
- `GuidanceSection` does not need its own file yet. It is only used here, is always loaded with this section, and extracting it would create extra file hopping without creating a clearer ownership boundary.
- `guidanceSetter()` also does not need a separate hook or lib helper. It is small, component-local wiring, and does not represent a separate state/effects concern or a pure reusable domain helper.

### What should remain in the original file
- The four guidance subsection render calls.
- The local ordered-list add/remove interaction for each subsection.
- The small component-local draft input state for each repeated subsection.
- The prop-level shaping that turns `guidance` plus `onGuidanceChange` into per-section add/remove handlers.

### Keep-together notes
- This file is under the component cap and passes the cohesion test: the filename still accurately describes the contents.
- Keeping the repeated guidance-entry UI together is better for future-agent maintainability than splitting each subsection or the helper into separate files.
- The current split with `ExerciseForm.js` as orchestrator, `ExerciseFormCore.js` for sections 1–4, this file for guidance/cues, and `ExerciseFormLifecycle.js` for lifecycle is structurally sound.

### Open questions / ambiguities
- There is a contract mismatch around ownership wording that could mislead future agents:
  - File header comment says this file is `exercise form section 5: guidance & cues`.
  - `ExerciseForm.js` doc comment still says `ExerciseFormCues (sections 5, 7, 8)`.
  - `README.md` groups the split files more broadly and historical notes mention roles editing in this file.
- In the live code, roles/dosage/vocabulary management appear to have moved out to separate workspaces, and this file now only renders section 5 guidance data. That narrower contract is the one the code currently matches.
- Recommendation from a maintainability standpoint: treat the file header as the correct contract unless there is hidden intended scope not visible from the current imports/usages.

notes:
## Read-only structure review for `components/ExerciseFormCore.js`

### Responsibilities identified
- Owns exercise form sections 1-4 exactly as the header comment says: basic info, equipment, muscles, and form parameters.
- Acts as a presentational section renderer under `components/ExerciseForm.js`, receiving all state and mutation callbacks through props.
- Adapts shared UI building blocks rather than owning persistence or route orchestration: it uses `NativeSelect` for vocab-backed selects and `ExerciseFormTagSection` for repeated tag-entry UI.
- Contains a small amount of section-local shaping logic: field/select adapter helpers, pattern-modifier toggle behavior, simple string normalization helpers, and vocabulary-term option shaping.

### Recommended extractions or divisions
- No urgent file split is needed right now. The file is over the `components/` target cap (231 lines vs 200L target) but still under the hard cap, and its current contents remain one cohesive concern: the core exercise-form section renderer.
- The inline `mapVocabTermsToOptions()` helper should move to the existing shared helper in `lib/vocab-options.js` rather than remain duplicated here. Repo guidance already says vocab-backed editor controls should use that shared helper, so this is the clearest ownership improvement.
- If this file grows again, the first extraction should be a `BasicInformationSection` child component. That subsection is the most distinct chunk because it owns the basic text fields, vocab selects, and pattern modifier checkboxes, while the equipment/muscle/form-parameter areas already share `ExerciseFormTagSection` and read more uniformly.

### What should remain in the original file
- The composition of sections 1-4 should stay here. That matches both the file header contract and the README entry describing `ExerciseFormCore` as one split portion of `ExerciseForm`.
- The lightweight prop-to-input wiring helpers (`field`, `selectField`) can stay local unless another exercise-form section starts needing the same adapter shape.
- The hardcoded `MODIFIERS` list should stay with this component. It is behavior configuration for this specific section, already documented with the required approval comment, and does not look like shared domain data.
- The simple normalization callbacks used only by this file (`toSentenceCase`, `toLower`) can stay local for now. They are tiny and tightly coupled to how this section feeds `ExerciseFormTagSection`.

### Keep-together notes
- Equipment, muscles, and form parameters are already on the right side of the boundary: the generic chip/select mechanics live in `ExerciseFormTagSection`, while this file keeps only the domain-specific labels, option sources, and add/remove wiring.
- Keeping sections 1-4 together is still maintainable because they are always composed together by `ExerciseForm` and do not currently represent independently reused workspaces.

### Open questions
- None that block the review. The only mild ambiguity is whether future growth should split by visual subsection (`BasicInformationSection`) or by input type (`TagSections` vs non-tag fields). Today the visual-subsection split looks cleaner if expansion happens.

notes:
Structure review for `components/DosageModal.js`

Responsibilities identified
- The file is currently serving three responsibilities at once: modal shell/rendering (`components`-appropriate), form state/prefill lifecycle (`useState` + `useEffect`), and dosage-domain parsing/validation/payload shaping.
- The shared placement itself is correct. The modal is reused from both `/program` and tracker overlay flows (`app/(protected)/program/ProgramPage.js:321-327`, `app/(protected)/TrackerOverlays.js:169-175`), so it should remain a shared surface rather than becoming route-local.
- The header comment and README entry both describe this file as a shared dosage editor, but the implementation currently contains domain rules about `dosage_type`, `seconds_per_rep`, `seconds_per_set`, and modifier-driven validation (`components/DosageModal.js:17-26`, `41-65`, `67-123`). That is broader than UI-only ownership under the repo structure rules.

Recommended extractions or divisions
- Recommended extraction: move dosage form normalization/prefill/validation into a non-UI helper or dedicated hook, and leave `DosageModal` as the modal shell plus field wiring.
- Best-fit split based on current repo rules:
  - `components/DosageModal.js` should keep modal structure, labels, inputs, submit/cancel controls, and prop-driven conditional field rendering.
  - A focused hook such as `hooks/useDosageForm.js` could own the string state, prefill/reset effect, validation, and submit shaping.
  - If the team prefers pure helpers over a hook, a dosage-domain lib helper should own the pure rules: deriving visible fields/labels from modifiers, converting program data into form defaults, validating parsed values, and building the outgoing payload.
- Reason for recommending extraction: `components/` are supposed to stay UI-only, but this file currently embeds business rules that are already meaningful outside the modal UI. The strongest example is dosage type inference/payload shaping in `components/DosageModal.js:98-113`, while other write paths already rely on shared domain helpers such as `inferDosageType` in `lib/program-optimistic.js:56-62` and optimistic program construction in `hooks/useTrackerDosageEditor.js:15-28` / `hooks/useProgramMutationActions.js:53-83`.
- There is a maintainability risk from having dosage semantics split across layers. Right now the modal decides what fields exist and what payload keys are emitted, while save hooks separately infer dosage type again. That is not an immediate bug report here, but it is a clear future-agent risk because changing dosage semantics would require coordinated edits in multiple places.

What should remain in the original file
- Keep the shared modal ownership in `components/DosageModal.js`; it is correctly shared across program and tracker surfaces.
- Keep the actual JSX shell, overlay close behavior, accessibility attributes, field labels, and input rendering in this component.
- Keep the prop-level decision of whether the modal is open and which exercise/program it edits in parent orchestrators; current callers already handle that cleanly.

Keep-together notes
- The modal should stay paired with its CSS module; there is no structural reason to split styling from the component.
- The conditional field rendering itself belongs close to the rendered inputs, as long as the underlying dosage-rule derivation is supplied from one focused place. In other words, “which rows to show” can stay near JSX, but “how dosage data is interpreted and serialized” should have one authoritative owner.
- No extraction is needed just for size. At ~201 lines, this is right at the component aim but still under the hard cap. The reason to split is ownership clarity, not line count.

Open questions
- The repo already has `lib/dosage-summary.js` for display wording and `lib/program-optimistic.js` for dosage-type inference, but there does not appear to be one shared helper that owns the full dosage form contract. It is unclear whether the intended long-term home is a new dosage-domain lib file or a focused hook local to the modal.
- `inferDosageType` currently lives in `lib/program-optimistic.js`, whose header says it is for `/program` optimistic mutations. If tracker and shared modal flows need the same dosage-domain rule set, that helper may already be living in a file whose filename no longer fully describes its contents.
- If the team wants `components/` to remain strictly UI-only, this modal is a good candidate for the next small boundary cleanup. If the team instead treats self-contained modal form logic as acceptable inside components, then the current structure is still cohesive enough to keep together for now, with the duplication risk documented above.
close_reason: Completed read-only structure review and wrote findings to notes

notes:
Read-only structure review for app/(protected)/pt-view/PtViewPage.js.

Responsibilities identified
- PtViewPage.js is acting as a route client host / orchestrator, which matches its header contract and the repo rule that app/**/page.js stays thin while *Page.js files coordinate shared hooks and UI.
- Current responsibilities in the file are: auth gate handoff, patient/user-context wiring, pt-view data bootstrap wiring, email toggle wiring, message modal wiring, route-local modal/open state, route-local expanded-session state, and passing derived view models into UI components.
- The file is 226 lines, under the 350L aim / 500L cap for a route client host, and most of that code is composition rather than mixed domain logic.

Recommended extractions or divisions
- No required extraction from PtViewPage.js right now. The file is cohesive as a route orchestrator and does not currently violate the page-host boundary.
- Optional future extraction only if this route grows: move the route-local presentation/controller state around expanded sessions and exercise-history modal targeting (currently lines 75-114) into a focused hook such as usePtViewHistoryPanelState. That is not necessary today, but it is the cleanest split point if more history-panel behavior lands here.
- Separate follow-up ambiguity outside the target file: lib/pt-view.js currently mixes pt-view dashboard helpers with message transport/counting helpers used by hooks/useMessages.js (lib/pt-view.js lines 64-123 and 277-305). That does not make PtViewPage.js wrong, but it weakens domain naming/cohesion for future agents.
- Separate follow-up ambiguity outside the target file: hooks/usePtViewUiState.js combines persisted filter/panel state with note keyword/highlight shaping (lines 37-114). It is still manageable at 115 lines, but if either filters or note processing grows, that hook should split by concern rather than pushing anything back into PtViewPage.js.

What should remain in the original file
- Auth/session branching and sign-in fallback.
- Wiring useUserContext, usePtViewData, useEmailNotifications, useMessages, and usePtViewUiState together.
- Small route-local state that exists only to connect page sections and modals.
- Final derivation/composition that prepares props for PatientNotes, PtViewNeedsAttention, PtViewSummaryStats, PtViewFiltersPanel, HistoryList, MessagesModal, and ExerciseHistoryModal.
- Dynamic import ownership for the two modals, since that is route-composition behavior rather than modal internals.

Keep-together notes
- The page-level derived values (filteredLogs/dateGroups, needsAttention with urgency, stats) should stay close to the route host unless they become reused elsewhere. Today they are part of assembling this screen, not an independently shared domain.
- The small inline header actions block, including the unread badge button, is still acceptable to keep here. It is route-specific UI glue, not a reusable subsystem.
- HistoryList, PatientNotes, PtViewFiltersPanel, PtViewNeedsAttention, and PtViewSummaryStats are already correctly extracted as UI-only components, so this file should not reclaim any of their rendering logic.

Open questions
- Should message fetch/mutation helpers continue to live in lib/pt-view.js, or should they move to a message-focused lib file so the filename matches the contents more accurately?
- If pt-view filtering expands beyond the current four fields, should filter state become its own hook separate from note-dismiss/highlight behavior?

notes:
Read-only structure review for `app/(protected)/program/ProgramPage.js`.

Responsibilities identified
- `ProgramPage.js` is in the correct layer: a client route host / orchestrator. The file header contract matches the actual role.
- It currently owns auth gating and route entry fallback (`ProgramPage.js:43-59`, `185-187`).
- It wires route-level data/workspace/mutation/offline hooks together (`60-183`).
- It keeps route-level lazy-loading boundaries for heavier editor surfaces (`33-41`).
- It composes the main editor workspaces and route-local modal/disclosure state (`45`, `189-328`).

Recommended extractions or divisions
- Optional extraction: move the queue status/retry banner (`196-220`) into a dedicated UI component such as `components/ProgramQueueBanner.js`.
  Why: this is the most independently maintainable JSX block still living in the host. It owns a distinct display-state matrix (failed vs syncing vs queued), recovery copy, and retry affordance. Future edits to queue messaging or retry UX should not require loading the whole route host.
  Boundary: keep it UI-only and pass in `queueSummary`, `queueError`, `queueSyncing`, `onRetry`, and any label/copy inputs as props.
- No other extraction is required right now. The file is 318 lines, below the 350L aim for a route client host, and most remaining code is orchestration glue rather than feature implementation.

What should remain in the original file
- Dynamic imports for `ExerciseForm`, `DosageModal`, and `ProgramVocabEditor` (`33-41`) should stay here because the route host should own route-level loading boundaries.
- Snapshot assembly / commit glue (`102-119`) should stay here. It coordinates `useProgramPageData`, `useProgramDataSnapshot`, mutation hooks, and offline replay, so it belongs in the orchestrator rather than a UI component.
- The hook wiring block (`121-183`) should stay here. It is the route-level composition point for auth, bootstrap, workspace state, optimistic mutations, and queue lifecycle.
- Route-local UI state such as `isVocabOpen` and `dosageTarget` handling (`45`, `294-326`) should stay here unless the vocabulary workspace becomes a larger standalone surface.
- The main workspace composition (`247-327`) should stay here; the page is correctly acting as the place where the selector, exercise form, roles workspace, dosage workspace, vocab editor, and modal are assembled.

Keep-together notes
- The current split is already strong: data/bootstrap in `useProgramPageData`, workspace derivation in `useProgramWorkspaceState`, queue lifecycle in `useProgramOfflineQueue`, optimistic writes in mutation hooks, and major UI surfaces in dedicated components.
- `getCurrentSnapshot` and `commitSnapshot` should stay close to the hook wiring because they bridge multiple hook domains and are not reusable UI concerns.
- I would not split the host further just to reduce line count. Beyond the queue banner, additional splitting would risk artificial fragmentation rather than clearer ownership.

Open questions
- `ProgramPage.js:94-100` still performs some UI-facing derived shaping (grouped select options, vocab option mapping, dosage summary formatting). This is acceptable today, but if those derivations grow, the clean next home is a focused route hook or existing workspace-state hook rather than more logic in the host.
- Related-import observation only, not a requested scope expansion: `components/ProgramExerciseSelector.js` currently imports `buildGroupedLifecycleOptions` from `lib/`, while `ProgramPage.js` also does similar option shaping. Repo structure docs say components should stay UI-only, so there may be a broader boundary choice to clarify later around where option-shaping belongs.

## Read-only structure review for `app/(protected)/TrackerRouteShell.js`

### Responsibilities identified
- `TrackerRouteShell` is in the route-client-host layer, but it is acting as a render-only shell rather than an orchestrator. That matches its header contract on line 1 and the README ownership note.
- It owns four presentation concerns that are still one cohesive shell concern: header chrome (`PT Tracker`, connectivity indicator, messages button, nav menu), banner/toast display, tab-body switching, and bottom navigation. These appear in lines 48-129.
- It delegates feature-detail UI to existing shared components instead of re-implementing them: `ExercisePicker`, `HistoryPanel`, `BottomNav`, `NavMenu`, and `Toast`.

### Recommended extractions or divisions
- No extraction is required now. The file is small, under the component-size guidance, and cohesive around one concern: tracker route shell composition.
- The most likely future split point would be the header block at lines 48-79 if it grows meaningfully beyond current shell chrome. Today that would be premature because the header state is still simple and loaded only with this shell.
- A lower-priority cleanup candidate is prop-shape compression rather than file extraction. The shell currently receives a very wide prop list (lines 9-44) because `TrackerPage` passes through raw props for the picker/history areas. If this surface grows, consider grouped props such as `headerProps`, `bannerProps`, `exerciseTabProps`, and `historyTabProps` or route-local view-model objects. That would improve future-agent readability without changing the layer split.

### What should remain in the original file
- The shell-level layout and tab switching should remain here: header, banners/toast placement, conditional body selection between `ExercisePicker` and `HistoryPanel`, and `BottomNav` wiring.
- The inline connectivity glyph and message badge markup can remain here because they are shell chrome, not reusable domain UI on their own.
- The direct composition of `ExercisePicker` and `HistoryPanel` should remain here. This is the route shell choosing which major workspace is visible, which matches orchestrator/shell ownership.

### Keep-together notes
- `Toast` should stay colocated with the shell rather than moving into either tab panel because it is route-wide feedback, not panel-local UI.
- The banner stack (`fromCache`, `error`, `historyError`) should stay together here because they are route-level status surfaces and would otherwise be duplicated or split across unrelated components.
- The shell should stay separate from `TrackerPage`. Keeping this render-only file distinct from the route host is helpful for maintainability because `TrackerPage` already owns many hooks, handlers, and derived state values.

### Open questions
- `NavMenu` is hardcoded with `isAdmin={true}` on line 72. I did not widen scope far enough to determine whether that is an intentional shell contract, a temporary placeholder, or a layering leak from `TrackerPage`/user context. It is worth validating before additional header behavior is added.
- The file uses `TrackerPage.module.css` rather than a shell-specific stylesheet. That is not automatically a problem, but if the CSS module is also carrying overlay or non-shell concerns, the styling ownership may become less clear than the JS ownership.
- The prop width suggests `TrackerPage` may eventually benefit from route-local prop grouping, but the current shell file itself is not over-broad.

notes:
-
## Structure review for `app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js`

### What layer does this belong in?
This file belongs in the route-client-host layer. Its header contract says it is the tracker logging route host and `ORCHESTRATOR ONLY`, so it should coordinate hooks, route-level state, and render handoff into shell/overlay components rather than own feature UI or domain logic.

### Responsibilities identified
- Auth gate handoff and protected-route bootstrap: `useAuth`, `useUserContext`, `useIndexData`, and `useIndexOfflineQueue` wiring at [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:40).
- Route-local UI state orchestration: active tab, messages open state, email toggle mirror, and online/offline state at [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:45), [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:66), and [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:265).
- Tracker workflow composition: `useTrackerSession`, `useManualLog`, `useSessionLogging`, `useLoggerFeedback`, reconnect recovery, and dosage editor coordination at [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:122), [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:178), [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:189), and [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:288).
- Render delegation into thin view files: shell rendering is already pushed into [app/(protected)/TrackerRouteShell.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerRouteShell.js:1) and overlays into [app/(protected)/TrackerOverlays.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerOverlays.js:1).

### Recommended extractions or divisions
- Extract the program-to-picker shaping block at [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:92) into a pure tracker lib helper, because it is data shaping rather than route orchestration. The current filename still passes the cohesion test overall, but this block reads like page-domain transformation logic.
- Extract the picker-history badge shaping at [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:192) into a pure tracker lib helper for the same reason. It mixes adherence-state derivation into the route host even though the host already imports lib helpers for adjacent derived values.
- Consider extracting the online-status subscription at [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:265) into a tiny shared hook only if another route needs the same browser-online state. If this remains tracker-only, leaving it here is acceptable.
- Consider extracting the message/email modal control pair at [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:301) and [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:324) into a focused hook if this area keeps growing. Right now it is still small enough to stay in the host, but it is the clearest cluster that could become its own concern.

### What should remain in the original file
- Route-level wiring among auth, data bootstrap, offline queue, reconnect recovery, tracker session, logger, messages, and toast hooks.
- Thin event handlers that only bridge one route concern to another, such as sign-out, manual sync, tab change, and edit-log launch.
- Final prop assembly for `TrackerRouteShell` and `TrackerOverlays`, because these files are already the correct render-only splits.

### Keep-together notes
- Keep the shell/overlay split as-is. `TrackerPage.js` plus `TrackerRouteShell.js` plus `TrackerOverlays.js` form a clean orchestrator/view boundary today, and those pieces would still be loaded together by this route.
- Keep the `manualOpenRef` / `feedbackRef` bridge with `useTrackerSession` and `useManualLog` together unless there is a larger redesign. It is a coordination seam between hooks rather than stray business logic, even though it adds mental overhead at [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:116).
- The file is 427 lines, so it is under the 500-line route-host cap, but it is close enough that future additions should prefer extraction into hooks/lib helpers rather than expanding this host.

### Open questions
- `handleEmailToggle` currently performs an inline dynamic import and mutation call at [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:301). It is small, but it is also mutation logic in the route host. If this flow gains retries, status UI, or shared reuse, it should move to a dedicated hook.
- `handleEditLog` at [app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js](C:/Users/cindi/OneDrive/Documents/GitHub/pttracker/app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js:237) includes fallback exercise reconstruction that may belong in a tracker-domain helper if edit-path edge cases continue to grow. Right now it is still defensible as route glue because it exists solely to hand the logger the right exercise object.

### Review conclusion
No urgent structural split is required today. The file still reads as a route orchestrator, and the existing extractions into `TrackerRouteShell`, `TrackerOverlays`, and focused hooks are doing the right work. The main maintainability opportunity is to move the remaining pure derived data shaping out of the host before the next feature adds more weight.

notes:
## Read-only structure review for `app/(protected)/TrackerOverlays.js`

### Responsibilities identified
- `app/(protected)/TrackerOverlays.js` is in the route-local client support layer, not the shared `components/` layer. That fits the current rules because it is adapting tracker-route orchestration state into a render-only overlay stack.
- The file owns three cohesive responsibilities that belong together:
  - lazy-loading overlay UI modules (`next/dynamic`, lines 4-17)
  - conditional mounting of tracker overlays (lines 65-176)
  - light prop-shaping between route state names and component APIs, especially for `TimerPanel` through `TimerPanelSection` (lines 181-212)
- The file header comment remains accurate. I would not rename the file based on its current contents.

### Recommended extractions or divisions
- No mandatory split recommended right now. The file stays within one concern: route-local overlay composition for the tracker.
- The one area that is close to mixed responsibility is the `SessionLoggerModal` block (lines 65-99). It is not just wiring visibility; it is encoding two different workflows in one prop matrix:
  - manual set logging via `manualLogState` / `manualLog`
  - history/session editing via `logger`
- Signs that this block is carrying workflow-shaping logic instead of only overlay composition:
  - many repeated `manualLogState.isOpen ? ... : ...` branches across most props
  - two no-op callbacks used only to satisfy the shared modal API (`onPerformedAtChange` and `onNotesChange`, lines 84-85)
  - computed defaults that differ by workflow (`performedAt`, `notes`, `submitting`, `error`, lines 74-82)
- If this area grows, prefer one of these route-local extractions:
  - a focused prop-adapter helper such as `getSessionLoggerOverlayProps(...)` in a nearby route-local file, or
  - two explicit route-local wrappers such as `ManualSetLoggerOverlay` and `HistorySessionLoggerOverlay` that each render `SessionLoggerModal` with a clean, single-workflow contract.
- I would not split the rest of the overlays into separate files yet. They are still straightforward conditional mounts with direct prop pass-through.

### What should remain in the original file
- Dynamic imports for the overlay stack.
- The top-level conditional mounting of tracker overlays.
- Route-local prop translation for overlays whose public APIs differ slightly from tracker naming, including `TimerPanelSection`.
- Close/open wiring for notes, messages, next-set confirmation, and dosage overlays, as long as they remain thin adapters.

### Keep-together notes
- Keeping the full overlay stack in one route-local file is helpful for future agents because these overlays are opened from the same tracker workflow and are typically inspected together.
- `TimerPanelSection` should stay with this file for now. It is a small adapter, not an independently maintainable feature, and splitting it out would create artificial fragmentation.
- The messages, dosage, notes, and next-set sections currently read as true composition, not feature ownership drift.

### Open questions
- Is `SessionLoggerModal` intentionally meant to stay a shared shell for both manual set logging and session-history editing long-term? If yes, the current approach is acceptable but should stay capped. If those flows are expected to diverge, explicit wrappers would be the safer next step.
- Is the inline notes updater on lines 139-141 intentionally route-local? It is still small enough to keep here, but if more draft-session mutation rules collect around notes, that behavior should move back into the tracker session-finalization hook instead of growing in this file.

notes:
-
responsibilities identified
- README.md currently acts as the live file-ownership map for the repo. That is the correct primary layer for this file, and it matches the guidance in docs/NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md and docs/SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md.
- It also carries three secondary responsibilities: operational tooling inventory under Folder Structure, tracker-specific execution-boundary guidance under Tracker Execution Stack, and README authoring policy under README Maintenance Rules.
- The route map, shared layer ownership lists, and canonical docs index all fit the file-map role and should stay discoverable from this file.

recommended extractions or divisions, if any
- Recommended extraction: move the repo-operational tooling inventory out of README.md into a focused doc such as docs/REPO_OPERATIONS_MAP.md or docs/TOOLING_AND_GUARDRAILS.md, then keep a short pointer in README.md. The long block under Folder Structure that documents analyzer scripts, Biome helpers, pre-commit hooks, secret-scan workflows, and Dependabot is useful, but it reads more like operator/tooling guidance than a live codebase map.
- Recommended extraction: move the detailed Tracker Execution Stack section into a tracker-focused architecture doc or implementation-pattern doc, then leave a short ownership pointer in README.md. It is coherent on its own, but it is a sub-domain deep dive rather than general repo-map content.
- Recommended division if the file grows further: separate README maintenance authoring rules into docs/README_MAINTENANCE.md or fold them into docs/AGENT_PLAYBOOK.md. They are policy for contributors, not part of the live runtime/file map itself.

what should remain in the original file
- Keep Repo Shape, Current Route And Legacy Surface Map, top-level Folder Structure, Shared Components, Shared Utilities, Shared Hooks, Canonical Docs, and Deployment References in README.md.
- Keep short ownership statements for shared files in README.md because the repo docs explicitly designate README.md as the maintained live file map.
- Keep brief boundary notes such as UI-only, pure helper, orchestrator, and route-host ownership in README.md.

keep-together notes, if any
- The route map plus shared file ownership lists should stay together. Future agents need one landing page that answers what exists now and where a concern belongs.
- Shared component, utility, and hook ownership can remain in the same file as long as they stay concise. Splitting those out too early would weaken the repo's single-entry map and force extra document hopping.
- README.md is only 239 lines, so this is not a size-pressure recommendation. This is a cohesion recommendation: the file is still maintainable, but some sections are drifting from map-of-the-codebase into contributor-operations handbook.

open questions, if any
- It is slightly ambiguous whether the repo intentionally wants README.md to be both the live file map and the contributor maintenance handbook. docs/SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md says to use README.md as the live file map, not as a catch-all architecture narrative, which suggests the current tooling and maintenance-policy detail may be overextended.
- The target file does not have a file-header comment, so I could not apply the header-contract rule literally. For a Markdown landing document that may be acceptable, but the lack of an explicit one-line contract makes the mixed responsibilities easier to accumulate.

title: Restrict Biome bypass to one approved file

- README.md

Issue:
Change the Biome staged-file helper so the bypass path only works for one explicitly approved file instead of all staged files.

Context:
The current bypass env var skips Biome auto-write for the whole commit. The user wants a third option: keep an emergency path, but require explicit approval and limit it to a single named file.

Constraints/Caveats:
Keep the default behavior unchanged for normal commits. Preserve the ability to lint all staged files, and do not widen the bypass beyond one file in this pass.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Default staged-file behavior is unchanged when no bypass env vars are set

- Bypass can target only one named staged file
- Non-bypassed staged files still go through normal Biome auto-write behavior
- README accurately documents the new one-file approval flow

- [ ] Hook still runs normally with no bypass env vars
- [ ] README documents the approval, reason, and one-file requirement

n/a

title: Decide how to handle PatientNotes highlighted HTML under Biome security rule
description:
Source Reference: pt-lnfp.1
Status: open
Priority: P2
Risk: medium
Scope: current
Agent Owner: shared
Agent Eligible: codex,claude
Tags: ui,security,biome,notes
File Scope:
- C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\pttracker\components\PatientNotes.js
- C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\pttracker\hooks\usePtViewUiState.js

Issue:
Biome flags PatientNotes.js for lint/security/noDangerouslySetInnerHtml because the component renders preprocessed highlighted note text with dangerouslySetInnerHTML. This should not be changed blindly during markup cleanup because the current rendering may be intentional and tied to highlighted note UX.

Context:
During pt-lnfp.1 accessibility cleanup, the commit-blocking button-type and label/control issues were resolved safely. PatientNotes.js was intentionally left out of that cleanup because the remaining Biome concern is a product/security decision, not a mechanical lint fix. The component currently receives preprocessed displayText from upstream note processing and renders it as HTML to preserve keyword highlighting.

Questions to resolve:
- Keep the current highlighted HTML rendering and add a targeted suppression/comment?
- Refactor the note-highlighting pipeline to avoid dangerouslySetInnerHTML entirely?
- Adjust Biome config or local rule scope for this one intentional pattern?

Constraints/Caveats:
Do not remove note highlighting or alter note rendering behavior without an explicit decision. Any chosen path should preserve current visible note highlighting unless the user approves a UX change.

Acceptance Criteria:
- A clear decision is recorded for how PatientNotes should satisfy or intentionally bypass the Biome security rule
- The chosen path preserves current note UX unless an explicit UX change is approved
- Follow-up implementation can proceed without rediscovering the tradeoff

Validation Checklist:
- [ ] Current reason for dangerouslySetInnerHTML is documented
- [ ] Recommended path is chosen or escalated clearly
- [ ] Future implementation scope is separated from pt-lnfp.1 safe markup cleanup

Dependencies:
- discovered-from: pt-lnfp.1
- blocks: none

Reactivation Trigger:
Address when resuming Biome cleanup or when deciding whether highlighted note HTML remains intentional.
notes: 2026-04-10 Codex review: decision-shaped Biome item is lint/security/noDangerouslySetInnerHtml at components/PatientNotes.js line 38. The rendered HTML string is created upstream in hooks/usePtViewUiState.js lines 47-56 by inserting highlight span markup into raw log.notes text, so note content is treated as markup at render time. Other nearby findings are mechanical: missing button type at PatientNotes.js line 39, plus format and import-order cleanup in PatientNotes.js and usePtViewUiState.js. Options: 1) preferred: replace HTML-string highlighting with tokenized plain-text rendering that returns React nodes and keeps the same visible highlight behavior; 2) fallback: sanitize note text before adding highlight markup, then keep a narrow suppression with a reason comment for the remaining intentional dangerouslySetInnerHTML usage; 3) lowest-friction: suppress the rule locally or in config and accept the remaining injection risk and debt.
close_reason: Completed safe PatientNotes highlighting refactor and touched-file Biome cleanup

Issue:
The local analyzer helper can silently inspect the wrong bundle because the analyzer UI defaults to an unrelated route and the helper does not require an explicit route or surface choice.

Context:
During the audio investigation, the analyzer opened on /api/exercises/[id] instead of /, which would have produced misleading bundle results. The helper should require the operator to choose the route first and also choose the client or server surface explicitly.

Constraints/Caveats:
Keep the helper plain-language and fail clearly when route or surface are omitted. Match the current analyzer output structure for this repo.

n/a
notes: Completed on 2026-04-10: both analyzer helpers now require an explicit surface choice, and the route helper also requires an explicit route choice or --root. The packed JSON boundary logic was rewritten to read from raw bytes so modules.data no longer overruns the final CSR range. The module graph helper now filters matches and related modules by client vs server tags. The route helper treats mixed files as eligible for both surfaces based on output-chunk membership, which matches the live analyzer behavior where some files are surface-specific and mixed files appear in both. README examples were updated to show the guarded usage.

Agent Eligible: codex,claude
Tags: tooling,analyzer,scripts
File Scope:
- scripts/dump-analyzer-paths.mjs

Issue:
The analyzer path helper silently defaults to /, which can produce misleading results when the browser analyzer is currently showing a different default route such as /api/exercises/[id].

Context:
During audio investigation, the analyzer UI opened on /api/exercises/[id] by default. The helper script did not force an explicit route choice, so it was easy to inspect the wrong bundle by accident. The desired behavior is to require --route or --root explicitly.

Constraints/Caveats:
Keep the root-route convenience flow for Windows shells. The fix should fail clearly when the route is omitted.

Validation Checklist:
- [ ] Running the helper without --route or --root exits with a clear error
- [ ] Running the helper with --root still works for the tracker route
- [ ] Existing explicit --route usage still works

Dependencies:
- discovered-from: pt-yirr
- blocks: none

Reactivation Trigger:
n/a
close_reason: Closed

title: Add periodic toolchain update checks
description:
## Summary
Add a recurring maintenance lane for checking whether the key local and repo-scoped tools used by the PT Tracker agent workflow have updates available and, when they do, note the correct update path instead of guessing.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] This work is tracked as a child of the repo safeguards epic.
- [ ] The check scope includes the actual current toolchain used in this environment: bd, dolt, go, node, npm, vercel, supabase, rg, git, gh, docker, local gitleaks, and repo Biome.
- [ ] The bead notes the correct update method for each tool family so future work does not improvise upgrades.
- [ ] Follow-up automation or operating guidance can point back to this bead as the maintenance lane.

## Notes
Requested after establishing that PT Tracker is fully agent-managed and should rely on tools and automation rather than memory for upkeep.
notes: Carryover at session end 2026-04-14: periodic toolchain update-check lane remains future maintenance work; no active implementation in this session.

title: Analyzer module-graph script fails on current Next analyzer output
description:
## Summary
While debugging the deployed PRN save path, the bundle analyzer helper scripts were used as intended. 
pm run analyze:bundle succeeded and dump-analyzer-paths.mjs worked, but dump-analyzer-module-graph.mjs crashes with RangeError [ERR_OUT_OF_RANGE] when reading the current analyzer output format.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Run 
pm run analyze:bundle.
2. Run 
pm run analyze:modules -- --term updateExercise.

## Actual
- The script crashes with a buffer offset RangeError while decoding CSR data.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] 
pm run analyze:modules -- --term <value> runs successfully against current Next analyzer output.
- [ ] The helper can be used for future route/module investigations without manual patching.
notes: Enhancing analyzer-live-helper.js to expose richer dependent metadata from the live analyzer UI: branch index, path label, client/server side marker, and Current route only state.

title: Assess PRN/as-needed exercise support design
description:
## Summary
Evaluate what would be required to support a PRN/as-needed exercise state in PT Tracker and whether lifecycle_status is the right existing surface to carry it.

## Questions
- Which current code paths rely on lifecycle_status versus archived?
- What would need to change for tracker filtering, rehab counts, pt-view, and program/editor behavior?
- Is lifecycle_status='as_needed' the safest design, or should this be a separate field?

## Acceptance Criteria
- Identify affected files and behavior surfaces.
- Recommend the safest implementation direction.
- Call out required API, UI, and counting/filtering changes.
- Note risks, migration needs, and verification scope.
notes:
Key design finding: PRN/as-needed appears patient-specific, so using exercises.lifecycle_status would make one exercise PRN for every patient. Logged follow-up decision bead for the data-model direction.
Assessment summary: the existing archived show/hide checkbox pattern is reusable for the UI, but the recommended data model is patient_programs-level PRN state rather than exercises.lifecycle_status. Key affected surfaces if implemented: patient_programs schema + API, tracker picker filtering/toggles, pt-view needs-attention/program filtering, rehab coverage input filtering, and program editor controls.
close_reason: Assessment complete; recommendation captured and follow-up decision bead pt-1eq7 created.

title: Trim bd prime token load
description:
## Why
bd prime is injecting too much low-signal workflow context for PT Tracker sessions, which increases token cost and hides the most useful guidance.

## Design
Focus on Beads remembered memories and other repo-controlled prime inputs, not app code.

title: Verify tracker behavior after concurrent rendering update
description:
## Why
pt-injf changed TrackerPage render priority wiring with startTransition and useDeferredValue. We need a follow-up verification pass to confirm tracker behavior, including cache-backed behavior, still works as expected after that route-host change.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tracker still loads correctly from cache or network after the concurrent rendering update.
- Switching between Exercises and History still works correctly.
- Adherence badges still render correctly in the exercise picker.
- No regression is observed in the tracker flow attributable to the pt-injf change.

## Design
Verification-only bead. No code changes unless a regression is found.
notes:
Starting browser verification on preview for pt-injf: tracker load, Exercises/History tab switching, adherence badge rendering, and basic cache-backed behavior check.
2026-04-04 verification pass: preview authenticated successfully with patient2 and the live-data admin account. Tracker cached offline content still rendered after returning to / while offline: exercise list loaded, Offline badge showed, Exercises/History tab switching still worked, and adherence badges/recency text rendered in the picker. Admin account then signed in online and loaded /program successfully with privileged UI present (PT Editor, New button, role assignment workspace, dosage workspace, vocabulary disclosure) without performing any save actions. No regression attributable to pt-injf observed in tracker or admin route-host behavior.
close_reason: Verified on preview: tracker still loads from cached state, tabs/adherence render correctly, and admin account reaches /program without regression after pt-injf.

title: Fix commit-msg AI trailer warning text
description:
## Why
The commit-msg hook treats missing AI trailers as a warning, not a blocking error, but its output still tells agents to use PT_AI_TRAILER_OK to bypass the warning. That guidance is inconsistent and confusing because there is nothing to bypass when the commit already succeeds.

## Acceptance Criteria
- The commit-msg hook no longer suggests using PT_AI_TRAILER_OK to bypass a non-blocking warning.
- The warning text clearly distinguishes required Beads issue IDs from optional AI attribution trailers.
- Repo guidance stays consistent with the hook behavior.

## Design
Keep Beads issue IDs as the actual gate. Keep AI trailer handling as advisory unless separately made mandatory later. Make the hook messaging reflect that reality.

title: Thin per-arm truth-check formula
description:
Why this issue exists and what needs to be done:
- The current mol-bead-truth-check formula creates a heavy epic plus child-step chain for each investigated bead.
- That structure helped fanout but created too much tracker overhead and cleanup cost during the open-bead truth-check pass.
- Rewrite the per-arm formula to preserve dynamic bonding and evidence capture while minimizing temporary bead surface.
- Update repo Beads docs to match the lighter pattern and command guidance.

Acceptance:
- The per-arm formula no longer creates a multi-child epic workflow for each investigated bead.
- The parent truth-pass formula still supports one arm per bead with explicit variables.
- Repo docs describe the lighter arm shape and when to use it.

Design:
- Keep the parent-plus-arms graph model.
- Reduce arm structure to the minimum needed for evidence capture and recommendation.
- Avoid new repo-specific formula rules beyond what the docs support.

title: Backlog cleanup pass protocol and restart brief
description: Record the approved two-pass backlog cleanup method under pt-fegm so a post-compaction session can resume consistently. Scope: document pass order, confidence thresholds, labeling scheme, evidence fields, and the rule to use pt-fegm as the cleanup container rather than creating standalone cleanup beads.
notes:
APPROVED CLEANUP METHOD FOR POST-COMPACTION RESUME (2026-04-04): Use pt-fegm as the cleanup container; do not create standalone cleanup beads outside pt-fegm. Two-pass method. PASS 1 = flag only, no closures except obvious accidental duplicates if found. Start with all open non-epic beads, then epics, then decisions/process beads. For each flagged bead add structured cleanup labels plus a short evidence note. Recommended labels: cleanup:candidate, cleanup:reason:resolved-open|obsolete-context|duplicate|process-stale|unclear-scope|defer-instead, cleanup:confidence:<0.0-1.0> using one decimal place or bucket that preserves ordering. Evidence fields to include in the note when relevant: last_touched_age, has_open_children, has_parent, matches_current_repo_shape yes/no, decision_already_recorded yes/no, implementation_already_landed yes/no, active_blocker_still_real yes/no. PASS 2 = determination only after pass-1 list exists. Thresholds: 0.85+ usually act directly unless product intent is affected; 0.60-0.84 recommend strongly but may batch for user approval; 0.40-0.59 elevate to user; below 0.40 do not flag. Open should mean only: actively actionable now, intentionally deferred with explicit reason/date, or truly blocked by a still-real dependency. Main diagnosis from pre-compaction review: tracker noise is driven more by dead/half-dead migration and process beads than by missing epic containers. Epic parenting is mostly okay; stale open items are the bigger problem. Specific already-reviewed uncertain review-labeled beads at handoff: pt-477, pt-msyc, pt-6sa, pt-1nn. Current recommendations before final user instruction: pt-1nn likely obsolete; pt-6sa likely obsolete because it refers to an old repo/cutover model involving main/static fallback; pt-msyc likely no longer needed because only 4 open non-epic beads are parentless and they look intentional (pt-brx3, pt-rlxo, pt-xhwg, pt-e7wq); pt-477 and child pt-477.1 likely stale process layer, but were left for user decision because they are governance items. Known mistake to avoid from prior pass: do not create fresh standalone cleanup-triage beads when pt-fegm already exists. If a separate task is necessary, create it as a child under pt-fegm and close it promptly.


INTENDED PASS-1 LABEL SET:
- cleanup:candidate = bead is flagged for second-pass review
- cleanup:reason:resolved-open = work appears already done but bead stayed open
- cleanup:reason:obsolete-context = bead premise no longer matches current repo/product/workflow shape
- cleanup:reason:duplicate = another bead appears to cover the same live scope
- cleanup:reason:process-stale = old governance/process/container work that may no longer add value
- cleanup:reason:unclear-scope = bead is too vague or mismatched to judge without user input
- cleanup:reason:defer-instead = work may still matter someday but should probably be deferred/backlog rather than open-now

CONFIDENCE BUCKETS FOR LABELING:
- cleanup:confidence:0.9 = almost certainly stale/closeable from current evidence
- cleanup:confidence:0.8 = very likely stale/closeable, light confirmation only
- cleanup:confidence:0.7 = likely stale, but second-pass review still matters
- cleanup:confidence:0.6 = mixed evidence; probably elevate unless user has already given directional guidance
- cleanup:confidence:0.5 = true judgment call, elevate to user
Pass-1 rule: do not flag below 0.5.

PASS-1 NOTE TEMPLATE (short):
- Reason type
- Confidence
- Evidence bullets in prose: current repo match yes/no, decision already recorded yes/no, implementation already landed yes/no, open children yes/no, blocker still real yes/no
- One-sentence recommendation for pass 2: close completed / close obsolete / defer / keep open / elevate

PASS-2 ACTION RULES:
- If closed with confidence >= 0.85, remove all cleanup labels in same pass after closure
- If kept open after review, remove cleanup labels and add a note explaining why it is truly live
- If elevated to user, leave cleanup:candidate + reason + confidence labels until user decides

WORK ORDER AFTER COMPACTION:
1. Start under pt-fegm using pt-fze3 as protocol reference
2. Pass 1 on open non-epic beads first
3. Batch high-confidence candidates
4. Elevate mid-confidence items to user
5. Then repeat for epics
6. Decisions/process beads last

title: Migrate beads from server mode to embedded Dolt
description: Currently running dolt_mode=server in .beads/metadata.json which requires a TCP Dolt server process. 0.63.3 added embedded mode as default for new installs, eliminating TCP entirely. Migration may be as simple as changing dolt_mode to embedded in metadata.json if the bd binary was built with the embeddeddolt tag. Need to verify build tags and test migration path.
notes: Build check: current bd binary (0.63.3 dev) at ~/go/bin/bd has NO embeddeddolt tag — built without it (strings check shows nothing). Repo at PT_Backup/beads compiled successfully with -tags embeddeddolt. Migration requires: 1) install new binary built with embeddeddolt tag, 2) set dolt_mode=embedded in .beads/metadata.json. GetDoltMode() defaults to embedded when field is empty, but ResolveServerMode() only returns ServerModeEmbedded when dolt_mode is explicitly 'embedded'. Data migration: bd dolt push first, then after reinstall bd dolt pull into new embedded store.

title: Verify loadMoreHistory interaction with two-phase tracker_bootstrap write
description: Pre-implementation child for pt-ezlg. Confirm whether hooks/useIndexData.js loadMoreHistory also writes tracker_bootstrap, whether a new phase-1 bootstrap write after exercises+programs would interact safely with that later history write, and whether any patientId/empty-logs edge case changes the intended semantics. Scope is read-first validation of this edge case before coding the pt-ezlg fix.
notes:
Validation result before coding pt-ezlg:

- loadMoreHistory DOES already call offlineCache.cacheTrackerBootstrap(patientId, { exercises, programs, logs: mergedLogs }) in hooks/useIndexData.js:253-262.
- This does not conflict with a new phase-1 write after exercises+programs succeed because loadMoreHistory is only reachable after initial reload has already established historyHasMore/historyNextCursor.
- Semantics are compatible: phase 1 would create a minimal bootstrap {exercises, programs, logs: []}; phase 2 initial logs fetch would upgrade it with first-page logs; loadMoreHistory would later upgrade it again with merged logs.
- getCachedBootstrapSummary already treats empty logs as valid cached data when exercises or programs are non-empty (hooks/useIndexData.js:28-39), so a phase-1 bootstrap with logs: [] will still restore offline exercise/program coverage.
- PatientId handling is unchanged: all writes still key the bootstrap to the resolved patientId, and offlineCache.getCachedTrackerBootstrap(patientId) still rejects mismatched patient ids. No new race introduced beyond the existing patientId gate.

Conclusion: safe to proceed with the two-phase write in pt-ezlg.
