title: Two-phase tracker_bootstrap write: cache after exercises+programs, update after logs
description: tracker_bootstrap is only written after fetchIndexLogsPage succeeds (useIndexData.js:184). If logs fail on first load, the tracker has no offline coverage even if exercises and programs loaded. Fix: write tracker_bootstrap with exercises+programs after line 119, then update with logs at line 184. Before coding, verify: (1) does loadMoreHistory also call cacheTrackerBootstrap and does the two-phase write interact with it? (2) does hasCachedData in getCachedBootstrapSummary handle empty logs correctly? (3) any patientId race between phase 1 and phase 2 writes?
notes:
Implemented two-phase tracker_bootstrap write in hooks/useIndexData.js.

What changed:
- Preserve any cached logs restored during cache-first bootstrap in a local nextBootstrapLogs variable.
- After exercises + programs succeed, write tracker_bootstrap immediately with { exercises, programs, logs: nextBootstrapLogs } so tracker has offline exercise/program coverage even if the first logs request never completes.
- After fetchIndexLogsPage succeeds, update nextBootstrapLogs and rewrite tracker_bootstrap with the fetched logs, preserving the existing full-bootstrap behavior.

Why this is safe:
- The pre-check in pt-tfu3 confirmed loadMoreHistory already rewrites tracker_bootstrap later and remains compatible with this earlier phase-1 write.
- getCachedBootstrapSummary already treats non-empty exercises/programs with empty logs as valid cached bootstrap data.
close_reason: Implemented two-phase tracker_bootstrap write so tracker gets offline bootstrap coverage after exercises/programs and upgrades it when logs load.

title: Allow tracker route to edit prescribed dosage
description:
User request: the tracker/index route should be able to edit prescribed dosage instead of forcing all dosage edits through the program editor. This is not just a small UI tweak; it introduces a shared program-data write path from the tracker surface and must be treated as such. Scope should cover ownership, permissions, offline behavior, cache refresh, and consistency with the existing dosage editor semantics used on /program.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tracker route can open an approved dosage-edit flow for the currently relevant exercise/program context.
- Writes use the same dosage semantics and validation rules as the existing program dosage editor.
- Offline behavior, optimistic state, and cache refresh rules are defined before implementation.
- Any permission/role constraints are explicit.
- Docs are updated if route ownership or mutation patterns expand.
notes:
Legacy reference reviewed in C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\rukuba\pt\pt_tracker.html. Old tracker did support dosage writes directly from the tracker surface, but it was not a tiny inline tweak. It had dedicated modal UI (#edit-dosage-modal and #dosage-prompt-modal), tracker-owned state (ditingDosageExerciseId), modifier-aware field behavior (hide reps for duration/distance, require seconds for hold/duration, require distance for distance patterns), and direct shared-library mutation via saveEditedDosage() / importExerciseWithDosage(). The edit path updated x.current, appended a revision entry to x.history with previous/next payloads and summary text, then persisted and re-rendered the library. This is useful precedent for product behavior and field semantics, but the implementation shape should NOT be copied forward literally because it mixed tracker UI, shared program write logic, history recording, and persistence in one giant file. Use it as behavioral reference only: tracker dosage edit is a full shared-data write path with revision semantics, not just a button on the tracker card.
Implementation direction from operator discussion: preserve the useful legacy behavior but implement it through existing shared structures rather than tracker-local duplicate logic. Preferred shape is: tracker opens shared dosage UI via components/DosageModal.js for the relevant exercise/program context; actual writes flow through shared program mutation ownership (hooks/useProgramMutationActions.js + hooks/useProgramOfflineQueue.js or adjacent shared helpers), and dosage text/display continues to use lib/dosage-summary.js. Treat the old tracker file as behavioral precedent only, not structural precedent. Goal is to add tracker-side dosage edit capability without rebuilding a second dosage write stack inside the tracker route host.
CODEX WORKING. Current design/reference file lane for tracker-side dosage edit feature: C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\pttracker\app\(protected)\TrackerPage.js ; C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\pttracker\components\DosageModal.js ; C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\pttracker\hooks\useProgramMutationActions.js ; C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\pttracker\hooks\useProgramOfflineQueue.js ; C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\pttracker\lib\dosage-summary.js ; C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\pttracker\lib\pt-editor.js. Legacy behavior reference only: C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\rukuba\pt\pt_tracker.html.
Additional product/architecture context from operator discussion: this feature is another example of why framework/structure-first mattered. In a giant mixed tracker file, dosage edit looked like a small UI affordance but was actually a shared write path. Preserve that lesson in the implementation/design note: tracker-side dosage editing must be treated as shared program mutation ownership exposed through tracker, not as route-local convenience logic.
Legacy tracker behavior review from rukuba/pt/pt_tracker.html: dosage work was a true shared write path, not a tracker-local display tweak. The old tracker had both a new-dosage prompt flow and an edit-dosage flow. UI rules were: sets always shown; reps hidden for duration/distance; seconds shown for hold or duration with label switching between per-rep hold and per-set duration; distance shown only for distance exercises. The save path built a dosage spec, updated the shared program/exercise structure, recorded a revision/history entry, and surfaced summary text back in the tracker. Current repo already has the right structural pieces to recreate that behavior cleanly: components/DosageModal.js for the adaptive modal fields and hooks/useProgramMutationActions.js -> handleDosageSave for the shared optimistic/offline program write. Recommended implementation shape: TrackerPage opens DosageModal against the selected exercise + current program row, then delegates the write to shared program mutation logic rather than introducing tracker-owned dosage CRUD.
Tracker insertion-point review in current App Router code: app/(protected)/TrackerPage.js is still the right host, but only as an orchestrator. The useful existing data is already there: useIndexData provides programs; pickerExercises is already enriched with current dosage fields from each program row; and selectedExercise/session flow already feeds TimerPanel plus manual logging. Recommended integration shape for future implementation: add tracker-local state only for 'open dosage modal for selected exercise/program row', derive the current program row from programs + selectedExerciseId, render components/DosageModal from the route host, and delegate save to a shared mutation hook extracted from or aligned with hooks/useProgramMutationActions.js. That preserves the route as a wiring layer and avoids rebuilding dosage persistence logic inside TrackerPage.
Implementation pass is now in progress locally. Current behavior on repaired local dev: tracker picker shows a dosage-edit affordance for non-patient users, opens the shared DosageModal with the existing program dosage prefilled, and save updates the tracker card dosage text plus the timer panel target after reselecting the exercise. Important shared fix discovered in the process: lib/pt-editor.js updateProgram still targeted the pre-App-Router query-string endpoint (/api/programs?id=...), which returned 405 against the active app/api/programs/[id] route. I corrected that helper locally so both tracker dosage editing and the shared program-offline mutation path use the current endpoint shape. Remaining local verification before commit: one coherence pass on the new tracker dosage hook plus queue/offline behavior notes.
Local tracker dosage editing now works end-to-end in dev: picker button -> shared DosageModal -> saved dosage text updates on the card, and reselecting the exercise updates the timer-panel target. Shared fix found during implementation: lib/pt-editor.js updateProgram still used /api/programs?id=... and needed to move to /api/programs/[id]. Remaining before commit: quick pass on queue/offline behavior and final status check.
Local verification passed on http://localhost:3000/: tracker now opens shared dosage modal, online save persists to /api/programs/[id], offline dosage edit updates card immediately, queue_state entry pt_program_offline_mutations_<user> drained back to [] after returning online, hard reload still showed Long Arc Quad at 2 x 14 reps per side, and selecting the exercise showed a 14 reps timer target. Remaining step in this lane is commit/push of the isolated tracker dosage files only.
Implemented and pushed in ba50418. Tracker now exposes a dosage-edit action through ExercisePicker, opens the shared DosageModal from TrackerPage, uses the new hooks/useTrackerDosageEditor.js to overlay queued program dosage mutations and replay them through the shared program offline queue, and updates lib/pt-editor.js updateProgram to the current /api/programs/[id] route. Local browser verification on http://localhost:3000/ passed: online save persisted, offline save updated the card immediately, queue_state drained back to [], hard reload preserved Long Arc Quad at 2 x 14 reps per side, and selecting the exercise showed a 14 reps timer target.
Implemented and pushed in ba50418. Local browser verification on http://localhost:3000/ passed: online save persisted, offline save updated the card immediately, queue_state drained back to [], hard reload preserved Long Arc Quad at 2 x 14 reps per side, and selecting the exercise showed a 14 reps timer target.

title: Document what Next.js enforces vs what agents still must follow
description:
The App Router and modern Next.js structure give stronger guardrails than the old mixed Pages Router setup, but they do not fully enforce the repo's architectural rules by themselves. We need a clear operator-facing doc update that explains which boundaries the framework/build catches automatically and which ones still require repo policy and agent discipline (for example: thin orchestrator page files, layer ownership, no feature logic in route entries, and when a technically valid change is still architecturally wrong).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Repo docs explain the difference between framework-enforced structure and repo-enforced architecture rules in plain language.
- Guidance names specific examples of what build/framework catches versus what still needs agent discipline.
- Operator guidance makes it easier to know what changes still need policing during agent work.
- README and/or docs index point to the new or updated guidance if file ownership expectations change.
notes:
Expanded scope from operator discussion: the docs/update should not only explain what Next.js/App Router enforces versus what still depends on repo rules, but also evaluate which of those repo rules can be automated. Capture practical enforcement options such as pre-commit hooks, CI checks, structure-validation scripts, and possible lint/custom-rule coverage for route-host boundaries (for example: banning certain imports or logic in page/layout/route files, catching pages/ reintroduction, colocated-style expectations, file size/complexity thresholds). Also note that some architecture judgment calls will remain partly human/review-driven even after automation.
Context read from AGENTS.md + docs/NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md + docs/IMPLEMENTATION_PATTERNS.md. Current state: the repo already has strong architecture rules (thin app/page.js route entries, orchestrator-only page hosts, layer ownership, import-layer rules, size caps, shared-first patterns), but the guidance is distributed across multiple docs. This means the core rule exists, yet the operator still has to remember where enforcement ends and judgment begins. Draft direction for this bead: add one plain-language section that explicitly separates (1) framework-enforced boundaries, (2) repo-enforced rules that should become hook/script/CI checks, and (3) still-human review judgments. Candidate examples from existing docs: page.js can be mechanically checked for hooks/state/import violations; pages/ reintroduction can be mechanically checked; route-host size/cap and banned imports can be mechanically checked; 'this helper belongs in a hook not this client host' remains a judgment call even when the code is technically valid.
Operator framing to preserve: phrases like 'until later', 'if you need it', or 'we can defer that' should trigger higher scrutiny when the topic is foundational architecture or data-model shape. This bead should explicitly document that some deferred items are safe scope control, while others are expensive future-rebuild seeds (history/versioning/soft deletes/data authority/layer ownership). Include that distinction in the guidance so agents do not normalize deferring foundation work just because it is technically possible.
Potential automation follow-through once docs are written: evaluate whether this repo should add a lightweight structure-check script plus pre-commit/CI gate for App Router boundaries. Candidate mechanical checks from current docs: app/**/page.js may only export metadata/viewport and render the route host; page.js must not import hooks/lib/components directly; app/layout.js must not import route hooks/lib data functions; pages/ reintroduction should fail; route-host and shared-layer size caps can be enforced as warning/error thresholds. Keep this as a possible discovered implementation bead after the docs guidance is finalized, not an immediate silent tooling change.
CODEX WORKING. Active file lane: C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\pttracker\docs\NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md ; C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\GitHub\pttracker\docs\README.md. Current work: add plain-language guidance separating framework-enforced boundaries, repo-enforced architecture rules, future automation candidates, and still-human judgment.
Implemented and pushed in git commit 168c165. Updated docs/NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md with a new plain-language section separating framework-enforced boundaries, repo-enforced architecture rules, possible future automation checks, and still-human judgment calls. Updated docs/README.md so the docs index points readers there when they need to know what Next.js enforces versus what still needs repo rules. This pass is docs-only; no tooling/check hooks were added yet.
close_reason: Docs guidance added and pushed

title: React reference audit for PT Tracker
description:
## Summary
Review react.dev reference pages (React, React DOM, Hooks, and Components) to identify APIs or patterns that may benefit PT Tracker (performance, data loading, UX).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Notes include links to relevant react.dev reference pages.
- Notes list candidate APIs/patterns and why they might apply to PT Tracker.

## Notes
Use for investigation only; no code changes implied.
notes: React reference links: https://react.dev/reference/react ; https://react.dev/reference/react/hooks ; https://react.dev/reference/react/components ; https://react.dev/reference/react-dom ; https://react.dev/reference/react-dom/hooks ; https://react.dev/reference/react-dom/client . Candidate APIs to evaluate for PT Tracker: Suspense boundaries around slow data/route segments; useTransition/useDeferredValue for expensive list filters or tab switches; useEffectEvent for non-reactive event handlers inside effects; useId for form/label wiring; Profiler for targeted render measurement; createPortal for modal placement if needed; resource preloading APIs (prefetchDNS/preconnect) only if Next.js does not already cover a specific external endpoint.
close_reason: Captured react.dev reference links and candidate APIs for PT Tracker follow-up.

title: React reference audit for PT Tracker (components/hooks to investigate)
description: Scan react.dev/reference for components, hooks, and APIs that may help PT Tracker (Suspense, Activity, Profiler, StrictMode, startTransition/useDeferredValue, useEffectEvent, etc.). Capture which ones are relevant, which are not, and any constraints. Include direct reference links to the relevant React docs pages and a short note on why each might matter for PT Tracker.
acceptance_criteria: 1. A short list of relevant React reference pages is recorded with links. 2. Each item has a one-line note on potential usefulness or why it is not relevant. 3. Any caution notes (e.g., Suspense data-source requirements, StrictMode dev-only behaviors) are captured.
notes:
React reference audit (initial):
- https://react.dev/reference/react/StrictMode — Dev-only checks: extra re-render, re-run effects and ref callbacks, deprecated API checks; no opt-out inside the tree. Use to surface duplicate side effects and cleanup gaps in dev. 
- https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense — Fallback UI while children load; only works with Suspense-enabled data sources (framework data, lazy, or use). Caveats: state not preserved for trees that suspend before first mount; already revealed content will show fallback again unless update is a Transition or uses useDeferredValue.
- https://react.dev/reference/react/lazy — Defers component code until first render; must be wrapped in Suspense fallback. Useful for modal/panel code-splitting.
- https://react.dev/reference/react/Activity — Hide/restore UI + internal state; hidden children are display:none, effects are cleaned up, children still re-render at lower priority. Potential for tabs/panels that should preserve state but avoid side effects while hidden.
- https://react.dev/reference/react/Profiler — Programmatic render measurement; adds overhead and is disabled in production by default (profiling build required). Useful for targeted dev profiling of heavy trees.
- https://react.dev/reference/react/useEffectEvent — Separate events from effects; gives latest values without re-subscribing. Must be called from effects; not for skipping dependencies. Useful for timers/subscriptions with latest values.
- https://react.dev/reference/react/startTransition — Marks updates as non-blocking; avoids showing loading indicators; no pending flag (use useTransition for that). Async updates after await need another startTransition.
- https://react.dev/reference/react/memo — Skips re-render when props unchanged; performance optimization only (not guaranteed). Avoid if props are always new (objects/functions) unless stabilized.

Relevance for PT Tracker (summary):
- High: lazy + Suspense for modal/panel code-splitting; useEffectEvent for timer/subscription patterns; startTransition/useTransition and useDeferredValue for expensive UI updates.
- Medium: Profiler for targeted dev-only performance measurement.
- Situational: Activity for tab/panel state preservation without background side effects (needs careful effect cleanup). StrictMode for dev-only bug surfacing in subscriptions and offline warmers.
close_reason: React reference audit notes captured; closing.

title: Eliminate redundant getUser() calls on every protected page load
description: Every protected page load currently fires 3 separate Supabase auth checks: (1) proxy.js calls supabase.auth.getUser() on every request, (2) app/(protected)/layout.js calls supabase.auth.getUser() again on every protected page, (3) hooks/useAuth.js mounts and calls getSession() + getUser() on the client. The proxy and layout checks are both server-side and both hit the Supabase auth API — that is one redundant round-trip per page load. The client useAuth check is a third call. Decide whether the proxy getUser() can be skipped or replaced with a cookie existence check, and whether layout.js can consume the session the proxy already validated rather than re-fetching it.
notes: ROOT CAUSE CONFIRMED via docs review: proxy.js was calling getUser() (network round-trip to Supabase auth server on every request) when getClaims() (local JWT validation, triggers refresh without network call) is what Supabase recommends. getUser() in layout.js is correct and intentional — it is the actual security gate. The proxy and layout calls are NOT redundant — they serve different purposes: proxy=token refresh only, layout=identity verification. FIX: proxy.js line 41 changed from supabase.auth.getUser() to supabase.auth.getClaims().
close_reason: Root cause was specifically proxy.js using getUser() instead of getClaims(). Fixed: proxy.js now calls getClaims() (local JWT, no network round-trip). The proxy + layout dual call is intentional per Supabase SSR docs — they serve different purposes (cookie refresh vs identity verification) and cannot be collapsed. Auth call hierarchy documented in docs/SYSTEM_ARCHITECTURE.md.

title: Replace per-request getUser(token) network call in lib/auth.js with local JWT verification
description: lib/auth.js authenticateRequest() calls supabase.auth.getUser(token) on every single API request (logs, users, roles, reference-data, vocab). This is a live network round-trip to Supabase auth servers to validate the JWT — it adds latency to every API call the tracker makes. Supabase JWTs are signed RS256/HS256 and can be verified locally using the JWT secret without a network call. Replace getUser(token) with local verification (e.g. jose or jsonwebtoken against SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET), then load the user row from the DB as today. This removes the Supabase auth round-trip from every API hit.
notes: Source evidence: lib/auth.js line 33 — supabase.auth.getUser(token) called inside authenticateRequest(), which wraps every handler in api/logs.js, api/users.js, api/roles.js, api/reference-data.js, api/vocab.js. Each API response the tracker makes (logs fetch, users fetch, reference data, etc.) triggers this call. On the tracker index page alone, bootstrap fires at least 3-4 parallel API calls, each incurring this round-trip. Supabase JWT secret is available as SUPABASE_JWT_SECRET env var in Vercel.
close_reason: Supabase docs explicitly recommend keeping getUser() in API routes that protect user data — it is the only call that detects server-side session revocation (banned user, manual session deletion, signout from another device). Local JWT verification (getClaims() or jose) cannot detect revocation. The per-request network cost is the correct tradeoff for API security. Closing as won't-fix per upstream recommendation.

title: Joint decision: SSR auth migration scope and timeline
description:
Decision 2 from pt-679e (server/client boundary) is provisionally NOT NOW, blocked on whether SSR auth via @supabase/ssr is a realistic initiative.

Both Claude and Codex need to weigh in jointly before this is settled:
1. Is SSR auth intentionally out of scope for the current pass, or a near-term initiative?
2. If auth moves server-side, what does the migration look like and who owns it?
3. Does Codex's rehab finding (components are server-ready, auth is the only blocker) change priority?
4. Should this be a separate epic or folded into the cutover track?

This is a user + dual-agent decision. Do not act on server/client boundary work until this is answered.
notes:
Rationale: Decision 2 in pt-679e cannot be finalized unilaterally. Codex found that rehab components are already server-ready but auth is the blocker — that changes the complexity estimate for SSR migration. User needs to decide scope before agents proceed.\n\nAlternatives Considered:\n- Mark as NOT NOW permanently: rejected — ignores Codex's finding that components are already compatible\n- Mark as DO NOW: rejected — SSR auth migration is non-trivial and needs explicit scoping\n- Provisional NOT NOW pending joint review: accepted — correct posture until both agents align\n\nBlocks: any server/client boundary work beyond current thin-shell pattern
CLAUDE QUESTIONS FOR CODEX (re: items 17 and 18 from pt-679e):

Item 17 — 'bounded auth-boundary improvement now':
Flat No is too broad — agreed. But 'bounded improvement' needs to be concrete before we record it as a decision. What specifically are you proposing?
Options I can see without full SSR auth migration:
- Auth check in middleware using existing JWT (no cookie migration needed — middleware can read a cookie that carries the JWT)
- Auth gating at App Router layout level using existing client session
Which of these, or something else? Without a concrete proposal we cannot scope, assign, or decide it.

Item 18 — 'server-only data assembly: case-by-case':
Splitting middleware/auth (No) from server-only data assembly (case-by-case) is correct in principle. But every user-facing surface in this app fetches auth-gated data requiring the access token. What specific surface do you have in mind that could assemble data server-side without it?
Rehab coverage was the pilot candidate from pt-sjli.18.2 — but your own analysis concluded auth is the blocker there too. If there is a surface I am missing, name it. Otherwise 'case-by-case' has no current cases and should be recorded as No now with an explicit reactivation trigger.

Both questions need concrete answers before pt-679e items 17 and 18 can be recorded as final decisions.
Codex response to Claude questions:\n\nItem 17 — concrete bounded auth-boundary proposal now:\nI am NOT proposing middleware auth or SSR auth migration in this pass. I am proposing a route/layout ownership change inside App Router that keeps auth client-side but moves the gating boundary higher and makes it consistent. Concretely: create one canonical protected-app boundary for the authenticated routes (/, /program, /pt-view, /rehab) using an App Router route group and shared client AuthGate/AppShell component at the protected layout level, with reset-password remaining in a public route group unless/until migrated. That means auth stays IndexedDB/client-session based, but route protection and loading/redirect ownership stop being per-page drift and become one explicit App Router boundary. Scope is bounded because it does not require cookie migration, SSR data fetching, or mutation/auth rewrites. This is the 'yes now' change I mean.\n\nItem 18 — server-only data assembly:\nI agree there is no strong current user-data case that bypasses the auth blocker today. So I would narrow my earlier phrasing: server-only data assembly is NO NOW for current auth-gated route data, with an explicit reactivation trigger instead of an open-ended maybe. Reactivation trigger: if SSR auth/cookie-readable session becomes an approved initiative, or if we identify a surface whose first render depends only on non-user-specific or already-public data. Until then, do not count this as an active current-pass tool.\n\nSo my refined position is:\n- Full auth-architecture shift / SSR auth migration: No now\n- Bounded App Router auth-boundary standardization using a protected client layout/group: Yes now\n- Server-only data assembly for auth-gated route data: No now, reactivate only if SSR auth or a real public-data case appears
Codex final position, stated plainly: I am not comfortable blessing the current client-only auth shape as the settled long-term foundation if the goal is to avoid any fifth major revisit after cut-over. From a clean design-stage perspective, the better architecture is hybrid: server-readable cookie auth boundary plus client-side offline storage/cache/queue. The fact that PT Tracker is a PWA does NOT by itself prove client-only auth is the better final architecture; many PWAs use server-readable first-party cookies for auth/session boundary while still using IndexedDB for offline data. Supabase SSR docs explicitly require cookies if the server is to read session state; local/IndexedDB auth is not server-readable. So the real trade is not 'cookies are more stable' versus 'IndexedDB is more stable' — it is whether we want the server/layout to participate in auth boundary decisions. If we choose not to do SSR/hybrid auth now, that should be recorded as an explicit compromise made to preserve the current implementation shape, not as the stronger best-practice architecture. Under the user's stated goal ('pre-cutover, want to be done, no more major work after this'), I think the hybrid auth boundary deserves serious now-consideration rather than a comfortable 'not now' deferral.
USER DECISION — HYBRID AUTH FOUNDATION: DO NOW (2026-03-29).

Lay the SSR-capable auth foundation before cutover:
- Migrate to cookie-readable session (@supabase/ssr: createServerClient + createBrowserClient)
- Server can read auth state — full SSR is available when wanted
- Then choose selectively what runs server-side vs. client-side

Hybrid model:
- SSR foundation: enabled (cookie session, server-readable auth)
- Auth gating / calmer data surfaces: can move server-side where it helps
- Offline queue, reconnect recovery, IndexedDB cache, interaction-heavy surfaces: stay client-side where that is the better fit

This is NOT fully client-only auth (blocks server participation) and NOT fully server-first (wrong fit for a PWA with offline requirements).

This resolves pt-pseg. Creates a new implementation epic.
Also resolves:
- pt-679e Decision 2 (server/client boundary): revised to DO NOW — lay foundation
- pt-679e item 17: protected route group YES, and now properly grounded in SSR-capable auth
- pt-679e item 18: server-only data assembly on calmer surfaces becomes available after foundation lands
- pt-sjli.18.2 (Codex rehab server-side pilot): now has the auth foundation it was missing
- pt-0x8o (bundle investigation): @supabase/ssr is more modular than @supabase/supabase-js — may help framework floor
Reviewed 2026-04-04. Closing as completed. The bead body records the user decision from 2026-03-29 to do the hybrid auth foundation, and the implementation epic pt-8whf is already closed as complete. This decision bead was resolved but left in an inconsistent open/in_progress state.
close_reason: Completed; user decision recorded and implementation epic pt-8whf finished.

title: Apply startTransition and useDeferredValue in TrackerPage
description:
Apply two targeted concurrent rendering improvements in TrackerPage.js:
1. Wrap setActiveTab calls in startTransition — tab switch to history is non-urgent but triggers expensive HistoryPanel render over 454+ logs.
2. Apply useDeferredValue on allLogs in the pickerPrograms useMemo — getAdherenceBadgeState runs for every exercise against full log history on every render; deferring prevents badge recalculation from blocking picker responsiveness.
Both are one-line changes. No architectural impact.
notes:

- allLogs passed through useDeferredValue before pickerPrograms useMemo

- Verified on preview: tab switch feels immediate, no regression on badge display

title: Estimate Pages-to-App migration from actual route files
description: Read the current Next.js Pages Router route files in this repo and estimate the effort to migrate them to the App Router. Base the estimate on actual file shape, current orchestrator discipline, likely client/server boundaries, and route-specific migration risk. No code changes unless explicitly requested.
notes:
2026-03-28 inspection summary: pages/index.js (477L), pages/pt-view.js (216L), pages/rehab.js (194L), and pages/program.js (320L) are all visibly orchestrator-style route files with hooks/components/lib split out. None of the four use getServerSideProps/getStaticProps or next/router, which lowers App Router migration friction. Main migration costs now look concentrated in route shell conversion, Head/layout/metadata replacement, moving service-worker registration from pages/_app.js into an App Router client boundary, and preserving highly interactive client behavior on index/program. Estimated route risk: rehab low-medium, pt-view medium, program medium, index medium-high. Overall this looks like a controlled migration project rather than another ground-up rebuild.
2026-03-28 estimate: read pages/index.js (442L), pt-view.js (193L), rehab.js (181L), program.js (304L), plus _app.js and _document.js. Pages are genuinely thinner than a typical Pages Router app and there is no next/router or getServerSideProps/getStaticProps coupling. App Router migration would mainly be route moves, metadata/layout conversion, and client-boundary decisions. Index and program remain medium-high because they are still highly interactive client surfaces; pt-view is medium; rehab is low-medium and is the best pilot route.
2026-03-28 estimate summary: reviewed current pages routes and shared shell. pages/index.js (477 lines) is still the highest-risk App Router migration because it orchestrates the densest interactive flow, but it is already thin relative to its behavior load. pages/program.js (320), pages/pt-view.js (216), and pages/rehab.js (194) are all migration-friendly because orchestration has been pushed into hooks/components/lib. No getServerSideProps/getStaticProps or next/router usage found in the route set. Main migration work is shell conversion (_app/_document, next/head replacement, auth/layout decisions, and regression verification), not route cleanup.
close_reason: Completed: read the current Pages Router route files and produced a route-specific App Router migration estimate based on actual file shape, thin-page orchestration, shell conversion work, and likely client-boundary pressure.

title: Resolve bd dolt pull failure after pttracker migration
description:
Source Reference
- discovered after pt-umxi tracker cleanup on 2026-03-28

Status
- open

Priority
- 2

Risk
- Medium: failed Dolt pulls can leave tracker sync one-way and cause metadata drift between clones.

Scope
- Diagnose and resolve why bd dolt pull fails in pttracker after the Dolt origin was repointed.

Agent Owner
- codex

Agent Eligible
- codex,claude

Tags
- beads,dolt,tracker,sync,migration

File Scope
- .beads metadata/config
- Dolt remote state

Issue
- bd dolt push succeeds, but bd dolt pull still fails after migration from rukuba to pttracker.

Context
- The tracker was moved into pttracker and the Dolt origin was updated from rukuba to pttracker. A follow-up failure remains on pull and needs a non-destructive sync repair.

Constraints/Caveats
- No app code changes
- Prefer metadata or remote-state repair over deleting tracker data
- Keep history intact

Validation Checklist
- [ ] bd dolt pull succeeds
- [ ] bd dolt push still succeeds
- [ ] bd doctor remains at 0 errors

Dependencies
- discovered-from: pt-umxi
- blocks: n/a

Reactivation Trigger
- Reopen if Dolt pull fails again after the sync state is repaired.
notes: Reproduced bd dolt pull failure. Root cause is not remote mismatch anymore; Dolt pull is blocked by one uncommitted Beads config change in the local working set ('cannot merge with uncommitted changes'). Plan: commit the pending tracker config state, then retry pull/push and doctor.
close_reason: Problem: bd dolt pull failed after the pttracker migration even after the remote was repointed. Root Cause: the Dolt working set still had an uncommitted config row (kv.memory.browser-automation-mcp-guide-1-vercel-mcp-mcp), so pull aborted with 'cannot merge with uncommitted changes'. Change Made: diagnosed the exact dirty config row, committed the pending config state in Dolt, synced origin/main to the new pttracker remote, and verified both Beads wrapper commands and raw Dolt commands succeed. Files Touched: Beads/Dolt config state only. Validation: bd dolt pull succeeds; bd dolt push succeeds; bd doctor returns 0 errors. Follow-ups: the custom status value browser_verify:active still triggers a config warning and can be normalized separately.

title: Replace archived boolean reads with lifecycle_status throughout codebase
description:
Source Reference: pt-t1qr (epic)
Status: open
Priority: P2
Risk: Low — JS read path changes only, no schema migration, no data loss
Scope: pt-rebuild JS files only; DB column left intact
Agent Owner: claude
Agent Eligible: claude
Tags: exercise,lifecycle,archived,refactor
File Scope: to be confirmed by pt-t1qr.1 (search bead) before implementation

Issue:
Archived exercises appear in the exercise list because filtering code reads exercise.archived (boolean) but the Next.js archive action only writes lifecycle_status = 'archived'. The boolean is never updated, so archived exercises still surface in lists and selectors.

Context:
archived is a legacy boolean column. lifecycle_status is the canonical field. The codebase should read only from lifecycle_status everywhere. A sync hack exists in the updateExercise handler to keep the two in sync — remove it. Do NOT implement until pt-t1qr.1 (search) has added its inventory as a note on this bead.

Constraints/Caveats:
- Do NOT start implementation until pt-t1qr.1 notes are present on this bead
- Do NOT drop the archived column from the DB — leave for pt-v4k4
- Do NOT use bd edit — use bd update flags only
- Read actual file contents before every change — no line numbers from memory
- Follow layer rules in AGENTS.md before touching any file

Validation Checklist:
- [ ] Archived exercises do NOT appear in list when show archived is off
- [ ] Archived exercises DO appear when show archived is on
- [ ] Archived label still shows correctly in dropdowns
- [ ] Creating a new exercise still works
- [ ] npm run build passes with no new errors

Reactivation Trigger: pt-t1qr.1 closed with inventory note added here
notes:
Exercise archived/lifecycle_status inventory for scoped .js files in pt-rebuild/.
Search scope: all .js files, excluding node_modules, .next, .serena, and db/migrate.js.
Method: direct exercise-field references only. Excluded unrelated message archival usage and comment/UI-copy-only mentions.

Live exercise-field hits
- api/roles.js:27 — reads archived — `archived` included in `exercises!inner (...)` select list.
- api/roles.js:31 — reads archived — `.eq('exercises.archived', false)` filters out archived exercises.
- components/ExerciseForm.js:11 — writes archived — initializes new exercise form state with `archived: false`.
- components/ExerciseForm.js:57 — reads archived — seeds edit form state from `exercise.archived`.
- components/ExerciseForm.js:120 — writes lifecycle_status — sends `lifecycle.status` in save payload as `lifecycle_status`.
- components/ExerciseRolesWorkspace.js:40 — reads archived — labels archived exercises in selector options.
- components/ProgramDosageWorkspace.js:46 — reads archived — labels archived exercises in selector options.
- components/ProgramExerciseSelector.js:40 — reads archived — labels archived exercises in selector options.
- hooks/useProgramWorkspaceState.js:7 — reads archived — filters archived exercises out when `showArchived` is off.
- hooks/useProgramWorkspaceState.js:16 — reads archived — hides archived exercises from workspace options unless already selected.
- hooks/usePtViewData.js:61 — reads archived — filters cached programs whose joined `exercises` row is archived.
- hooks/usePtViewData.js:82 — reads archived — same filter on freshly fetched programs.
- lib/exercise-sort.js:88 — reads archived — excludes archived exercises from visible ordered results.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:90 — reads archived — copies DB row field `ex.archived` into assembled exercise object.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:91 — reads lifecycle_status — checks `ex.lifecycle_status` when building normalized lifecycle object.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:92 — reads lifecycle_status — maps DB field to `lifecycle.status`.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:236 — reads lifecycle_status — validates incoming payload field.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:346 — reads archived — destructures incoming create payload with default `false`.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:353 — reads lifecycle_status — destructures incoming create payload with default `null`.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:399 — writes archived — inserts `archived` into `exercises` row.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:400 — writes lifecycle_status — inserts `lifecycle_status` into `exercises` row.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:528 — reads archived — destructures incoming update payload.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:535 — reads lifecycle_status — destructures incoming update payload.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:583 — writes archived — applies direct archived update to DB update object.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:584 — reads lifecycle_status — branches on presence of update payload field.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:585 — writes lifecycle_status — applies lifecycle status to DB update object.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:587 — writes archived — derives archived boolean from lifecycle status during update.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:760 — writes archived — soft-delete path updates exercise to `archived: true`.
- lib/handlers/programs.js:200 — reads archived — includes joined exercise `archived` field in program query.
- lib/program-optimistic.js:19 — reads archived — builds optimistic exercise object from payload field.
- lib/program-optimistic.js:26 — reads lifecycle_status — checks payload field when building optimistic lifecycle object.
- lib/program-optimistic.js:27 — reads lifecycle_status — maps payload field to optimistic `lifecycle.status`.
- lib/pt-view.js:171 — reads archived — skips overdue calculations for programs whose joined exercise is archived.

Excluded as non-operational / not direct exercise-field reads-writes
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:37 — comment mentions `lifecycle_status`.
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:38 — lifecycle status vocabulary includes `'archived'`, but this is not archived-boolean field access.
- components/ExerciseFormCore.js:85 — JSDoc mentions `archived`.
- components/ExerciseFormLifecycle.js:7 — lifecycle status option list includes `'archived'`.
- components/ExerciseFormLifecycle.js:56 — UI copy says `archived`.

Summary counts
- reads archived: 17
- writes archived: 6
- reads lifecycle_status: 8
- writes lifecycle_status: 3

Cross-check result
- Independent subagent pass matched this inventory with no extra live exercise-field hits.
DIRECTION DECISION (pending pt-t1qr.1 results): Preferred approach is a Supabase DB trigger, NOT a JS codebase sweep. Trigger: BEFORE UPDATE on exercises, sets archived = (lifecycle_status = 'archived'). Rationale: (1) minimal code changes, (2) boolean index stays efficient for read-heavy app (app drains ~0.4%/min battery — keep reads fast), (3) .eq() on boolean beats .neq() on string for query planner, (4) trigger fires only on writes (archiving is rare). Wait for pt-t1qr.1 search results before implementing — need to confirm no code is writing archived directly in a way that would conflict with the trigger. Also need to confirm how deprecated exercises are filtered (suspected lifecycle_status filter already exists).

title: Replace archived boolean with lifecycle_status throughout codebase
description:
**Source Reference:** pt-hzij
**Status:** open
**Priority:** 2 (medium)
**Risk:** Low — read path change only, no schema migration, no data loss
**Scope:** pt-rebuild JS files only; DB column left intact
**Agent Owner:** codex

**File Scope:** api/roles.js, components/ExerciseForm.js, components/ExerciseRolesWorkspace.js, components/ProgramDosageWorkspace.js, components/ProgramExerciseSelector.js, hooks/useProgramWorkspaceState.js, hooks/usePtViewData.js, lib/exercise-sort.js, lib/handlers/exercises.js, lib/handlers/programs.js, lib/program-optimistic.js, lib/pt-view.js

**Issue:**
Archived exercises appear in the exercise list because filtering code reads exercise.archived (boolean) but the Next.js archive action only writes lifecycle_status = 'archived' — the boolean is never updated.

**Context:**
archived is a legacy boolean column. lifecycle_status is the canonical field. The codebase should read only from lifecycle_status everywhere. A sync hack was added to updateExercise in lib/handlers/exercises.js (lines 586-587) — remove it.

**Constraints/Caveats:**
- Do NOT drop the archived column from the DB — leave for a separate migration
- Do NOT use bd edit — use bd update flags only
- Follow layer rules in AGENTS.md before writing to any file
- Read both local MEMORY.md files before starting (C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\claude-memory\MEMORY.md and C:\Users\cindi\OneDrive\Documents\PT_Backup\agent_memory\MEMORY.md)

**Required Changes:**
- api/roles.js:31 — .eq('exercises.archived', false) to .neq('exercises.lifecycle_status', 'archived')
- components/ExerciseForm.js:11,57 — remove archived from initial state and form population
- components/ExerciseRolesWorkspace.js:40, ProgramDosageWorkspace.js:46, ProgramExerciseSelector.js:40 — exercise.archived to exercise.lifecycle_status === 'archived'
- hooks/useProgramWorkspaceState.js:7,16 — same
- hooks/usePtViewData.js:61,82 — p.exercises?.archived (exercises is an array — inspect actual shape first)
- lib/exercise-sort.js:88 — exercise?.archived to exercise?.lifecycle_status === 'archived'

- lib/handlers/exercises.js:346,399 — remove archived from createExercise destructure and DB insert
- lib/handlers/exercises.js:528,583,586-587 — remove archived from updateExercise, remove sync hack

- lib/handlers/programs.js:200 — check if archived is exercise-related; fix if so

- lib/pt-view.js:171 — program.exercises?.archived to lifecycle_status check

**Validation Checklist:**

- [ ] Archived exercises DO appear when show archived is on

- [ ] Creating a new exercise still works

title: Stop using archived boolean — migrate all exercise archive logic to lifecycle_status
description:
The `archived` boolean column on the `exercises` table is redundant with `lifecycle_status`. Archiving an exercise sets `lifecycle_status = 'archived'` but the boolean is not always written consistently. All read paths (filters, display labels, sort) still check `exercise.archived`, so exercises archived via lifecycle_status alone still appear in lists.

The fix is to stop treating `archived` as the source of truth. All filtering and display logic should check `lifecycle_status === 'archived'`, and all write paths should stop setting the `archived` boolean (or only set `lifecycle_status`). The boolean column can be left in the DB for now and dropped in a follow-up migration.

**Affected files:**
- `api/roles.js:31` — `.eq('exercises.archived', false)` → `.neq('exercises.lifecycle_status', 'archived')`
- `components/ExerciseForm.js:11,57` — remove `archived` from initial state
- `components/ExerciseRolesWorkspace.js:40` — label check
- `components/ProgramDosageWorkspace.js:46` — label check
- `components/ProgramExerciseSelector.js:40` — label check
- `hooks/useProgramWorkspaceState.js:7,16` — filter logic
- `hooks/usePtViewData.js:61,82` — filter logic (also verify `p.exercises?.archived` is correct — exercises is an array)
- `lib/exercise-sort.js:88` — filter logic
- `lib/handlers/exercises.js:90,399,528,583,587,760` — read shape, insert, update, delete handlers (remove archived boolean writes; remove sync hack at 586-588)
- `lib/program-optimistic.js:19` — remove `archived` from optimistic shape
- `lib/pt-view.js:171` — filter logic

**Do not change** `db/migrate.js` — historical migration script.
acceptance_criteria:
- Archiving an exercise via lifecycle_status hides it from all exercise lists and selectors
- No code path writes to the `archived` boolean column
- The `archived` boolean sync block in handlers/exercises.js updateExercise is removed
- All display labels use lifecycle_status check
- The `archived` field is removed from ExerciseForm initial state

title: Clarify per-side versus total dosage wording in program and tracker summaries
description:
Source Reference: pt-ykxr
Status: open

Risk: medium

Issue:
Dosage summaries such as 4 x 20 ft and 2 x 10 reps are currently ambiguous about whether the set count is per side or total. For sided exercises, the wording should reflect that sets are per side; for non-sided exercises, the wording should remain total.
Context:
User clarified the canonical rule: SxR (side) means S sets per side, while SxR (both) means S total sets. The same rule applies when distance replaces reps. Existing tracker/program summary strings show raw sets x target without saying whether the value is per side or total, which can mislead users reviewing dosage or logged-set summaries.
Constraints/Caveats:
- Keep the wording concise and readable on mobile
- Do not change the underlying stored dosage values while fixing labels
- Use the same terminology consistently across tracker and program surfaces
Validation Checklist:
- [ ] Sided dosage summaries explicitly indicate per-side semantics where needed
- [ ] Non-sided summaries continue to read as total sets
- [ ] Distance-based and rep-based summaries use consistent wording
- [ ] Compact/mobile layouts remain readable

- discovered-from: pt-ykxr
- blocks: n/a

notes:

Program dosage summary does the same at pt-rebuild/components/ProgramDosageWorkspace.js:6-14. Next-set confirmation renders distance summary at pt-rebuild/components/NextSetConfirmModal.js:3-16 and side separately at pt-rebuild/components/NextSetConfirmModal.js:67-68, which may still leave dosage semantics ambiguous. History/list surfaces also show distance and side as separate tokens rather than a combined semantic summary at pt-rebuild/components/HistoryList.js:23-24 and pt-rebuild/components/ExerciseHistoryModal.js:43-44.
Implemented shared prescribed-dosage formatter in pt-rebuild/lib/dosage-summary.js and routed ExercisePicker plus ProgramDosageWorkspace through it. New wording uses canonical grammar: S x R reps, S x R reps x T sec hold, S x T sec, S x D ft, with 'per side' appended when pattern === 'side'. Scan confirmed no hidden third prescribed-dosage builder on the Next.js side; NextSetConfirmModal, HistoryList, ExerciseHistoryModal, and lib/timer-panel.js format logged sets or live targets rather than prescribed dosage summaries.

title: Decide how deleted pt_category values affect assigned exercises
description:
## Summary
Define what should happen when a pt_category is deleted while exercises are still assigned to it.

## Decision
Pending. This bead exists to capture and resolve the decision before implementation.

## Rationale
Deleting an in-use pt_category could leave active exercises in an invalid or confusing state, so the allowed behavior needs to be defined intentionally.

## Alternatives Considered
- Prohibit deletion while active exercises are assigned
- Require reassignment before delete
- Allow deletion but bulk-reassign affected exercises during the flow

## Acceptance Criteria
- The allowed delete behavior is explicitly decided
- Required safeguards or reassignment flow is documented

## Notes
Requested as child 2.2.2 under the vocab editor deletion-behavior parent.

Issue:
Evaluate and, if approved, add a first-class erification issue type for this repo so verification-only work is tracked distinctly from implementation, bug-fix, and maintenance work.
Context:
Open and recently closed beads show a meaningful cluster of verification-shaped work: parity checks, browser verification, review-only checks, and acceptance validation. The repo already uses  rowser_verify as a status, but this bead covers whether the issue type system should also distinguish verification work.
Constraints/Caveats:
- Do not duplicate what is already captured by status alone unless the added type improves issue creation and triage clarity
- Keep the type system small
- Compare a custom erification type against deliberate use of existing built-in types before changing config
## Acceptance Criteria
- Verification-shaped issue examples are reviewed against the current type system
- A recommendation is made for or against adding erification as a custom type
- If approved, repo config and guidance are updated consistently
- If declined, the docs clarify how verification work should be typed instead

- [ ] A recommendation is made for or against adding erification as a custom type
- [ ] If approved, repo config and guidance are updated consistently
- [ ] If declined, the docs clarify how verification work should be typed instead

title: Extract /program page state into orchestrator-safe hooks
description:
Source Reference: program.js orchestrator review 2026-03-22
Status: open
Priority: P2
Risk: low
Scope: current
Agent Owner: codex
Agent Eligible: codex,claude
Tags: nextjs,structure,refactor,program,orchestrator
File Scope:
- pt-rebuild/pages/program.js
- pt-rebuild/hooks/useProgramPageData.js
- pt-rebuild/hooks/useProgramPageState.js
- pt-rebuild/README.md

Issue:
pages/program.js is over the page hard cap and currently owns non-orchestrator bootstrap, filtering, snapshot, and mutation-wrapper state that should live in hooks.

Context:
AGENTS.md and NEXTJS_CODE_STRUCTURE.md now treat page files as pure orchestrators with no exception for files under cap. Current program.js is 567 lines and mixes route wiring with program bootstrap loading, offline snapshot persistence, derived filtering, and wrapper handlers around mutation hooks.

Constraints/Caveats:
- Refactor only; do not change /program behavior
- Keep page-level auth guard and route composition in pages/program.js
- Extract state/effects into hooks, not lib, because the logic is stateful and page-specific
- Update pt-rebuild/README.md for any new shared hook ownership
- Prefer Vercel/deploy signal for routine validation; use local build only if needed

Validation Checklist:
- [ ] pages/program.js reduced under the 500L hard cap
- [ ] Non-orchestrator bootstrap/state logic moved out of the page into cohesive hooks
- [ ] Existing /program behavior remains unchanged
- [ ] README updated with new hook ownership notes
- [ ] Validation run and recorded

Dependencies:
- discovered-from: n/a
- blocks: n/a

Reactivation Trigger:
n/a
notes:
Relevant decomposition guidance copied from pt-ps9 on 2026-03-22:
- Do not use page size alone as the stopping rule; use ownership clarity for future agent maintenance as the bar.
- Keep the page focused on auth/use* hook composition, route-level modal state, and passing props to child components.
- Prefer scoped hooks/components over leaving mixed logic in the page file.
- Preserve current offline fallback behavior while extracting bootstrap/state concerns.
- Update README ownership notes in the same change so future agents know where the extracted /program concerns live.
- Treat this as an AI-manageable ownership-boundary refactor, not just a shrink-the-file pass.
Implementation plan for pt-21o as of 2026-03-22:
1. Extract hooks/useProgramPageData.js for /program bootstrap and offline data ownership.
   - Move out of pages/program.js: applyBootstrap(), loadData(), offline cache hydration/fallback, persistProgramSnapshot(), commitProgramSnapshot(), access/load/offline state, snapshot refs/getCurrentSnapshot(), and the session-driven bootstrap effects.
   - Keep behavior unchanged: therapist/admin access gate, patient-scoped user resolution, cached fallback notice, cache writes, and sign-out state clearing.
2. Extract hooks/useProgramPageState.js for /program workspace state ownership.
   - Move out of pages/program.js: search/showArchived/roleSearch/dosageSearch, activeExercise/roleExerciseId/dosageExerciseId/dosageTarget, rolesLoading/vocabSaving, derived filtered option lists, formExercise/roleExercise/dosageExercise/selectedProgram, and wrapper handlers around useProgramMutationActions/useProgramVocabActions.
   - Keep behavior unchanged: selecting an exercise opens the right form, successful save updates role/dosage selectors, dosage modal target closes on save, and role/vocab saving flags still bracket async work.
3. Reduce pages/program.js to route orchestration only.
   - Keep in page: auth guard, NavMenu route shell, calling the extracted hooks, wiring queue banner + child components, and passing props.
   - Do not leave bootstrap logic, filtering logic, snapshot persistence, or mutation-wrapper state in the page.
4. Update pt-rebuild/README.md.
   - Add entries for any new /program hooks with ownership guidance so future agents know where bootstrap/offline logic and page-state/mutation wiring now live.
5. Validate after refactor.
   - Prefer Vercel signal if available; otherwise run local build only if needed.
   - Explicit confidence review pass: verify the queue banner, New button, selector filtering, ExerciseForm open/edit flow, roles workspace, dosage workspace, vocab editor, and DosageModal wiring all remain present in pages/program.js after extraction.
Target outcome:
- pages/program.js under the 500L hard cap and functioning as a true orchestrator.
- New hooks stay at or under the 150L hook cap; if one exceeds cap, split again rather than pushing logic back into the page.
Progress update 2026-03-22:
- Extracted hooks/useProgramPageData.js for /program bootstrap, access gating, online load, and cached offline fallback.
- Extracted hooks/useProgramDataSnapshot.js for cached editor-data snapshot writes.
- Extracted hooks/useProgramWorkspaceState.js for workspace selection/search/modal state and derived option lists.
- Extracted hooks/useProgramMutationUi.js for mutation loading flags and UI-facing wrapper handlers.
- Reduced pages/program.js from 567 lines to 303 lines; page now mainly composes auth, hooks, route shell, queue banner, and child components.
- Updated README hook ownership notes.
- Validation: npm run build passed.
Problem:
program.js was 567 lines and violated the page-orchestrator rule by owning bootstrap logic, cached fallback, workspace state, derived filters, and mutation UI wrapper state.

Root Cause:
The /program route had accumulated multiple independently changeable concerns in one page file, which exceeded both the page hard cap and the ownership boundary expected for AI-maintained pages.

Change Made:
Reduced pages/program.js to 303 lines by extracting useProgramPageData, useProgramDataSnapshot, useProgramWorkspaceState, and useProgramMutationUi, while preserving the existing /program UI composition and behavior.

Files Touched:
- pages/program.js
- hooks/useProgramPageData.js
- hooks/useProgramDataSnapshot.js
- hooks/useProgramWorkspaceState.js
- hooks/useProgramMutationUi.js

Validation:
- npm run build
- Verified pages/program.js reduced from 567 lines to 303 lines
- Verified new hooks are each within the 150-line hard cap
- Reviewed the diff to confirm queue banner, New button, selector flow, ExerciseForm, roles workspace, dosage workspace, vocab editor, and DosageModal wiring remained present

Follow-ups:
- none
close_reason: Completed

title: Keep active side on selected side after Log Set save
description:

Issue:
When logging a sided exercise through Log Set, the tracker does not preserve the currently selected side after a save. In Playwright preview verification, Long Arc Quad was logged once on right, then the main tracker side was switched to left, Log Set opened with Side=Left as expected, and saving that left-side set returned to the panel showing Working right side instead of staying on left. Context:
This was found while verifying pt-spg.4 against the deployed nextjs preview. Existing side behavior that does work: side controls render; switching to left updates the panel label; Log Set modal inherits the current tracker side; manual log saves use modal-local side for the saved set. The remaining bug is persistence of active side context after the modal save completes.
Constraints/Caveats:
Do not change bilateral null-side semantics while fixing this. Preserve the existing correct modal-local side save behavior.
Validation Checklist:
- [ ] After saving a left-side Log Set, the panel remains on left
- [ ] After saving a right-side Log Set, the panel remains on right

notes: 2026-03-22 Playwright verification started on https://pt-rehab-git-nextjs-pt-tracker.vercel.app using test-patient@pttracker.app. Result so far: FAIL for Log Set path. For Long Arc Quad, switching to left and opening Log Set preserved Side=Left in the modal, but after saving the left-side set the panel snapped back to Working right side. Local next build passes. Awaiting preview verification for Log Set and Next Set flows before closure.

Issue:
Strengthen the documented Beads workflow so agents close work promptly and manually, then reconcile stale-open parity and verification beads that already have enough evidence to close or narrow.

Context:
Open beads have been lingering after work was already done, especially verification-only items. That leads to repeated verification work, unclear current state, and extra user cost. The docs need a harder rule, and the current tracker state needs a reconciliation pass so open beads reflect real remaining work instead of stale history.

Constraints/Caveats:
- Manual bead closure is required in the current Dolt workflow; commits do not auto-close issues.
- The rule is close when the scoped work is done, not a blanket slogan about commits.
- Verification-only beads may have no commit and still must close immediately when they pass.
- Only close or narrow beads when commit history, notes, and current status provide enough evidence.
- Do not close broad parent epics unless all required child scope is actually done.

title: Create static branch from current main
description:
Source Reference: pt-cf0
Status: open
Priority: P2
Risk: low
Scope: current
Agent Owner: codex
Agent Eligible: codex
Tags: cutover,deployment,git,vercel

Issue:
Create a static branch from current main and add a temporary redirect so legacy.pttracker.app redirects to pttracker.app.

Context:
The static branch will be deployed via a second Vercel project (pt-static) to legacy.pttracker.app. While the static app is still the primary app at pttracker.app, legacy.pttracker.app should redirect there rather than serve duplicate content. The redirect is removed post-flip (pt-cf0.7).

Implementation:
1. Create static branch from main (git checkout -b static origin/main or equivalent)
2. In pt-rebuild/vercel.json on the static branch, add a redirect rule:
   { "source": "/", "destination": "https://pttracker.app", "permanent": false }
   (or use a catch-all source: "/:path*" so all paths redirect)
3. Push static branch to origin

Constraints/Caveats:
- Branch from main, not from nextjs
- Use a 302 (temporary) redirect, not 301 — will be removed post-flip
- Catch-all redirect preferred so no URL on legacy.pttracker.app serves content directly yet
- Do not make any other code changes on this branch

Validation Checklist:
- [ ] static branch exists on origin
- [ ] static branch vercel.json contains catch-all 302 redirect to pttracker.app
- [ ] static branch matches main otherwise exactly

- discovered-from: pt-cf0

title: Migrate reset-password to Next.js route (replace reset-password.html)
description:
public/reset-password.html is the current Supabase auth redirect target for password reset emails. It uses the vendored supabase.min.js client to process the recovery token and set a new password.

This should be a proper Next.js page at pages/reset-password.js, using the shared Supabase client (lib/supabase.js) and consistent app styling.

Steps:
1. Create pages/reset-password.js — handles Supabase recovery token from URL hash, shows new password form, calls supabase.auth.updateUser()
2. Update Supabase auth redirect URL in Supabase dashboard (Authentication > URL Configuration > Redirect URLs) to point to Next.js route
3. Verify password reset flow works end-to-end on preview URL before pruning the static file
4. Once verified: prune public/reset-password.html and public/js/vendor/supabase.min.js (the only remaining consumer)

Context:
- App has 2 users — this flow is rarely used but is real and must work
- Current static page is functional; do not break it before the Next.js replacement is verified
- supabase.min.js can only be pruned after this migration is complete (it has no other consumers)

File scope:
- pt-rebuild/pages/reset-password.js (create)
- public/reset-password.html (prune after verified)
- public/js/vendor/supabase.min.js (prune after verified)
- Supabase dashboard: auth redirect URL update required

- components/AuthForm.js: added forgot/sent views, supabase.auth.resetPasswordForEmail() with redirectTo: window.location.origin + /reset-password

- public/reset-password.html + public/js/vendor/supabase.min.js: pruned
Commits: 75c435e, 11d3f02, 60308a5

Pending (pt-b65.1): end-to-end verification with real recovery email. Also need to add preview URL to Supabase allowed redirect URLs before testing (Authentication > URL Configuration > Redirect URLs): https://pt-rehab-git-nextjs-pt-tracker.vercel.app/reset-password

title: Define legacy-prune cutover requirement for nextjs branch
description:
Cutover requirement captured from 2026-03-21 discussion.

Requirement:
The nextjs branch must remain deployable on Vercel after legacy static app code is pruned out, so it can eventually replace main cleanly instead of permanently carrying both architectures.

Intent:
- Preserve the legacy static app on a separate branch/subdomain as fallback
- Prune legacy static app code from nextjs once that fallback is secured
- Keep only true shared browser-served assets in public/ on nextjs
- Verify nextjs still builds/deploys correctly on Vercel after legacy pruning
- Make eventual branch replacement (nextjs becoming the primary branch) operationally straightforward

Why this matters:
Keeping old static pages inside nextjs forever preserves unnecessary complexity and increases the chance that future agents edit the wrong surface. The fallback should live on its own branch/deployment, not inside the modern app branch.

Next-session use:
Treat this as a cutover-plan requirement, not an immediate cleanup task. Do not prune legacy code from nextjs until the fallback branch/subdomain plan is ready.

title: Add tracker bootstrap timing marks for observability
description:
Source Reference: discovered during 2026-03-21 tracker load-time investigation after pt-eg2.12 bootstrap split
Status: open

Issue:
Tracker load timing needs first-class observability so future agents can see when the picker becomes usable and when history finishes, instead of inferring route latency from raw request timings alone.

Context:
Vercel Speed Insights is already mounted globally, but the tracker route does not expose route-specific bootstrap marks. The current tracker path has multiple overlapping async phases (auth/session, users/messages, exercises/programs, logs, picker render), and DevTools traces do not clearly label which phase is delaying the first useful tracker surface.

Constraints/Caveats:
- Keep instrumentation lightweight and browser-only
- Prefer stable performance.mark/performance.measure names that future agents can search
- Do not add noisy user-facing UI just for instrumentation
- README should document where tracker timing instrumentation lives

Validation Checklist:
- [ ] Tracker bootstrap start, primary-ready, history-ready, and picker-ready marks are visible in the browser performance timeline
- [ ] Marks do not break tracker render or offline fallback
- [ ] README points agents to the timing instrumentation location

- discovered-from: pt-eg2.12
- parent: pt-eg2
notes: 2026-03-21 implementation update: added browser-only tracker timing instrumentation in lib/tracker-performance.js and wired stable mark/measure names into useIndexData and pages/index.js. Tracker now marks bootstrap start, primary-ready, history-ready, and picker-ready phases so DevTools can show route-specific timing instead of only raw request durations. README ownership notes were updated for the new helper.

title: Unblock tracker exercise picker from full history bootstrap
description:
Source Reference: discovered during 2026-03-21 tracker load-time investigation after pt-wde closure
Status: open
Priority: P2
Risk: medium
Scope: current
Agent Owner: codex
Agent Eligible: codex
Tags: nextjs,tracker,performance,bootstrap,index,agent:codex
File Scope:
- pt-rebuild/hooks/useIndexData.js
- pt-rebuild/pages/index.js
- pt-rebuild/lib/index-data.js

Issue:
The Next.js tracker should not block the exercise picker on the full history bootstrap payload. Signed-in preview measurements show /api/logs?include_all=true&limit=1000 is the heaviest initial request and delays the first useful tracker surface even though exercise selection only needs exercises and programs first.

Context:
On 2026-03-21, signed-in preview resource timings showed /api/logs?include_all=true&limit=1000 at about 879 ms and ~521 KB decoded, while /api/exercises and /api/programs were only ~320-400 ms and ~53-57 KB. useIndexData currently waits for all three via one Promise.all before setting tracker state, so the picker cannot render until the history payload finishes.

Constraints/Caveats:
- Preserve correctness for logs/history once history finishes loading
- Do not regress offline cache writes or read fallback for tracker bootstrap
- Keep the page orchestrator thin; bootstrap sequencing belongs in hooks/lib
- Avoid broad behavior changes to messaging/history unless directly needed for load-path isolation

Validation Checklist:
- [ ] Exercise picker can render from exercises + programs before full history load completes
- [ ] History still loads and populates once the logs request resolves
- [ ] Failed history load does not block exercise selection when exercises/programs succeeded
- [ ] Offline tracker cache behavior is not regressed

Dependencies:
- discovered-from: pt-s81
- parent: pt-eg2
notes:
2026-03-21 implementation update: split tracker bootstrap in useIndexData so exercises + programs resolve and render before the heavier logs payload, while history continues loading in the background with its own non-blocking error state. ExercisePicker now suppresses placeholder adherence badges until history has actually arrived, and README ownership notes were updated for the staged-bootstrap boundary.
2026-03-22 deferred by user to next week so Vercel Analytics / Speed Insights can collect real multi-user, multi-instance tracker data before more route-side load-time tuning. Do not resume this lane before then unless a new production-facing load regression appears.

title: PT program page select dropdowns missing arrow indicator
description:
Source Reference: discovered during pt-unj.1 preview verification (2026-03-21).

Bug:
The <select> elements on /program (Select Exercise to Edit, Assign Roles, Manage Patient Dosages pickers) render without a dropdown arrow indicator. The tracker page (/) selects show arrows correctly.

Evidence:
Screenshot shows 'Add New Exercise (leave blank)' and 'Choose an exercise' selects with no arrow — just a flat dark box.

Root cause hypothesis:
/program CSS module or ProgramWorkspace components use custom select styles that omit the appearance override + SVG arrow background-image that other pages apply. The select arrow on / comes from globals.css or the ExercisePicker CSS module.

File scope:
pt-rebuild/pages/program.js (or its CSS module), pt-rebuild/components/ (whichever workspace component owns the select elements)
close_reason: Fixed: .exerciseSelect in all three program workspace CSS modules used 'background' shorthand which reset the SVG arrow background-image inherited from NativeSelect.module.css. Changed to background-color and removed redundant properties (border, color, width, padding, min-height) already set by NativeSelect base styles.

title: PT program page NavMenu missing Program Editor link for therapist and admin after pt-unj.1 isAdmin fix
description:
Source Reference: discovered during pt-unj.1 preview verification (2026-03-21).

Bug:
After Codex's pt-unj.1 fix replaced hardcoded isAdmin=true with role-based detection, the NavMenu no longer shows the '📋 Program Editor' link for either therapist (test-therapist@pttracker.app) or admin (cindi@puppyraiser.com) when on /program. Both accounts can still access the editor directly (page renders correctly), but the nav link is hidden.

Evidence:
- test-patient: blocked with 'Therapist or admin access required.' ✓ (correct)
- test-therapist: editor renders, but NavMenu shows only PT Tracker / View History / Coverage Analysis — no Program Editor
- cindi (admin): same — editor renders, NavMenu missing Program Editor link
- The access gate logic is correct; only the NavMenu isAdmin derivation is wrong for /program

Root cause hypothesis:
/program derives isAdmin from the DB role field. Admin user (cindi) has role 'patient' in the DB (she is the sole patient). Therapist has role 'therapist'. isAdmin check may be using role === 'admin' which matches neither. The correct check should be role === 'therapist' || hasAdminPrivilege.

File scope:
pt-rebuild/pages/program.js
close_reason: Closed

title: Refactor: shared useUserContext hook for messaging identity
description:
MessagesModal needs viewerName/otherName/otherIsTherapist/profileId/recipientId on every page that uses messaging. Currently index.js fetches users locally and derives these fields; pt-view.js is missing them entirely. Any future page adding messaging would duplicate this logic again.

Fix: create hooks/useUserContext.js as the single reusable source of truth for user identity and messaging context. Refactor usePtViewData to accept { token, patientId } instead of session (eliminating its internal fetchUsers call). Both index.js and pt-view.js call useUserContext(session) and pass messaging fields to MessagesModal.

Files:
- NEW: hooks/useUserContext.js — fetches users, returns profileId, patientId, recipientId, userRole, emailEnabled, viewerName, otherName, otherIsTherapist, loading, error
- MODIFY: hooks/usePtViewData.js — change signature to ({ token, patientId }), remove fetchUsers call, return only logs/programs/dataError/offlineNotice
- MODIFY: pages/pt-view.js — call useUserContext, pass messaging props to MessagesModal, local emailEnabled state synced from userCtx
- MODIFY: pages/index.js — replace local fetchUsers useEffect with useUserContext call

emailEnabled toggle pattern: pages keep local state initialized from userCtx.emailEnabled, call patchEmailNotifications + local setter on toggle.

Offline cache: useUserContext handles cacheUsers; usePtViewData handles cacheLogs/cachePrograms.
close_reason: Implemented: hooks/useUserContext.js created, usePtViewData refactored, both pages updated. Commit c302242.
